</div><div class="mw-content-ltr mw-parser-output" lang="en" dir="ltr"><div class="shortdescription nomobile noexcerpt noprint searchaux" style="display:none">Modular space station in low Earth orbit</div>

</td></tr></tbody></table>
<p>The <b>International Space Station</b> (<b>ISS</b>) is a <a href="/wiki/Space_station" title="Space station">space station</a> in <a href="/wiki/Low_Earth_orbit" title="Low Earth orbit">low Earth orbit</a> (LEO). It is the product of the <a href="/wiki/International_Space_Station_program" title="International Space Station program">International Space Station program</a> and is operated by five partner <a href="/wiki/Space_agency" class="mw-redirect" title="Space agency">space agencies</a>: <a href="/wiki/NASA" title="NASA">NASA</a> (United States), <a href="/wiki/Roscosmos" title="Roscosmos">Roscosmos</a> (Russia), <a href="/wiki/European_Space_Agency" title="European Space Agency">ESA</a> (Europe), <a href="/wiki/JAXA" title="JAXA">JAXA</a> (Japan), and <a href="/wiki/Canadian_Space_Agency" title="Canadian Space Agency">CSA</a> (Canada).<sup id="cite&#95;ref-x270&#95;15-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-x270-15"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>13<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> It is the first space station built, maintained and crewed through <a href="/wiki/International_cooperation" class="mw-redirect" title="International cooperation">international cooperation</a> and the largest human spacecraft ever constructed.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-z085&#95;16-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-z085-16"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>14<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-:5&#95;17-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:5-17"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>15<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> It is an orbital <a href="/wiki/Research_station" title="Research station">research station</a>, where scientific experiments in <a href="/wiki/Microgravity" class="mw-redirect" title="Microgravity">microgravity</a> are conducted and the <a href="/wiki/Space_environment" title="Space environment">space environment</a> is studied.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-:5&#95;17-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:5-17"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>15<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Since 2 November 2000, it has hosted the longest continuous <a href="/wiki/Human_presence_in_space" title="Human presence in space">presence of humans in space</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-d504&#95;18-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-d504-18"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>16<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Alongside <a href="/wiki/Tiangong_space_station" title="Tiangong space station"><i>Tiangong</i></a>, it is one of the only two currently operating space stations.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-19" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-19"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>17<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The station orbits between <a href="/wiki/Circles_of_latitude_between_the_50th_parallel_north_and_the_55th_parallel_north#51st_parallel_north" title="Circles of latitude between the 50th parallel north and the 55th parallel north">51.64° north</a> and <a href="/wiki/Circles_of_latitude_between_the_50th_parallel_south_and_the_55th_parallel_south#51st_parallel_south" title="Circles of latitude between the 50th parallel south and the 55th parallel south">south</a>, at about 400 kilometres (250 miles)<sup id="cite&#95;ref-20" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-20"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>18<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> above Earth, below the <a href="/wiki/Van_Allen_radiation_belt" title="Van Allen radiation belt">Van Allen radiation belts</a> and most <a href="/wiki/Space_debris" title="Space debris">space debris</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-nasa9&#95;21-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-nasa9-21"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>19<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Its orbit takes it at 7.67&#160;km/s (27,600&#160;km/h; 17,200&#160;mph) roughly every 93 minutes around Earth, 15.5 times a day.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-tracking2&#95;22-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-tracking2-22"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>20<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Measuring 109&#160;m (358&#160;ft) (with solar arrays) by 73&#160;m (239&#160;ft),<sup id="cite&#95;ref-y955&#95;23-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-y955-23"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>21<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> it is as large as a full-sized football or soccer field,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-q020&#95;24-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-q020-24"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>22<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and has a pressurised internal volume of 1,005 m<sup>3</sup> (35,491 ft<sup>3</sup>), comparable to a <a href="/wiki/Boeing_747" title="Boeing 747">Boeing 747</a> airliner.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-y955&#95;23-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-y955-23"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>21<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The station is a <a href="/wiki/Modular_space_station" class="mw-redirect" title="Modular space station">modular space station</a> divided into two main sections: the <a href="/wiki/Russian_Orbital_Segment" title="Russian Orbital Segment">Russian Orbital Segment</a> (ROS), developed by Roscosmos, and the <a href="/wiki/US_Orbital_Segment" title="US Orbital Segment">US Orbital Segment</a> (USOS), built by NASA, ESA, JAXA, and CSA. A striking feature of the ISS is the <a href="/wiki/Integrated_Truss_Structure" title="Integrated Truss Structure">Integrated Truss Structure</a>, which connect the station's vast system of <a href="/wiki/Solar_panel" title="Solar panel">solar panels</a> and <a href="/wiki/Spacecraft_thermal_control" title="Spacecraft thermal control">radiators</a> to its pressurized modules. These modules support diverse functions, including scientific research, crew habitation, storage, spacecraft control, and airlock operations. The ISS has eight <a href="/wiki/Docking_and_berthing_of_spacecraft" title="Docking and berthing of spacecraft">docking and berthing ports</a> for visiting spacecraft. In total, the station consists of 43 different modules and elements.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-25" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-25"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>23<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The ISS is <a href="/wiki/Politics_of_the_International_Space_Station" title="Politics of the International Space Station">the political product</a> of the development of <a href="/wiki/International_cooperation_in_space" class="mw-redirect" title="International cooperation in space">international cooperation in space</a> throughout the <a href="/wiki/Space_age" class="mw-redirect" title="Space age">space age</a>. The station combines two previously planned crewed Earth-orbiting stations: the United States' <a href="/wiki/Space_Station_Freedom" title="Space Station Freedom">Space Station <i>Freedom</i></a> and the Soviet Union's<i> <a href="/wiki/Mir-2" title="Mir-2">Mir-2</a></i>. The <a href="/wiki/Zarya_(ISS_module)" title="Zarya (ISS module)">first ISS module</a> was launched in 1998, with major components delivered by <a href="/wiki/Proton_(rocket_family)" title="Proton (rocket family)">Proton</a> and <a href="/wiki/Soyuz_(rocket_family)" title="Soyuz (rocket family)">Soyuz</a> rockets and the <a href="/wiki/Space_Shuttle" title="Space Shuttle">Space Shuttle</a>. Long-term occupancy began with the arrival of the <a href="/wiki/Expedition_1" title="Expedition 1">Expedition 1</a> crew on 2 November 2000. Since then, the ISS has remained continuously inhabited for 25&#160;years and 157&#160;days, the longest continuous human presence in space. As of August&#160;2025<sup class="plainlinks noexcerpt noprint asof-tag update" style="display:none;"><a class="external text" href="https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=International_Space_Station&amp;action=edit">&#91;update&#93;</a></sup>, 290 individuals from 26 countries had visited the station.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-26" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-26"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>24<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Future plans for the ISS include the addition of at least one module, <a href="/wiki/Axiom_Station#PPTM" title="Axiom Station">Payload Power Thermal Module</a> by <a href="/wiki/Axiom_Space" title="Axiom Space">Axiom Space</a>, forming the commercial segment of the station. The station is expected to remain operational until the end of 2030, by which parts of it are to be used for <a href="/wiki/Axiom_Station" title="Axiom Station">Axiom Station</a> and the <a href="/wiki/Russian_Orbital_Service_Station" title="Russian Orbital Service Station">Russian Orbital Service Station</a>. After this the ISS is planned to be de-orbited using the <a href="/wiki/US_Deorbit_Vehicle" class="mw-redirect" title="US Deorbit Vehicle">US Deorbit Vehicle</a>,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-:2b&#95;27-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:2b-27"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>25<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> but critique of this plan and the proposal of parking the station at a more stable higher orbit has gathered congressional support as of 2026<sup class="plainlinks noexcerpt noprint asof-tag update" style="display:none;"><a class="external text" href="https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=International_Space_Station&amp;action=edit">&#91;update&#93;</a></sup>.
</p>
<meta property="mw:PageProp/toc" />
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Conception">Conception</h2></div>
<div class="excerpt-block"><style data-mw-deduplicate="TemplateStyles:r1066933788">.mw-parser-output .excerpt-hat .mw-editsection-like{font-style:normal}</style><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable dablink excerpt-hat selfref">This section is an excerpt from <a href="/wiki/International_Space_Station_program#Conception" title="International Space Station program">International Space Station program § Conception</a>.<span class="mw-editsection-like plainlinks"><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">[</span><a class="external text" href="https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=International_Space_Station_program&amp;action=edit">edit</a><span class="mw-editsection-bracket">]</span></span></div><div class="excerpt">
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:S74-24913.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/S74-24913.jpg/250px-S74-24913.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="192" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/S74-24913.jpg/500px-S74-24913.jpg 2x" data-file-width="3923" data-file-height="3011" /></a><figcaption>A painting of <a href="/wiki/Apollo%E2%80%93Soyuz" title="Apollo–Soyuz">Apollo–Soyuz</a>, a first milestone in international spaceflight</figcaption></figure>
<p>Early into the <a href="/wiki/Space_age" class="mw-redirect" title="Space age">space age</a> and ensuing <a href="/wiki/Space_race" class="mw-redirect" title="Space race">space race</a> the US and <a href="/wiki/USSR" class="mw-redirect" title="USSR">USSR</a> began to find opportunities for potential collaborations in outer space. This culminated in the 1975 <a href="/wiki/Apollo%E2%80%93Soyuz" title="Apollo–Soyuz">Apollo–Soyuz Test Project</a>, the first docking of spacecraft from two different spacefaring nations. The ASTP was considered a success, and further joint missions were also contemplated. 
</p><p>One such concept was International Skylab, which proposed launching the backup <a href="/wiki/Skylab_B" title="Skylab B">Skylab B</a> space station for a mission that would see multiple visits by both <a href="/wiki/Apollo_CSM" class="mw-redirect" title="Apollo CSM">Apollo</a> and <a href="/wiki/Soyuz_(spacecraft)" title="Soyuz (spacecraft)">Soyuz</a> crew vehicles.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-28" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-28"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>26<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> More ambitious was the Skylab-Salyut Space Laboratory, which proposed docking the Skylab B to a Soviet <a href="/wiki/Salyut_program" class="mw-redirect" title="Salyut program">Salyut</a> space station. Falling budgets and rising <a href="/wiki/Cold_War" title="Cold War">Cold War</a> tensions in the late 1970s saw these concepts fall by the wayside, along with another plan to have the <a href="/wiki/Space_Shuttle" title="Space Shuttle">Space Shuttle</a> dock with a Salyut space station.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-International&#95;Space&#95;Station&#95;program&#95;wired-20120326&#95;29-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-International_Space_Station_program_wired-20120326-29"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>27<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> 
</p><p>In the early 1980s, <a href="/wiki/NASA" title="NASA">NASA</a> planned to launch a modular space station called <i>Freedom</i> as a counterpart to the Salyut and <i><a href="/wiki/Mir" title="Mir">Mir</a></i> space stations. In 1984 the <a href="/wiki/European_Space_Agency" title="European Space Agency">European Space Agency</a> (ESA) was invited to participate in Space Station <i>Freedom</i>, and the ESA approved the Columbus laboratory by 1987.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-International&#95;Space&#95;Station&#95;program&#95;ESA&#95;-&#95;Columbus&#95;30-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-International_Space_Station_program_ESA_-_Columbus-30"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>28<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The <a href="/wiki/Japanese_Experiment_Module" class="mw-redirect" title="Japanese Experiment Module">Japanese Experiment Module</a> (JEM), or <i>Kibō</i>, was announced in 1985, as part of the <i>Freedom</i> space station in response to a NASA request in 1982.
</p><p>In early 1985, science ministers from the ESA countries approved the <i>Columbus</i> program, the most ambitious effort in space undertaken by that organization at the time. The plan spearheaded by Germany and Italy included a module which would be attached to <i>Freedom</i>, and with the capability to evolve into a full-fledged European orbital outpost before the end of the century.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-International&#95;Space&#95;Station&#95;program&#95;astronautix.com&#95;31-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-International_Space_Station_program_astronautix.com-31"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>29<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Increasing costs threw these plans into doubt in the early 1990s. Congress was unwilling to provide enough money to build and operate <i>Freedom</i>, and demanded NASA increase international participation to defray the rising costs or they would cancel the entire project outright.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-International&#95;Space&#95;Station&#95;program&#95;nyt-19930608&#95;32-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-International_Space_Station_program_nyt-19930608-32"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>30<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Simultaneously, the USSR was conducting planning for the <a href="/wiki/Mir-2" title="Mir-2">Mir-2</a> space station, and had begun constructing modules for the new station by the mid-1980s. However the <a href="/wiki/Collapse_of_the_Soviet_Union" class="mw-redirect" title="Collapse of the Soviet Union">collapse of the Soviet Union</a> required these plans to be greatly downscaled, and soon Mir-2 was in danger of never being launched at all.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-33" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-33"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>31<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> With both space station projects in jeopardy, American and Russian officials met and proposed they be combined.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-34" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-34"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>32<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> 
</p><p>In September 1993, American Vice-President <a href="/wiki/Al_Gore_Jr." class="mw-redirect" title="Al Gore Jr.">Al Gore</a> and Russian Prime Minister <a href="/wiki/Viktor_Chernomyrdin" title="Viktor Chernomyrdin">Viktor Chernomyrdin</a> announced plans for a new space station, which eventually became the International Space Station.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-International&#95;Space&#95;Station&#95;program&#95;gao&#95;35-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-International_Space_Station_program_gao-35"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>33<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> They also agreed, in preparation for this new project, that the United States would be involved in the Mir program, including American Shuttles docking, in the <a href="/wiki/Shuttle%E2%80%93Mir_program" title="Shuttle–Mir program">Shuttle–<i>Mir</i> program</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-International&#95;Space&#95;Station&#95;program&#95;SMB&#95;36-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-International_Space_Station_program_SMB-36"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>34<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
</div></div>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Purpose">Purpose</h2></div>
<p>The ISS was originally intended to be a laboratory, observatory, and factory while providing transportation, maintenance, and a <a href="/wiki/Low_Earth_orbit" title="Low Earth orbit">low Earth orbit</a> staging base for possible future missions to the Moon, Mars, and asteroids. However, not all of the uses envisioned in the initial <a href="/wiki/Memorandum_of_understanding" title="Memorandum of understanding">memorandum of understanding</a> between <a href="/wiki/NASA" title="NASA">NASA</a> and <a href="/wiki/Roscosmos" title="Roscosmos">Roscosmos</a> have been realised.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-RSA-MOU&#95;37-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-RSA-MOU-37"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>35<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In the <a href="/wiki/Space_policy_of_the_Barack_Obama_administration" class="mw-redirect" title="Space policy of the Barack Obama administration">2010 United States National Space Policy</a>, the ISS was given additional roles of serving commercial, diplomatic,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-payette-2012&#95;38-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-payette-2012-38"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>36<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and educational purposes.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-USNSP&#95;39-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-USNSP-39"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>37<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Scientific_research">Scientific research</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Scientific_research_on_the_International_Space_Station" title="Scientific research on the International Space Station">Scientific research on the International Space Station</a></div>

2x" data-file-width="3032" data-file-height="2002" /></a></span></div><div class="thumbcaption"><a href="/wiki/Michael_Foale" title="Michael Foale">Michael Foale</a> conducts an inspection of the <a 8</a>.</div></div></div></div></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1273380762/mw-parser-output/.tmulti" /><div class="thumb tmulti tright"><div class="thumbinner multiimageinner" style="width:292px;max-width:292px"><div class="trow"><div class="tsingle" style="width:144px;max-width:144px"><div class="thumbimage" style="height:94px;overflow:hidden"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:STS-134_EVA4_view_to_the_Space_Shuttle_Endeavour.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/STS-134_EVA4_view_to_the_Space_Shuttle_Endeavour.jpg/250px-STS-134_EVA4_view_to_the_Space_Shuttle_Endeavour.jpg" decoding="async" width="142" height="94" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/STS-134_EVA4_view_to_the_Space_Shuttle_Endeavour.jpg/330px-STS-134_EVA4_view_to_the_Space_Shuttle_Endeavour.jpg 2x" data-file-width="4288" data-file-height="2848" /></a></span></div><div class="thumbcaption">Fisheye view of several labs and the Space Shuttle</div></div><div class="tsingle" style="width:144px;max-width:144px"><div class="thumbimage" style="height:94px;overflow:hidden"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:NanoRacksCubeSatLaunch_ISS038-E-056389.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/NanoRacksCubeSatLaunch_ISS038-E-056389.jpg/250px-NanoRacksCubeSatLaunch_ISS038-E-056389.jpg" decoding="async" width="142" height="94" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/NanoRacksCubeSatLaunch_ISS038-E-056389.jpg/330px-NanoRacksCubeSatLaunch_ISS038-E-056389.jpg 2x" data-file-width="4256" data-file-height="2832" /></a></span></div><div class="thumbcaption"><a href="/wiki/CubeSat" title="CubeSat">CubeSats</a> are deployed by the <a href="/wiki/NanoRacks_CubeSat_Deployer" class="mw-redirect" title="NanoRacks CubeSat Deployer">NanoRacks CubeSat Deployer</a>.</div></div></div></div></div>
<p>The ISS provides a platform to conduct scientific research, with power, data, cooling, and crew available to support experiments. Small uncrewed spacecraft can also provide platforms for experiments, especially those involving zero gravity and exposure to space, but space stations offer a long-term environment where studies can be performed potentially for decades, combined with ready access by human researchers.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-10th&#95;40-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-10th-40"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>38<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-Worldbook&#95;at&#95;NASA&#95;41-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Worldbook_at_NASA-41"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>39<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The ISS simplifies individual experiments by allowing groups of experiments to share the same launches and crew time. Research is conducted in a wide variety of fields, including <a href="/wiki/Astrobiology" title="Astrobiology">astrobiology</a>, <a href="/wiki/Astronomy" title="Astronomy">astronomy</a>, <a href="/wiki/Physical_science" class="mw-redirect" title="Physical science">physical sciences</a>, <a href="/wiki/Materials_science" title="Materials science">materials science</a>, <a href="/wiki/Space_weather" title="Space weather">space weather</a>, <a href="/wiki/Meteorology" title="Meteorology">meteorology</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Zero_g#Health_effects_of_weightlessness" class="mw-redirect" title="Zero g">human research</a> including <a href="/wiki/Space_medicine" title="Space medicine">space medicine</a> and the <a href="/wiki/Life_science" class="mw-redirect" title="Life science">life sciences</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-NASA&#95;Fields&#95;of&#95;Research&#95;42-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-NASA_Fields_of_Research-42"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>40<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-NASA&#95;ISS&#95;Goals&#95;43-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-NASA_ISS_Goals-43"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>41<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-44" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-44"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>42<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-esa-20110311&#95;45-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-esa-20110311-45"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>43<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Scientists on Earth have timely access to the data and can suggest experimental modifications to the crew. If follow-on experiments are necessary, the routinely scheduled launches of resupply craft allows new hardware to be launched with relative ease.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Worldbook&#95;at&#95;NASA&#95;41-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Worldbook_at_NASA-41"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>39<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Crews fly <a href="/wiki/List_of_International_Space_Station_expeditions" title="List of International Space Station expeditions">expeditions</a> of several months' duration, providing approximately 160 man-hours per week of labour with a crew of six. However, a considerable amount of crew time is taken up by station maintenance.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Science&#95;in&#95;School&#95;46-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Science_in_School-46"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>44<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Perhaps the most notable ISS experiment is the <a href="/wiki/Alpha_Magnetic_Spectrometer" title="Alpha Magnetic Spectrometer">Alpha Magnetic Spectrometer</a> (AMS), which is intended to detect dark matter and answer other fundamental questions about our universe. According to NASA, the AMS is as important as the <a href="/wiki/Hubble_Space_Telescope" title="Hubble Space Telescope">Hubble Space Telescope</a>. Currently docked on station, it could not have been easily accommodated on a free flying satellite platform because of its power and bandwidth needs.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-nasa-20110318&#95;47-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-nasa-20110318-47"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>45<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-nasa-20090814&#95;48-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-nasa-20090814-48"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>46<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> On 3 April 2013, scientists reported that hints of <a href="/wiki/Dark_matter" title="Dark matter">dark matter</a> may have been detected by the AMS.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-APS-20130403&#95;49-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-APS-20130403-49"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>47<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-AMS-20130403&#95;50-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-AMS-20130403-50"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>48<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-AP-20130403&#95;51-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-AP-20130403-51"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>49<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-BBC-20130403&#95;52-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-BBC-20130403-52"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>50<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-NASA-20130403&#95;53-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-NASA-20130403-53"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>51<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-NYT-20130403&#95;54-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-NYT-20130403-54"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>52<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> According to the scientists, "The first results from the space-borne Alpha Magnetic Spectrometer confirm an unexplained excess of high-energy positrons in Earth-bound cosmic rays".<sup id="cite&#95;ref-55" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-55"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>53<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The space environment is hostile to life. Unprotected presence in space is characterised by an intense radiation field (consisting primarily of protons and other subatomic charged particles from the <a href="/wiki/Solar_wind" title="Solar wind">solar wind</a>, in addition to <a href="/wiki/Cosmic_ray" title="Cosmic ray">cosmic rays</a>), high vacuum, extreme temperatures, and microgravity.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Space&#95;Microbiology&#95;56-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Space_Microbiology-56"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>54<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Some simple forms of life called <a href="/wiki/Extremophile" title="Extremophile">extremophiles</a>,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Beer&#95;microbes&#95;57-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Beer_microbes-57"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>55<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> as well as small invertebrates called <a href="/wiki/Tardigrade" title="Tardigrade">tardigrades</a>,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Waterbears&#95;58-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Waterbears-58"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>56<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> can survive in this environment in an extremely dry state through <a href="/wiki/Desiccation#Biology_and_ecology" title="Desiccation">desiccation</a>.
</p><p>Medical research improves knowledge about the effects of long-term space exposure on the human body, including <a href="/wiki/Muscle_atrophy" title="Muscle atrophy">muscle atrophy</a>, <a href="/wiki/Osteoporosis" title="Osteoporosis">bone loss</a>, and fluid shift. These data will be used to determine whether high duration <a href="/wiki/Human_spaceflight" title="Human spaceflight">human spaceflight</a> and <a href="/wiki/Space_colonisation" class="mw-redirect" title="Space colonisation">space colonisation</a> are feasible. In 2006, data on bone loss and muscular atrophy suggested that there would be a significant risk of fractures and movement problems if astronauts landed on a planet after a lengthy interplanetary cruise, such as the six-month interval required to <a href="/wiki/Human_mission_to_Mars" title="Human mission to Mars">travel to Mars</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-JCB&#95;59-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-JCB-59"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>57<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-newscientist-20090722&#95;60-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-newscientist-20090722-60"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>58<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Medical studies are conducted aboard the ISS on behalf of the <a href="/wiki/National_Space_Biomedical_Research_Institute" title="National Space Biomedical Research Institute">National Space Biomedical Research Institute</a> (NSBRI). Prominent among these is the <a href="/wiki/Advanced_Diagnostic_Ultrasound_in_Microgravity" title="Advanced Diagnostic Ultrasound in Microgravity">Advanced Diagnostic Ultrasound in Microgravity</a> study in which astronauts perform ultrasound scans under the guidance of remote experts. The study considers the diagnosis and treatment of medical conditions in space. Usually, there is no physician on board the ISS and diagnosis of medical conditions is a challenge. It is anticipated that remotely guided ultrasound scans will have application on Earth in emergency and rural care situations where access to a trained physician is difficult.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-nasa-adum&#95;61-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-nasa-adum-61"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>59<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-62" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-62"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>60<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-63" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-63"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>61<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>In August 2020, scientists reported that <a href="/wiki/Bacteria" title="Bacteria">bacteria</a> from Earth, particularly <i><a href="/wiki/Deinococcus_radiodurans" title="Deinococcus radiodurans">Deinococcus radiodurans</a></i> bacteria, which is highly resistant to <a href="/wiki/Environmental_hazard" title="Environmental hazard">environmental hazards</a>, were found to survive for three years in <a href="/wiki/Outer_space" title="Outer space">outer space</a>, based on studies conducted on the International Space Station. These findings supported the notion of <a href="/wiki/Panspermia" title="Panspermia">panspermia</a>, the hypothesis that <a href="/wiki/Life" title="Life">life</a> exists throughout the <a href="/wiki/Universe" title="Universe">Universe</a>, distributed in various ways, including <a href="/wiki/Space_dust" class="mw-redirect" title="Space dust">space dust</a>, <a href="/wiki/Meteoroid" title="Meteoroid">meteoroids</a>, <a href="/wiki/Asteroid" title="Asteroid">asteroids</a>, <a href="/wiki/Comet" title="Comet">comets</a>, <a href="/wiki/Minor_planet" title="Minor planet">planetoids</a> or <a href="/wiki/Contamination" title="Contamination">contaminated</a> <a href="/wiki/Spacecraft" title="Spacecraft">spacecraft</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-CNN-20200826&#95;64-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-CNN-20200826-64"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>62<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-FM-20200826&#95;65-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-FM-20200826-65"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>63<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p><a href="/wiki/Remote_sensing" title="Remote sensing">Remote sensing</a> of the Earth, astronomy, and deep space research on the ISS have significantly increased during the 2010s after the completion of the <a href="/wiki/US_Orbital_Segment" title="US Orbital Segment">US Orbital Segment</a> in 2011. Throughout the more than 20 years of the ISS program, researchers aboard the ISS and on the ground have examined <a href="/wiki/Aerosol" title="Aerosol">aerosols</a>, <a href="/wiki/Ozone" title="Ozone">ozone</a>, <a href="/wiki/Lightning" title="Lightning">lightning</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Oxide" title="Oxide">oxides</a> in Earth's atmosphere, as well as the <a href="/wiki/Sun" title="Sun">Sun</a>, cosmic rays, <a href="/wiki/Cosmic_dust" title="Cosmic dust">cosmic dust</a>, <a href="/wiki/Antimatter" title="Antimatter">antimatter</a>, and dark matter in the universe. Examples of Earth-viewing remote sensing experiments that have flown on the ISS are the <a href="/wiki/Orbiting_Carbon_Observatory_3" title="Orbiting Carbon Observatory 3">Orbiting Carbon Observatory 3</a>, <a href="/wiki/ISS-RapidScat" title="ISS-RapidScat">ISS-RapidScat</a>, <a href="/wiki/ECOSTRESS" title="ECOSTRESS">ECOSTRESS</a>, the <a href="/wiki/Global_Ecosystem_Dynamics_Investigation" title="Global Ecosystem Dynamics Investigation">Global Ecosystem Dynamics Investigation</a>, and the <a href="/wiki/Cloud_Aerosol_Transport_System" title="Cloud Aerosol Transport System">Cloud Aerosol Transport System</a>. ISS-based astronomy telescopes and experiments include <a href="/wiki/SOLAR_(ISS)" title="SOLAR (ISS)">SOLAR</a>, the <a href="/wiki/Neutron_Star_Interior_Composition_Explorer" title="Neutron Star Interior Composition Explorer">Neutron Star Interior Composition Explorer</a>, the <a href="/wiki/Calorimetric_Electron_Telescope" title="Calorimetric Electron Telescope">Calorimetric Electron Telescope</a>, the <a href="/wiki/MAXI_(ISS_experiment)" class="mw-redirect" title="MAXI (ISS experiment)">Monitor of All-sky X-ray Image (MAXI)</a>, and the <a href="/wiki/Alpha_Magnetic_Spectrometer" title="Alpha Magnetic Spectrometer">Alpha Magnetic Spectrometer</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-NASA&#95;Fields&#95;of&#95;Research&#95;42-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-NASA_Fields_of_Research-42"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>40<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-eol-rss&#95;66-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-eol-rss-66"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>64<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Microgravity_environment">Microgravity environment</h4></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Iss067e149651.webp" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/10/Iss067e149651.webp/250px-Iss067e149651.webp.png" decoding="async" width="250" height="167" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/10/Iss067e149651.webp/500px-Iss067e149651.webp.png 2x" data-file-width="5568" data-file-height="3712" /></a><figcaption><a href="/wiki/Jessica_Watkins" title="Jessica Watkins">Jessica Watkins</a> and <a href="/wiki/Robert_Hines_(astronaut)" title="Robert Hines (astronaut)">Bob Hines</a> working on XROOTS, an experiment using the Veggie facility of the station testing soilless hydroponic and aeroponic plant growth</figcaption></figure>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Space_Fire.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/Space_Fire.jpg/250px-Space_Fire.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="194" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/Space_Fire.jpg/500px-Space_Fire.jpg 2x" data-file-width="2250" data-file-height="1750" /></a><figcaption>A comparison between the combustion of a candle on <a href="/wiki/Earth" title="Earth">Earth</a> (left) and in a free fall environment, such as that found on the ISS (right)</figcaption></figure>
<p>Researchers are investigating the effect of the station's near-weightless environment on the evolution, development, growth and internal processes of plants and animals. In response to some of the data, NASA wants to investigate <a href="/wiki/Microgravity" class="mw-redirect" title="Microgravity">microgravity</a>'s effects on the growth of three-dimensional, human-like tissues and the unusual <a href="/wiki/Protein_crystal" class="mw-redirect" title="Protein crystal">protein crystals</a> that can be formed in space.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-NASA&#95;Fields&#95;of&#95;Research&#95;42-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-NASA_Fields_of_Research-42"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>40<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Investigating the physics of fluids in microgravity will provide better models of the behaviour of fluids. Because fluids can be almost completely combined in microgravity, physicists investigate fluids that do not mix well on Earth. Examining reactions that are slowed by low gravity and low temperatures will improve our understanding of <a href="/wiki/Superconductivity" title="Superconductivity">superconductivity</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-NASA&#95;Fields&#95;of&#95;Research&#95;42-3" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-NASA_Fields_of_Research-42"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>40<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The study of <a href="/wiki/Materials_science" title="Materials science">materials science</a> is an important ISS research activity, with the objective of reaping economic benefits through the improvement of techniques used on Earth.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-nasa-materials-science-101&#95;67-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-nasa-materials-science-101-67"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>65<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Other areas of interest include the effect of low gravity on combustion, through the study of the efficiency of burning and control of emissions and pollutants. These findings may improve knowledge about energy production and lead to economic and environmental benefits.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-NASA&#95;Fields&#95;of&#95;Research&#95;42-4" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-NASA_Fields_of_Research-42"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>40<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Exploration">Exploration</h3></div>
<p>The ISS provides a location in the relative safety of low Earth orbit to test spacecraft systems that will be required for long-duration missions to the Moon and Mars. This provides experience in operations, maintenance, and repair and replacement activities on-orbit. This will help develop essential skills in operating spacecraft farther from Earth, reduce mission risks, and advance the capabilities of interplanetary spacecraft.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-ResProg&#95;68-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-ResProg-68"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>66<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Referring to the <a href="/wiki/MARS-500" title="MARS-500">MARS-500</a> experiment, a crew isolation experiment conducted on Earth, ESA states, "Whereas the ISS is essential for answering questions concerning the possible impact of weightlessness, radiation and other space-specific factors, aspects such as the effect of long-term isolation and confinement can be more appropriately addressed via ground-based simulations".<sup id="cite&#95;ref-esa&#95;mars500&#95;69-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-esa_mars500-69"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>67<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Sergey Krasnov, the head of human space flight programs for Russia's space agency, Roscosmos, in 2011 suggested a "shorter version" of MARS-500 may be carried out on the ISS.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Mars&#95;thing&#95;on&#95;ISS&#95;70-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Mars_thing_on_ISS-70"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>68<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>In 2009, noting the value of the partnership framework itself, Sergey Krasnov wrote, "When compared with partners acting separately, partners developing complementary abilities and resources could give us much more assurance of the success and safety of space exploration. The ISS is helping further advance near-Earth space exploration and realisation of prospective programs of research and exploration of the Solar system, including the Moon and Mars."<sup id="cite&#95;ref-IAF2009&#95;71-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-IAF2009-71"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>69<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Human_mission_to_Mars" title="Human mission to Mars">A crewed mission to Mars</a> may be a multinational effort involving space agencies and countries outside the current ISS partnership. In 2010, ESA Director-General Jean-Jacques Dordain stated his agency was ready to propose to the other four partners that China, India, and South Korea be invited to join the ISS partnership.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-ESAproposesInvite&#95;72-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-ESAproposesInvite-72"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>70<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> NASA chief <a href="/wiki/Charles_Bolden" title="Charles Bolden">Charles Bolden</a> stated in February 2011, "Any mission to Mars is likely to be a global effort."<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Mars&#95;a&#95;global&#95;effort&#95;73-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Mars_a_global_effort-73"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>71<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Currently, <a href="/wiki/Wolf_Amendment" title="Wolf Amendment">US federal legislation</a> prevents NASA co-operation with China on space projects without approval by the <a href="/wiki/FBI" class="mw-redirect" title="FBI">FBI</a> and Congress.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-justice1&#95;74-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-justice1-74"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>72<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Education_and_cultural_outreach">Education and cultural outreach</h3></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Crew_in_ATV_with_Jules_Verne_manuscript.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/Crew_in_ATV_with_Jules_Verne_manuscript.jpg/250px-Crew_in_ATV_with_Jules_Verne_manuscript.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="166" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/Crew_in_ATV_with_Jules_Verne_manuscript.jpg/500px-Crew_in_ATV_with_Jules_Verne_manuscript.jpg 2x" data-file-width="3072" data-file-height="2040" /></a><figcaption>Original <a href="/wiki/Jules_Verne" title="Jules Verne">Jules Verne</a> manuscripts displayed by crew inside the <a href="/wiki/Jules_Verne_ATV" title="Jules Verne ATV"><i>Jules Verne</i> ATV</a> (Automated Transfer Vehicle)</figcaption></figure>
<p>The ISS crew provides opportunities for students on Earth by running student-developed experiments, making educational demonstrations, allowing for student participation in classroom versions of ISS experiments, and directly engaging students using radio, and email.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-ISSRG&#95;75-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-ISSRG-75"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>73<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-76" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-76"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>74<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> ESA offers a wide range of free teaching materials that can be downloaded for use in classrooms.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-esa-education-material&#95;77-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-esa-education-material-77"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>75<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In one lesson, students can navigate a <a href="/wiki/3D_modeling" title="3D modeling">3D model</a> of the interior and exterior of the ISS, and face spontaneous challenges to solve in real time.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-esa-iss-3d&#95;78-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-esa-iss-3d-78"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>76<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The <a href="/wiki/Japanese_Aerospace_Exploration_Agency" class="mw-redirect" title="Japanese Aerospace Exploration Agency">Japanese Aerospace Exploration Agency</a> (JAXA) aims to inspire children to "pursue craftsmanship" and to heighten their "awareness of the importance of life and their responsibilities in society".<sup id="cite&#95;ref-jaxa-peaceinyounggminds&#95;79-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-jaxa-peaceinyounggminds-79"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>77<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Through a series of education guides, students develop a deeper understanding of the past and near-term future of crewed space flight, as well as that of Earth and life.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-jaxa-sunflower-seeds&#95;80-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-jaxa-sunflower-seeds-80"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>78<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-jaxa-asagao&#95;81-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-jaxa-asagao-81"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>79<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In the JAXA "Seeds in Space" experiments, the mutation effects of spaceflight on plant seeds aboard the ISS are explored by growing sunflower seeds that have flown on the ISS for about nine months. In the first phase of <i>Kibō</i> utilisation from 2008 to mid-2010, researchers from more than a dozen Japanese universities conducted experiments in diverse fields.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-82" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-82"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>80<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Cultural activities are another major objective of the ISS program. Tetsuo Tanaka, the director of JAXA's Space Environment and Utilization Center, has said: "There is something about space that touches even people who are not interested in science."<sup id="cite&#95;ref-jaxa2&#95;83-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-jaxa2-83"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>81<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p><a href="/wiki/Amateur_Radio_on_the_International_Space_Station" title="Amateur Radio on the International Space Station">Amateur Radio on the ISS</a> (ARISS) is a volunteer program that encourages students worldwide to pursue careers in science, technology, engineering, and mathematics, through <a href="/wiki/Amateur_radio" title="Amateur radio">amateur radio</a> communications opportunities with the ISS crew. ARISS is an international working group, consisting of delegations from nine countries including several in Europe, as well as Japan, Russia, Canada, and the United States. In areas where radio equipment cannot be used, <a href="/wiki/Speakerphone" title="Speakerphone">speakerphones</a> connect students to ground stations which then connect the calls to the space station.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-84" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-84"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>82<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><span><audio id="mwe_player_0" controls="" preload="none" data-mw-tmh="" class="mw-file-element" width="250" style="width:250px;" type="text/vtt" srclang="tr" label="Türkçe ‪(tr)‬" data-dir="ltr" /></audio></span><figcaption>The first content made in space for Wikipedia, from November 2017. It is a voice recording of ESA astronaut <a href="/wiki/Paolo_Nespoli" title="Paolo Nespoli">Paolo Nespoli</a> in which he introduces himself by stating his spaceflight history</figcaption></figure>
<p><i><a href="/wiki/First_Orbit" title="First Orbit">First Orbit</a></i> is a 2011 feature-length documentary film about <a href="/wiki/Vostok_1" title="Vostok 1">Vostok 1</a>, the first crewed space flight around the Earth. By matching the orbit of the ISS to that of Vostok 1 as closely as possible, in terms of ground path and time of day, documentary filmmaker <a href="/wiki/Christopher_Riley" title="Christopher Riley">Christopher Riley</a> and ESA astronaut <a href="/wiki/Paolo_Nespoli" title="Paolo Nespoli">Paolo Nespoli</a> were able to film the view that <a href="/wiki/Yuri_Gagarin" title="Yuri Gagarin">Yuri Gagarin</a> saw on his pioneering orbital space flight. This new footage was cut together with the original Vostok 1 mission audio recordings sourced from the Russian State Archive. Nespoli is credited as the <a href="/wiki/Cinematographer" title="Cinematographer">director of photography</a> for this documentary film, as he recorded the majority of the footage himself during <a href="/wiki/Expedition_26" title="Expedition 26">Expedition 26</a>/<a href="/wiki/Expedition_27" title="Expedition 27">27</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-guardian-20110411&#95;85-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-guardian-20110411-85"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>83<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The film was streamed in a global YouTube premiere in 2011 under a free licence through the website <i>firstorbit.org</i>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-firstorbit-faq&#95;86-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-firstorbit-faq-86"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>84<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>In May 2013, commander <a href="/wiki/Chris_Hadfield" title="Chris Hadfield">Chris Hadfield</a> shot a music video of <a href="/wiki/David_Bowie" title="David Bowie">David Bowie</a>'s "<a href="/wiki/Space_Oddity" title="Space Oddity">Space Oddity</a>" on board the station, which was released on YouTube.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-87" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-87"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>85<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-bbc-20130513&#95;88-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-bbc-20130513-88"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>86<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> It was the first music video filmed in space.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-gizmodo-20130512&#95;89-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-gizmodo-20130512-89"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>87<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>In November 2017, while participating in <a href="/wiki/Expedition_52" title="Expedition 52">Expedition 52</a>/<a href="/wiki/Expedition_53" title="Expedition 53">53</a> on the ISS, <a href="/wiki/Paolo_Nespoli" title="Paolo Nespoli">Paolo Nespoli</a> made two recordings of his spoken voice (one in English and the other in his native Italian), for use on <a href="/wiki/Wikipedia" title="Wikipedia">Wikipedia</a> articles. These were the first content made in space specifically for Wikipedia.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Mabbett&#95;90-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Mabbett-90"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>88<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-Petris&#95;91-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Petris-91"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>89<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>In November 2021, a <a href="/wiki/Virtual_reality" title="Virtual reality">virtual reality</a> exhibit called The Infinite featuring life aboard the ISS was announced.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-spacecom-20211123&#95;92-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-spacecom-20211123-92"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>90<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="International_co-operation">International co-operation</h2></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main articles: <a href="/wiki/Politics_of_the_International_Space_Station" title="Politics of the International Space Station">Politics of the International Space Station</a> and <a href="/wiki/International_Space_Station_program" title="International Space Station program">International Space Station program</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:ISS_Agreements.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d7/ISS_Agreements.jpg/250px-ISS_Agreements.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="314" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d7/ISS_Agreements.jpg/500px-ISS_Agreements.jpg 2x" data-file-width="1100" data-file-height="1381" /></a><figcaption>A Commemorative Plaque honouring Space Station Intergovernmental Agreement signed on 28 January 1998</figcaption></figure>
<p>Involving five space programs and fifteen countries,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-International&#95;Cooperation&#95;93-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-International_Cooperation-93"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>91<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> the International Space Station is the most politically and legally complex space exploration program in history.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-International&#95;Cooperation&#95;93-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-International_Cooperation-93"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>91<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The 1998 Space Station Intergovernmental Agreement sets forth the primary framework for international cooperation among the parties. A series of subsequent agreements govern other aspects of the station, ranging from jurisdictional issues to a code of conduct among visiting astronauts.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-farand-iss-framework&#95;94-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-farand-iss-framework-94"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>92<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Brazil was also invited to participate in the program, the only developing country to receive such an invitation. Under the agreement framework, Brazil was to provide six pieces of hardware, and in exchange, would receive ISS utilization rights. However, Brazil was unable to deliver any of the elements due to a lack of funding and political priority within the country. Brazil officially dropped out of the ISS program in 2007.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-95" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-95"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>93<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-96" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-96"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>94<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Following the <a href="/wiki/2022_Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine" title="2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine">2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine</a>, continued cooperation between Russia and other countries on the International Space Station has been put into question. Roscosmos Director General <a href="/wiki/Dmitry_Rogozin" title="Dmitry Rogozin">Dmitry Rogozin</a> insinuated that Russian withdrawal could cause the International Space Station to de-orbit due to lack of reboost capabilities, writing in a series of tweets, "If you block cooperation with us, who will save the ISS from an unguided de-orbit to impact on the territory of the US or Europe? There's also the chance of impact of the 500-ton construction in India or China. Do you want to threaten them with such a prospect? The ISS doesn't fly over Russia, so all the risk is yours. Are you ready for it?"<sup id="cite&#95;ref-arstechnica-20220225&#95;97-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-arstechnica-20220225-97"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>95<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> (This latter claim is untrue: the ISS flies over all parts of the Earth between 51.6 degrees latitude north and south, approximately the latitude of <a href="/wiki/Saratov" title="Saratov">Saratov</a>.) Rogozin later tweeted that normal relations between ISS partners could only be restored once sanctions have been lifted, and indicated that Roscosmos would submit proposals to the Russian government on ending cooperation.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-arstechnica-20220402&#95;98-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-arstechnica-20220402-98"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>96<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> NASA stated that, if necessary, US corporation <a href="/wiki/Northrop_Grumman" title="Northrop Grumman">Northrop Grumman</a> has offered a reboost capability that would keep the ISS in orbit.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-guardian-20220301&#95;99-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-guardian-20220301-99"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>97<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>On 26 July 2022, <a href="/wiki/Yury_Borisov" title="Yury Borisov">Yury Borisov</a>, Rogozin's successor as head of Roscosmos, submitted to Russian President Putin plans for withdrawal from the program after 2024.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-26JulyBorisovQuote&#95;100-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-26JulyBorisovQuote-100"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>98<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> However, Robyn Gatens, the NASA official in charge of the space station, responded that NASA had not received any formal notices from Roscosmos concerning withdrawal plans.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Roulette&#95;2022&#95;101-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Roulette_2022-101"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>99<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Participating_countries">Participating countries</h3></div>
