<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Construction">Construction</h2></div>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Manufacturing">Manufacturing</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Manufacture_of_the_International_Space_Station" title="Manufacture of the International Space Station">Manufacture of the International Space Station</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:SSPF_interior.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b1/SSPF_interior.jpg/250px-SSPF_interior.jpg" decoding="async" width="180" height="276" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b1/SSPF_interior.jpg/500px-SSPF_interior.jpg 2x" data-file-width="1955" data-file-height="3000" /></a><figcaption><a href="/wiki/Harmony_(ISS_module)" title="Harmony (ISS module)"><i>Harmony</i></a> in the <a href="/wiki/Space_Systems_Processing_Facility" title="Space Systems Processing Facility">Space Station Processing Facility</a></figcaption></figure>
<p>The International Space Station is a product of global collaboration, with its components manufactured across the world.
</p><p>The modules of the <a href="/wiki/Russian_Orbital_Segment" title="Russian Orbital Segment">Russian Orbital Segment</a>, including <i><a href="/wiki/Zarya_(ISS_module)" title="Zarya (ISS module)">Zarya</a></i> and <i><a href="/wiki/Zvezda_(ISS_module)" title="Zvezda (ISS module)">Zvezda</a></i>, were produced at the <a href="/wiki/Khrunichev_State_Research_and_Production_Space_Center" title="Khrunichev State Research and Production Space Center">Khrunichev State Research and Production Space Center</a> in Moscow. <i>Zvezda</i> was initially manufactured in 1985 as a component for the <i><a href="/wiki/Mir-2" title="Mir-2">Mir-2</a></i> space station, which was never launched.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-:4&#95;102-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:4-102"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>100<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-103" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-103"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>101<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Much of the <a href="/wiki/US_Orbital_Segment" title="US Orbital Segment">US Orbital Segment</a>, including the <i><a href="/wiki/Destiny_(ISS_module)" title="Destiny (ISS module)">Destiny</a></i> and <i><a href="/wiki/Unity_(ISS_module)" title="Unity (ISS module)">Unity</a></i> modules, the <a href="/wiki/Integrated_Truss_Structure" title="Integrated Truss Structure">Integrated Truss Structure</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Electrical_system_of_the_International_Space_Station" title="Electrical system of the International Space Station">solar arrays</a>, were built at NASA's <a href="/wiki/Marshall_Space_Flight_Center" title="Marshall Space Flight Center">Marshall Space Flight Center</a> in <a href="/wiki/Huntsville,_Alabama" title="Huntsville, Alabama">Huntsville, Alabama</a> and <a href="/wiki/Michoud_Assembly_Facility" title="Michoud Assembly Facility">Michoud Assembly Facility</a> in <a href="/wiki/New_Orleans" title="New Orleans">New Orleans</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-:4&#95;102-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:4-102"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>100<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> These components underwent final assembly and processing for launch at the <a href="/wiki/Operations_and_Checkout_Building" title="Operations and Checkout Building">Operations and Checkout Building</a> and the <a href="/wiki/Space_Station_Processing_Facility" class="mw-redirect" title="Space Station Processing Facility">Space Station Processing Facility</a> (SSPF) at the <a href="/wiki/Kennedy_Space_Center" title="Kennedy Space Center">Kennedy Space Center</a> in Florida.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-nasa-20160219&#95;104-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-nasa-20160219-104"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>102<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The US Orbital Segment also hosts the <a href="/wiki/Columbus_(ISS_module)" title="Columbus (ISS module)"><i>Columbus</i></a> module contributed by the <a href="/wiki/European_Space_Agency" title="European Space Agency">European Space Agency</a> and built in Germany, the <a href="/wiki/Kib%C5%8D_(ISS_module)" title="Kibō (ISS module)"><i>Kibō</i></a> module contributed by <a href="/wiki/JAXA" title="JAXA">Japan</a> and built at the <a href="/wiki/Tsukuba_Space_Center" title="Tsukuba Space Center">Tsukuba Space Center</a> and the <a href="/wiki/Institute_of_Space_and_Astronautical_Science" title="Institute of Space and Astronautical Science">Institute of Space and Astronautical Science</a>, along with the <a href="/wiki/Canadarm2" class="mw-redirect" title="Canadarm2">Canadarm2</a> and <a href="/wiki/Dextre" title="Dextre">Dextre</a>, a joint Canadian-U.S. endeavor. All of these components were shipped to the SSPF for launch processing.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-:4&#95;102-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:4-102"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>100<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-nasa-img-ksc-08pd0991&#95;105-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-nasa-img-ksc-08pd0991-105"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>103<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Assembly">Assembly</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main articles: <a href="/wiki/Assembly_of_the_International_Space_Station" title="Assembly of the International Space Station">Assembly of the International Space Station</a> and <a href="/wiki/List_of_ISS_spacewalks" class="mw-redirect" title="List of ISS spacewalks">List of ISS spacewalks</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size mw-halign-right" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:ISS-assembly-animation.gif" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/f/fb/ISS-assembly-animation.gif" decoding="async" width="400" height="225" class="mw-file-element" data-file-width="400" data-file-height="225" /></a><figcaption>Animation of the <a href="/wiki/Assembly_of_the_International_Space_Station" title="Assembly of the International Space Station">assembly of the International Space Station</a></figcaption></figure>
<p>The assembly of the International Space Station, a major endeavour in <a href="/wiki/Space_architecture" title="Space architecture">space architecture</a>, began in November 1998.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-OnOrbit&#95;12-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-OnOrbit-12"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>10<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Modules in the Russian segment launched and docked autonomously, with the exception of <i><a href="/wiki/Rassvet_(ISS_module)" title="Rassvet (ISS module)">Rassvet</a></i>. Other modules and components were delivered by the <a href="/wiki/Space_Shuttle" title="Space Shuttle">Space Shuttle</a>, which then had to be installed by astronauts either remotely using robotic arms or during spacewalks, more formally known as <a href="/wiki/Extravehicular_activity" title="Extravehicular activity">extra-vehicular activities</a> (EVAs). By 5&#160;June 2011 astronauts had made over 159 EVAs to add components to the station, totaling more than 1,000 hours in space.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-ISStD&#95;106-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-ISStD-106"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>104<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-mcc&#95;107-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-mcc-107"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>105<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:ISS_from_Atlantis_-_Sts101-714-016.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/ISS_from_Atlantis_-_Sts101-714-016.jpg/250px-ISS_from_Atlantis_-_Sts101-714-016.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="245" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/ISS_from_Atlantis_-_Sts101-714-016.jpg/500px-ISS_from_Atlantis_-_Sts101-714-016.jpg 2x" data-file-width="3197" data-file-height="3130" /></a><figcaption><i>Zarya</i> and <i>Unity</i>, the first two modules of the ISS, pictured in May 2000</figcaption></figure>
<p>The beginning of the core of the ISS's tenure in orbit was the launch of the Russian-built <i><a href="/wiki/Zarya_(ISS_module)" title="Zarya (ISS module)">Zarya</a></i> module atop a <a href="/wiki/Proton_(rocket_family)" title="Proton (rocket family)">Proton rocket</a> on 20&#160;November 1998. <i>Zarya</i> provided propulsion, <a href="/wiki/Spacecraft_attitude_control" class="mw-redirect" title="Spacecraft attitude control">attitude control</a>, communications, and electrical power. Two weeks later on 4 December 1998, the American-made <i><a href="/wiki/Unity_(ISS_module)" title="Unity (ISS module)">Unity</a></i> was ferried aboard <a href="/wiki/Space_Shuttle" title="Space Shuttle">Space Shuttle</a> <a href="/wiki/Space_Shuttle_Endeavour" title="Space Shuttle Endeavour"><i>Endeavour</i></a> on <a href="/wiki/STS-88" title="STS-88">STS-88</a> and joined with <i>Zarya</i>. <i>Unity</i> provided the connection between the Russian and US segments of the station and would provide ports to connect future modules and visiting spacecraft.
</p><p>While the connection of two modules built on different continents by nations that were once bitter rivals was a significant milestone, these two initial modules lacked life-support systems and the ISS remained unmanned for the next two years. At the time, the Russian station <i><a href="/wiki/Mir" title="Mir">Mir</a></i> was still inhabited.
</p><p>The turning point arrived in July 2000 with the launch of the <i><a href="/wiki/Zvezda_(ISS_module)" title="Zvezda (ISS module)">Zvezda</a></i> module. Equipped with living quarters and life-support systems, <i>Zvezda</i> enabled continuous human presence aboard the station. The first crew, <a href="/wiki/Expedition_1" title="Expedition 1">Expedition 1</a>, arrived that November aboard <a href="/wiki/Soyuz_TM-31" title="Soyuz TM-31">Soyuz TM-31</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-108" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-108"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>106<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-109" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-109"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>107<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The ISS grew steadily over the following years, with modules delivered by both Russian rockets and the Space Shuttle.
</p><p>Expedition 1 arrived midway between the Space Shuttle flights of missions <a href="/wiki/STS-92" title="STS-92">STS-92</a> and <a href="/wiki/STS-97" title="STS-97">STS-97</a>. These two flights each added segments of the station's <a href="/wiki/Integrated_Truss_Structure" title="Integrated Truss Structure">Integrated Truss Structure</a>, which provided the station with <a href="/wiki/Ku_band" title="Ku band">Ku band</a> communications, additional attitude control needed for the additional mass of the USOS, and additional solar arrays.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-110" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-110"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>108<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Over the next two years, the station continued to expand. A <a href="/wiki/Soyuz-U" title="Soyuz-U">Soyuz-U</a> rocket delivered the <a href="/wiki/Pirs_(ISS_module)" title="Pirs (ISS module)"><i>Pirs</i> docking compartment</a>. The Space Shuttles <i><a href="/wiki/Space_Shuttle_Discovery" title="Space Shuttle Discovery">Discovery</a></i>, <i><a href="/wiki/Space_Shuttle_Atlantis" title="Space Shuttle Atlantis">Atlantis</a></i>, and <i><a href="/wiki/Space_Shuttle_Endeavour" title="Space Shuttle Endeavour">Endeavour</a></i> delivered the American <i><a href="/wiki/Destiny_(ISS_module)" title="Destiny (ISS module)">Destiny</a></i> laboratory and <a href="/wiki/Quest_Joint_Airlock" title="Quest Joint Airlock"><i>Quest</i> airlock</a>, in addition to the station's main robot arm, the <a href="/wiki/Canadarm2" class="mw-redirect" title="Canadarm2">Canadarm2</a>, and several more segments of the Integrated Truss Structure.
</p><p>
Tragedy struck in 2003 with the <a href="/wiki/Space_Shuttle_Columbia_disaster" title="Space Shuttle Columbia disaster">loss of the Space Shuttle <i>Columbia</i></a>, which grounded the rest of the Shuttle fleet, halting construction of the ISS.</p><figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:International_Space_Station_after_undocking_of_STS-132.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/04/International_Space_Station_after_undocking_of_STS-132.jpg/250px-International_Space_Station_after_undocking_of_STS-132.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="159" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/04/International_Space_Station_after_undocking_of_STS-132.jpg/500px-International_Space_Station_after_undocking_of_STS-132.jpg 2x" data-file-width="3319" data-file-height="2116" /></a><figcaption>The ISS as seen from Space Shuttle Atlantis during <a href="/wiki/STS-132" title="STS-132">STS-132</a>, pictured in May 2010</figcaption></figure><p>Assembly resumed in 2006 with the arrival of <a href="/wiki/STS-115" title="STS-115">STS-115</a> with <i>Atlantis</i>, which delivered the station's second set of solar arrays. Several more truss segments and a third set of arrays were delivered on <a href="/wiki/STS-116" title="STS-116">STS-116</a>, <a href="/wiki/STS-117" title="STS-117">STS-117</a>, and <a href="/wiki/STS-118" title="STS-118">STS-118</a>. As a result of the major expansion of the station's power-generating capabilities, more modules could be accommodated, and the US <i><a href="/wiki/Harmony_(ISS_module)" title="Harmony (ISS module)">Harmony</a></i> module and <i><a href="/wiki/Columbus_(ISS_module)" title="Columbus (ISS module)">Columbus</a></i> European laboratory were added. These were soon followed by the first two components of the Japanese <i><a href="/wiki/Kibo_(ISS_module)" class="mw-redirect" title="Kibo (ISS module)">Kibō</a></i> laboratory. In March 2009, <a href="/wiki/STS-119" title="STS-119">STS-119</a> completed the Integrated Truss Structure with the installation of the fourth and final set of solar arrays. The final section of <i>Kibō</i> was delivered in July 2009 on <a href="/wiki/STS-127" title="STS-127">STS-127</a>, followed by the Russian <i><a href="/wiki/Poisk_(ISS_module)" title="Poisk (ISS module)">Poisk</a></i> module. The US <i><a href="/wiki/Tranquility_(ISS_module)" title="Tranquility (ISS module)">Tranquility</a></i> module was delivered in February 2010 during <a href="/wiki/STS-130" title="STS-130">STS-130</a>, alongside the <i><a href="/wiki/Cupola_(ISS_module)" title="Cupola (ISS module)">Cupola</a></i>, followed by the penultimate Russian module, <i><a href="/wiki/Rassvet_(ISS_module)" title="Rassvet (ISS module)">Rassvet</a></i>, in May 2010. <i>Rassvet</i> was delivered by Space Shuttle <i>Atlantis</i> on <a href="/wiki/STS-132" title="STS-132">STS-132</a> in exchange for the Russian Proton delivery of the US-funded <i>Zarya</i> module in 1998.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-111" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-111"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>109<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The last pressurised module of the USOS, <i><a href="/wiki/Leonardo_(ISS_module)" title="Leonardo (ISS module)">Leonardo</a></i>, was brought to the station in February 2011 on the final flight of <i>Discovery</i>, <a href="/wiki/STS-133" title="STS-133">STS-133</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-nasa-sts-133&#95;112-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-nasa-sts-133-112"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>110<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Russia's new primary research module <i>Nauka</i> docked in July 2021,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-tass-20210928&#95;113-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-tass-20210928-113"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>111<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> along with the European Robotic Arm which can relocate itself to different parts of the Russian modules of the station.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-tass-20190325&#95;114-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-tass-20190325-114"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>112<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Russia's latest addition, the <a href="/wiki/Prichal_(ISS_module)" title="Prichal (ISS module)"><i>Prichal</i></a> module, docked in November 2021.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-roscomos-20211126&#95;115-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-roscomos-20211126-115"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>113<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>As of June 2025, nasa.gov states that there are 43 different modules and elements installed on the ISS.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-ISS&#95;component&#95;number&#95;116-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-ISS_component_number-116"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>114<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Structure">Structure</h2></div>
<p>The ISS functions as a modular space station, enabling the addition or removal of modules from its structure for increased adaptability.
</p>
<ul class="gallery mw-gallery-packed">
		<li class="gallerybox" style="width: 349.33333333333px">
			<div class="thumb" style="width: 347.33333333333px;"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:ISS_blueprint.png" class="mw-file-description" title="Blueprint of ISS (as of 2018)"><img alt="Blueprint of ISS (as of 2018)" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/ISS_blueprint.png/960px-ISS_blueprint.png" decoding="async" width="348" height="200" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/ISS_blueprint.png/1280px-ISS_blueprint.png 2x" data-file-width="4728" data-file-height="2723" /></a></span></div>
			<div class="gallerytext">Blueprint of ISS (as of 2018)</div>
		</li>
		<li class="gallerybox" style="width: 358px">
			<div class="thumb" style="width: 356px;"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:Iss_after_installation_of_all_roll_out_solar_arrays.jpg" class="mw-file-description" title="Rendering of ISS (as of 2023)"><img alt="Rendering of ISS (as of 2023)" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/Iss_after_installation_of_all_roll_out_solar_arrays.jpg/960px-Iss_after_installation_of_all_roll_out_solar_arrays.jpg" decoding="async" width="356" height="200" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/Iss_after_installation_of_all_roll_out_solar_arrays.jpg/1280px-Iss_after_installation_of_all_roll_out_solar_arrays.jpg 2x" data-file-width="1500" data-file-height="844" /></a></span></div>
			<div class="gallerytext">Rendering of ISS (as of 2023)</div>
		</li>
</ul>
<p>Below is a diagram of major station components. The <i>Unity</i> node joins directly to the <i>Destiny</i> laboratory; for clarity, they are shown apart. Similar cases are also seen in other parts of the structure.
</p><p>Key to box background colors:
</p>
<ul><li><style data-mw-deduplicate="TemplateStyles:r981673959">.mw-parser-output .legend{page-break-inside:avoid;break-inside:avoid-column}.mw-parser-output .legend-color{display:inline-block;min-width:1.25em;height:1.25em;line-height:1.25;margin:1px 0;text-align:center;border:1px solid black;background-color:transparent;color:black}.mw-parser-output .legend-text{}</style><span class="legend-color mw-no-invert" style="forced-color-adjust:none; background-color:#CCDDFF; color:black;">&#160;</span> Pressurised component, accessible by the crew without using spacesuits</li>
<li><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r981673959" /><span class="legend-color mw-no-invert" style="forced-color-adjust:none; background-color:#B9E192; color:black;">&#160;</span> <a href="/wiki/Docking_and_berthing_of_spacecraft" title="Docking and berthing of spacecraft">Docking/berthing port</a>, pressurized when a visiting spacecraft is present</li>
<li><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r981673959" /><span class="legend-color mw-no-invert" style="forced-color-adjust:none; background-color:#DCC5ED; color:black;">&#160;</span> <a href="/wiki/Airlock" title="Airlock">Airlock</a>, to move people or material between pressurized and unpressurized environment</li>
<li><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r981673959" /><span class="legend-color mw-no-invert" style="forced-color-adjust:none; background-color:#FFCCCC; color:black;">&#160;</span> Unpressurised station superstructure</li>
<li><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r981673959" /><span class="legend-color mw-no-invert" style="forced-color-adjust:none; background-color:#FFF4CC; color:black;">&#160;</span> Unpressurised component</li>
<li><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r981673959" /><span class="legend-color mw-no-invert" style="forced-color-adjust:none; background-color:#D2691E; color:black;">&#160;</span> Temporarily defunct or non-commissioned component</li>
<li><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r981673959" /><span class="legend-color mw-no-invert" style="forced-color-adjust:none; background-color:#808080; color:black;">&#160;</span> Former, no longer installed component</li>
<li><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r981673959" /><span class="legend-color mw-no-invert" style="forced-color-adjust:none; background-color:#FFFFFF; color:black;">&#160;</span> Future, not yet installed component</li></ul>

<tr style="height:1px;text-align:center"><td colspan="2" rowspan="2" style="height:2em;width:2em"></td><td colspan="2" rowspan="2" style="height:2em;width:2em"></td><td colspan="2" rowspan="2" style="height:2em;width:2em"></td><td colspan="2" rowspan="2" style="height:2em;width:2em"></td><td colspan="2" rowspan="2" style="height:2em;width:2em"></td><td colspan="2" rowspan="2" style="height:2em;width:2em"></td><td colspan="2" rowspan="2" style="height:2em;width:2em"></td><td colspan="2" rowspan="2" style="height:2em;width:2em"></td><td colspan="2" rowspan="2" style="height:2em;width:2em"></td><td colspan="2" rowspan="2" style="height:2em;width:2em"></td><td colspan="2" rowspan="2" style="height:2em;width:2em"></td><td rowspan="2" style="border-right:1px solid;height:2em;width:1em"></td><td rowspan="2" style="height:2em;width:1em"></td><td rowspan="2" style="border-right:1px solid;height:2em;width:1em"></td><td rowspan="2" style="height:2em;width:1em"></td></tr><tr style="height:1px;text-align:center"></tr>

</tbody></table>
</div>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Pressurised_modules">Pressurised modules</h3></div>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Zarya"><i>Zarya</i></h4></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Zarya_(ISS_module)" title="Zarya (ISS module)">Zarya (ISS module)</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Zarya_from_STS-88.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/db/Zarya_from_STS-88.jpg/250px-Zarya_from_STS-88.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="277" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/db/Zarya_from_STS-88.jpg/500px-Zarya_from_STS-88.jpg 2x" data-file-width="1792" data-file-height="1984" /></a><figcaption><i>Zarya</i> as seen by <span class="nowrap"><a href="/wiki/Space_Shuttle" title="Space Shuttle">Space Shuttle</a></span>&#32;<a href="/wiki/Space_Shuttle_Endeavour" title="Space Shuttle Endeavour"><i>Endeavour</i></a> during <a href="/wiki/STS-88" title="STS-88">STS-88</a></figcaption></figure>
<p><i>Zarya</i> (<a href="/wiki/Russian_language" title="Russian language">Russian</a>: <span lang="ru">Заря</span>, <small><a href="/wiki/Literal_translation" title="Literal translation">lit.</a>&#8201;</small><span class="gloss-quot">'</span><span class="gloss-text">Sunrise</span><span class="gloss-quot">'</span><sup id="cite&#95;ref-120" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-120"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>e<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>), also known as the <i>Functional Cargo Block</i> (Russian: <span lang="ru">Функционально-грузовой блок</span>), was the inaugural component of the ISS. Launched in 1998, it initially served as the ISS's power source, storage, propulsion, and guidance system. As the station has grown, <i>Zarya'</i>s role has transitioned primarily to storage, both internally and in its external fuel tanks.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-NASA2&#95;121-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-NASA2-121"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>116<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>A descendant of the <a href="/wiki/TKS_(spacecraft)" title="TKS (spacecraft)">TKS spacecraft</a> used in the <a href="/wiki/Salyut_program" class="mw-redirect" title="Salyut program"><i>Salyut</i> program</a>, <i>Zarya</i> was built in Russia but is owned by the United States. Its name symbolizes the beginning of a new era of international space cooperation.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-russianspaceweb-enterprise&#95;122-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-russianspaceweb-enterprise-122"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>117<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Unity"><i>Unity</i></h4></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Unity_(ISS_module)" title="Unity (ISS module)">Unity (ISS module)</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:ISS_Unity_module.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/ISS_Unity_module.jpg/250px-ISS_Unity_module.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="170" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/ISS_Unity_module.jpg/500px-ISS_Unity_module.jpg 2x" data-file-width="2392" data-file-height="1631" /></a><figcaption><i>Unity</i> as seen by <span class="nowrap"><a href="/wiki/Space_Shuttle" title="Space Shuttle">Space Shuttle</a></span>&#32;<a href="/wiki/Space_Shuttle_Endeavour" title="Space Shuttle Endeavour"><i>Endeavour</i></a> during <a href="/wiki/STS-88" title="STS-88">STS-88</a></figcaption></figure>
<p><i>Unity</i>, also known as <i>Node 1</i>, is the inaugural U.S.-built component of the ISS.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-nssdca-1998-069f&#95;123-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-nssdca-1998-069f-123"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>118<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-124" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-124"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>119<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Serving as the connection between the Russian and U.S. segments, this cylindrical module features six <a href="/wiki/Common_Berthing_Mechanism" title="Common Berthing Mechanism">Common Berthing Mechanism</a> locations (<a href="/wiki/Forward_(aircraft)" class="mw-redirect" title="Forward (aircraft)">forward</a>, <a href="/wiki/Aft" class="mw-redirect" title="Aft">aft</a>, <a href="/wiki/Port_(nautical)" class="mw-redirect" title="Port (nautical)">port</a>, <a href="/wiki/Starboard" class="mw-redirect" title="Starboard">starboard</a>, <a href="/wiki/Zenith" title="Zenith">zenith</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Nadir" title="Nadir">nadir</a>) for attaching additional modules. Measuring 4.57 metres (15.0&#160;ft) in diameter and 5.47 metres (17.9&#160;ft) in length, <i>Unity</i> was constructed of steel by <a href="/wiki/Boeing" title="Boeing">Boeing</a> for NASA at the <a href="/wiki/Marshall_Space_Flight_Center" title="Marshall Space Flight Center">Marshall Space Flight Center</a> in <a href="/wiki/Huntsville,_Alabama" title="Huntsville, Alabama">Huntsville, Alabama</a>. It was the first of three connecting nodes – <i>Unity</i>, <i>Harmony</i>, and <i>Tranquility</i> – that forms the structural backbone of the U.S. segment of the ISS.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-nasa-roy-20091020&#95;125-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-nasa-roy-20091020-125"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>120<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Zvezda"><i>Zvezda</i></h4></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Zvezda_(ISS_module)" title="Zvezda (ISS module)">Zvezda (ISS module)</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Zvezda_(tight_crop).jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/Zvezda_%28tight_crop%29.jpg/250px-Zvezda_%28tight_crop%29.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="95" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/Zvezda_%28tight_crop%29.jpg/500px-Zvezda_%28tight_crop%29.jpg 2x" data-file-width="1357" data-file-height="516" /></a><figcaption><i>Zvezda</i> as seen by <span class="nowrap"><a href="/wiki/Space_Shuttle" title="Space Shuttle">Space Shuttle</a></span>&#32;<a href="/wiki/Space_Shuttle_Atlantis" title="Space Shuttle Atlantis"><i>Atlantis</i></a> during <a href="/wiki/STS-106" title="STS-106">STS-106</a></figcaption></figure>
<p><i>Zvezda</i> (Russian: <span lang="ru">Звезда</span>, <small><abbr title="Literal translation">lit.</abbr>&#8201;</small><span class="gloss-quot">'</span><span class="gloss-text">star</span><span class="gloss-quot">'</span>) launched in July 2000, is the core of the <a href="/wiki/Russian_Orbital_Segment" title="Russian Orbital Segment">Russian Orbital Segment</a> of the ISS. Initially providing essential living quarters and <a href="/wiki/Life-support_system" title="Life-support system">life-support systems</a>, it enabled the first continuous human presence aboard the station. While additional modules have expanded the ISS's capabilities, Zvezda remains the command and control center for the Russian segment and it is where crews gather during emergencies.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-126" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-126"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>121<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-127" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-127"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>122<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-128" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-128"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>123<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>A descendant of the Salyut program's DOS spacecraft, Zvezda was built by <a href="/wiki/RKK_Energia" class="mw-redirect" title="RKK Energia">RKK Energia</a> and launched atop a <a href="/wiki/Proton_(rocket_family)" title="Proton (rocket family)">Proton rocket</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-ruspace&#95;129-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-ruspace-129"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>124<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Destiny"><i>Destiny</i></h4></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Destiny_(ISS_module)" title="Destiny (ISS module)">Destiny (ISS module)</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:ISS_Destiny_Lab.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/ISS_Destiny_Lab.jpg/250px-ISS_Destiny_Lab.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="200" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/ISS_Destiny_Lab.jpg/500px-ISS_Destiny_Lab.jpg 2x" data-file-width="2336" data-file-height="1868" /></a><figcaption>The <i>Destiny</i> module being installed on the ISS</figcaption></figure>
<p>The <i>Destiny</i> laboratory is the primary research facility for U.S. experiments on the ISS. NASA's first permanent orbital research station since Skylab, the module was built by Boeing and launched aboard <span class="nowrap"><a href="/wiki/Space_Shuttle" title="Space Shuttle">Space Shuttle</a></span>&#32;<a href="/wiki/Space_Shuttle_Atlantis" title="Space Shuttle Atlantis"><i>Atlantis</i></a> during <a href="/wiki/STS-98" title="STS-98">STS-98</a>. Attached to <i>Unity</i> over a period of five days in February 2001, <i>Destiny</i> has been a hub for scientific research ever since.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-boeing&#95;130-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-boeing-130"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>125<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-lab&#95;131-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-lab-131"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>126<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-sts-98&#95;132-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-sts-98-132"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>127<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Within <i>Destiny</i>, astronauts conduct experiments in fields such as medicine, engineering, biotechnology, physics, materials science, and Earth science. Researchers worldwide benefit from these studies. The module also houses life-support systems, including the <a href="/wiki/Oxygen_Generating_System" class="mw-redirect" title="Oxygen Generating System">Oxygen Generating System</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-133" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-133"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>128<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Quest_Joint_Airlock"><i>Quest Joint Airlock</i></h4></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Quest_Joint_Airlock" title="Quest Joint Airlock">Quest Joint Airlock</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Quest_airlock_exterior_-_STS-127.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/Quest_airlock_exterior_-_STS-127.jpg/250px-Quest_airlock_exterior_-_STS-127.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="225" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/Quest_airlock_exterior_-_STS-127.jpg/500px-Quest_airlock_exterior_-_STS-127.jpg 2x" data-file-width="2882" data-file-height="2590" /></a><figcaption><i>Quest</i> Joint Airlock Module</figcaption></figure>
<p>The <i>Quest Joint Airlock</i> enables <a href="/wiki/Extravehicular_activity" title="Extravehicular activity">extravehicular activities</a> (EVAs) using either the U.S. <a href="/wiki/Extravehicular_Mobility_Unit" title="Extravehicular Mobility Unit">Extravehicular Mobility Unit</a> (EMU) or the Russian <a href="/wiki/Orlan_space_suit" title="Orlan space suit">Orlan space suit</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-nasa-questairlock&#95;134-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-nasa-questairlock-134"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>129<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Before its installation, conducting EVAs from the ISS was challenging due to a variety of system and design differences. Only the Orlan suit could be used from the Transfer Chamber on the <i>Zvezda</i> module (which was not a purpose-built airlock) and the EMU could only be used from the airlock on a visiting Space Shuttle, which could not accommodate the Orlan.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-usaf-2010&#95;135-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-usaf-2010-135"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>130<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Launched aboard <span class="nowrap"><a href="/wiki/Space_Shuttle" title="Space Shuttle">Space Shuttle</a></span>&#32;<a href="/wiki/Space_Shuttle_Atlantis" title="Space Shuttle Atlantis"><i>Atlantis</i></a> during <a href="/wiki/STS-104" title="STS-104">STS-104</a> in July 2001 and attached to the Unity module, Quest is a 6.1-metre-long (20&#160;ft), 4.0-metre-wide (13&#160;ft) structure built by Boeing.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-nasa-20210714&#95;136-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-nasa-20210714-136"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>131<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> It houses the crew airlock for astronaut egress, an equipment airlock for suit storage, and has facilities to accommodate astronauts during their overnight pre-breathe procedures to prevent decompression sickness.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-usaf-2010&#95;135-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-usaf-2010-135"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>130<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The crew airlock, derived from the Space Shuttle, features essential equipment like lighting, handrails, and an Umbilical Interface Assembly (UIA) that provides life-support and communication systems for up to two spacesuits simultaneously. These can be either two EMUs, two Orlan suits, or one of each design.
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Poisk"><i>Poisk</i></h4></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Poisk_(ISS_module)" title="Poisk (ISS module)">Poisk (ISS module)</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:STS-129_ISS-21_Poisk.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/be/STS-129_ISS-21_Poisk.jpg/250px-STS-129_ISS-21_Poisk.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="166" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/be/STS-129_ISS-21_Poisk.jpg/500px-STS-129_ISS-21_Poisk.jpg 2x" data-file-width="4288" data-file-height="2848" /></a><figcaption><i>Poisk</i> with its own propulsion module (soon to be jettisoned)</figcaption></figure>
<p><i>Poisk</i> (Russian: <span lang="ru">По́иск</span>, <small><abbr title="Literal translation">lit.</abbr>&#8201;</small><span class="gloss-quot">'</span><span class="gloss-text">Search</span><span class="gloss-quot">'</span>), also known as the <i>Mini-Research Module 2</i> (Russian: <span lang="ru">Малый исследовательский модуль 2</span>), serves as both a secondary airlock on the Russian segment of the ISS and supports docking for Soyuz and Progress spacecraft, facilitates propellant transfers from the latter.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-137" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-137"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>132<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Launched on 10&#160;November 2009 attached to a modified <a href="/wiki/Progress_spacecraft" class="mw-redirect" title="Progress spacecraft">Progress spacecraft</a>, called <a href="/wiki/Progress_M-MIM2" title="Progress M-MIM2">Progress M-MIM2</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-energia0828&#95;138-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-energia0828-138"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>133<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-sfn-20091110&#95;139-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-sfn-20091110-139"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>134<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p><i>Poisk</i> provides facilities to maintain Orlan spacesuits and is equipped with two inward-opening hatches, a design change from <i>Mir</i>, which encountered a dangerous situation caused by an outward-opening hatch that opened too quickly because of a small amount of air pressure remaining in the airlock.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-russianspaceweb-mir-close-calls&#95;140-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-russianspaceweb-mir-close-calls-140"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>135<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Since the departure of <a href="/wiki/Pirs_(ISS_module)" title="Pirs (ISS module)"><i>Pirs</i></a> in 2021, it's become the sole airlock on the Russian segment.
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Harmony"><i>Harmony</i></h4></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Harmony_(ISS_module)" title="Harmony (ISS module)">Harmony (ISS module)</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Node_2_-_STS-134.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/Node_2_-_STS-134.jpg/250px-Node_2_-_STS-134.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="256" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/Node_2_-_STS-134.jpg/500px-Node_2_-_STS-134.jpg 2x" data-file-width="804" data-file-height="822" /></a><figcaption><i>Harmony</i> (center) shown connected to <i>Columbus</i>, <i>Kibo</i>, and <i>Destiny</i>. The dark <a href="/wiki/Pressurized_Mating_Adapter" title="Pressurized Mating Adapter">PMA-2</a> faces the camera. The nadir and zenith locations are open.</figcaption></figure>
<p><i>Harmony</i>, or <i>Node 2</i>, is the central connecting hub of the US segment of the ISS, linking the U.S., European, and Japanese laboratory modules. It's also been called the "utility hub" of the ISS as it provides essential power, data, and life-support systems. The module also houses sleeping quarters for four crew members.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-141" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-141"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>136<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Launched on 23&#160;October 2007 aboard <span class="nowrap"><a href="/wiki/Space_Shuttle" title="Space Shuttle">Space Shuttle</a></span>&#32;<a href="/wiki/Space_Shuttle_Discovery" title="Space Shuttle Discovery"><i>Discovery</i></a> on <a href="/wiki/STS-120" title="STS-120">STS-120</a>,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-launch&#95;142-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-launch-142"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>137<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-lat&#95;143-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-lat-143"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>138<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Harmony was initially attached to the Unity<sup id="cite&#95;ref-harmmate&#95;144-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-harmmate-144"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>139<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-room&#95;145-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-room-145"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>140<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> before being relocated to its permanent position at the front of the Destiny laboratory on 14&#160;November 2007.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-pma3&#95;146-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-pma3-146"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>141<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> This expansion added significant living space to the ISS, marking a key milestone in the construction of the U.S. segment.
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Tranquility"><i>Tranquility</i></h4></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Tranquility_(ISS_module)" title="Tranquility (ISS module)">Tranquility (ISS module)</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Node_3_-_Isolated_view.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/Node_3_-_Isolated_view.jpg/250px-Node_3_-_Isolated_view.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="229" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/e/ea/Node_3_-_Isolated_view.jpg 2x" data-file-width="448" data-file-height="411" /></a><figcaption><i>Tranquility</i> in 2011</figcaption></figure>
<p><i>Tranquility</i>, also known as <i>Node 3</i>, is a module of the ISS. It contains environmental control systems, <a href="/wiki/Life-support_system" title="Life-support system">life-support systems</a>, a toilet, exercise equipment, and an observation <a href="/wiki/Cupola_(ISS_module)" title="Cupola (ISS module)">cupola</a>.
</p><p>The European Space Agency and the <a href="/wiki/Italian_Space_Agency" title="Italian Space Agency">Italian Space Agency</a> had <i>Tranquility</i> manufactured by <a href="/wiki/Thales_Alenia_Space" title="Thales Alenia Space">Thales Alenia Space</a>. A ceremony on 20&#160;November 2009 transferred ownership of the module to NASA.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-147" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-147"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>142<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> On 8&#160;February 2010, NASA launched the module on the Space Shuttle's <a href="/wiki/STS-130" title="STS-130">STS-130</a> mission.
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Columbus"><i>Columbus</i></h4></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Columbus_(ISS_module)" title="Columbus (ISS module)">Columbus (ISS module)</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Columbus_module_-_cropped.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ec/Columbus_module_-_cropped.jpg/250px-Columbus_module_-_cropped.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="167" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ec/Columbus_module_-_cropped.jpg/500px-Columbus_module_-_cropped.jpg 2x" data-file-width="3828" data-file-height="2555" /></a><figcaption>The <i>Columbus</i> module on the ISS</figcaption></figure>
<p><i>Columbus</i> is a science laboratory that is part of the ISS and is the largest single contribution to the station made by the European Space Agency.
</p><p>Like the <i>Harmony</i> and <i>Tranquility</i> modules, the <i>Columbus</i> laboratory was constructed in <a href="/wiki/Turin" title="Turin">Turin</a>, Italy by <a href="/wiki/Thales_Alenia_Space" title="Thales Alenia Space">Thales Alenia Space</a>. The functional equipment and software of the lab was designed by <a href="/wiki/EADS" class="mw-redirect" title="EADS">EADS</a> in <a href="/wiki/Bremen" title="Bremen">Bremen</a>, Germany. It was also integrated in Bremen before being flown to the Kennedy Space Center in Florida in an <a href="/wiki/Airbus_Beluga" title="Airbus Beluga">Airbus Beluga</a> jet. It was launched aboard Space Shuttle <i>Atlantis</i> on 7&#160;February 2008, on flight <a href="/wiki/STS-122" title="STS-122">STS-122</a>. It is designed for ten years of operation. The module is controlled by the <a href="/w/index.php?title=Columbus_Control_center&amp;action=edit&amp;redlink=1" class="new" title="Columbus Control center (page does not exist)">Columbus Control center</a>, located at the <a href="/wiki/German_Space_Operations_Center" title="German Space Operations Center">German Space Operations Center</a>, part of the <a href="/wiki/German_Aerospace_Center" title="German Aerospace Center">German Aerospace Center</a> in <a href="/wiki/Oberpfaffenhofen" title="Oberpfaffenhofen">Oberpfaffenhofen</a> near <a href="/wiki/Munich" title="Munich">Munich</a>, Germany.
</p><p>The European Space Agency has spent <a href="/wiki/Euro" title="Euro">€</a>1.4 billion (about <a href="/wiki/United_States_dollar" title="United States dollar">US$</a>1.6 billion) on building <i>Columbus</i>, including the experiments it carries and the ground control infrastructure necessary to operate them.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-sfn-20080211&#95;148-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-sfn-20080211-148"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>143<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Kibō"><span id="Kib.C5.8D"></span><i>Kibō</i></h4></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Kib%C5%8D_(ISS_module)" title="Kibō (ISS module)">Kibō (ISS module)</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Japanese_Experiment_Module_exterior_-_cropped.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/Japanese_Experiment_Module_exterior_-_cropped.jpg/250px-Japanese_Experiment_Module_exterior_-_cropped.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="174" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/Japanese_Experiment_Module_exterior_-_cropped.jpg/500px-Japanese_Experiment_Module_exterior_-_cropped.jpg 2x" data-file-width="2300" data-file-height="1600" /></a><figcaption><i>Kibō</i> with its exposed facility on the right</figcaption></figure>
<p><i>Kibō</i><span style="font-weight: normal"> (<a href="/wiki/Japanese_language" title="Japanese language">Japanese</a>: <span lang="ja">きぼう</span>; <abbr style="font-size:85%" title="literal translation">lit.</abbr><span style="white-space: nowrap;">&#8201;</span><span class="gloss-quot">'</span><span class="gloss-text">hope</span><span class="gloss-quot">'</span>)</span>, also known as the <i>Japanese Experiment Module</i>, is Japan's research facility on the ISS. It is the largest single module on the ISS, consisting of a pressurized lab, an exposed facility for conducting experiments in the space environment, two storage compartments, and a robotic arm. Attached to the <i>Harmony</i> module, <i>Kibō</i> was assembled in space over three Space Shuttle missions: <a href="/wiki/STS-123" title="STS-123">STS-123</a>, <a href="/wiki/STS-124" title="STS-124">STS-124</a> and <a href="/wiki/STS-127" title="STS-127">STS-127</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-japantimes-20090630&#95;149-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-japantimes-20090630-149"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>144<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Cupola"><i>Cupola</i></h4></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Cupola_(ISS_module)" title="Cupola (ISS module)">Cupola (ISS module)</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:STS130_cupola_view1.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/STS130_cupola_view1.jpg/250px-STS130_cupola_view1.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="166" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/STS130_cupola_view1.jpg/500px-STS130_cupola_view1.jpg 2x" data-file-width="4288" data-file-height="2848" /></a><figcaption>The <i>Cupola</i><span class="nowrap" style="padding-left:0.1em;">&#39;s</span> windows with shutters open</figcaption></figure>
<p>The <i>Cupola</i> is an <a href="/wiki/ESA" class="mw-redirect" title="ESA">ESA</a>-built observatory module of the ISS. Its name derives from the Italian word <i><span title="Italian-language text"><i lang="it">cupola</i></span></i>, which means "<a href="/wiki/Dome" title="Dome">dome</a>". Its seven windows are used to conduct experiments, dockings and observations of Earth. It was launched aboard Space Shuttle mission STS-130 on 8 February 2010 and attached to the <i>Tranquility</i> (Node 3) module. With the <i>Cupola</i> attached, ISS assembly reached 85 per cent completion. The <i>Cupola</i><span class="nowrap" style="padding-left:0.1em;">&#39;s</span> central window has a diameter of 80&#160;cm (31&#160;in).<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Cupola:&#95;a&#95;window&#95;over&#95;the&#95;Earth&#95;150-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Cupola:_a_window_over_the_Earth-150"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>145<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Rassvet"><i>Rassvet</i></h4></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Rassvet_(ISS_module)" title="Rassvet (ISS module)">Rassvet (ISS module)</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:STS132_Mini-Research_Module-1.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/STS132_Mini-Research_Module-1.jpg/250px-STS132_Mini-Research_Module-1.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="167" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/STS132_Mini-Research_Module-1.jpg/500px-STS132_Mini-Research_Module-1.jpg 2x" data-file-width="3000" data-file-height="2000" /></a><figcaption><i>Rassvet</i> module with MLM-outfitting equipment (consisting of experiment airlock, RTOd radiators, and ERA workpost) at KSC</figcaption></figure>
<p><i>Rassvet</i> (Russian: <span lang="ru">Рассвет</span>, <small><abbr title="Literal translation">lit.</abbr>&#8201;</small><span class="gloss-quot">'</span><span class="gloss-text">first light</span><span class="gloss-quot">'</span>), also known as the <i>Mini-Research Module 1</i> (Russian: <span lang="ru">Малый исследовательский модуль 1</span>) and formerly known as the <i>Docking Cargo Module</i> is primarily used for cargo storage and as a docking port for visiting spacecraft on the Russian segment of the ISS. <i>Rassvet</i> replaced the cancelled Docking and Storage Module and used a design largely based on the <i><a href="/wiki/Mir_Docking_Module" title="Mir Docking Module">Mir Docking Module</a></i> built in 1995.
</p><p>Rassvet was delivered in on 14&#160;May 2010 <span class="nowrap"><a href="/wiki/Space_Shuttle" title="Space Shuttle">Space Shuttle</a></span>&#32;<a href="/wiki/Space_Shuttle_Atlantis" title="Space Shuttle Atlantis"><i>Atlantis</i></a> on <a href="/wiki/STS-132" title="STS-132">STS-132</a> in exchange for the Russian Proton delivery of the US-funded <i>Zarya</i> module in 1998.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-nsf-20090409&#95;151-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-nsf-20090409-151"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>146<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> <i>Rassvet</i> was attached to <i>Zarya</i> shortly thereafter.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-connect&#95;152-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-connect-152"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>147<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Leonardo"><i>Leonardo</i></h4></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Leonardo_(ISS_module)" title="Leonardo (ISS module)">Leonardo (ISS module)</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:STS-133_ISS-26_Permanent_Multipurpose_Module.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/STS-133_ISS-26_Permanent_Multipurpose_Module.jpg/250px-STS-133_ISS-26_Permanent_Multipurpose_Module.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="289" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/STS-133_ISS-26_Permanent_Multipurpose_Module.jpg/500px-STS-133_ISS-26_Permanent_Multipurpose_Module.jpg 2x" data-file-width="2320" data-file-height="2682" /></a><figcaption>The <i>Leonardo</i> module hours after berthing</figcaption></figure>
<p>The <i>Leonardo</i> Permanent Multipurpose Module (PMM) was flown into space aboard the Space Shuttle <i>Discovery</i> on <a href="/wiki/STS-133" title="STS-133">STS-133</a> on 24 February 2011 and installed on 1 March. <i>Leonardo</i> is primarily used for storage of spares, supplies and waste on the ISS, which was until then stored in many different places within the space station. It is also the personal hygiene area for the astronauts who live in the <a href="/wiki/US_Orbital_Segment" title="US Orbital Segment">US Orbital Segment</a>. The <i>Leonardo</i> PMM was a <a href="/wiki/Multi-Purpose_Logistics_Module" title="Multi-Purpose Logistics Module">Multi-Purpose Logistics Module</a> (MPLM) before 2011, but was modified into its current configuration. It was formerly one of two MPLM used for bringing cargo to and from the ISS with the Space Shuttle. The module was named for Italian polymath <a href="/wiki/Leonardo_da_Vinci" title="Leonardo da Vinci">Leonardo da Vinci</a>.
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Bigelow_Expandable_Activity_Module">Bigelow Expandable Activity Module</h4></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:BEAM_module_expansion_series.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/BEAM_module_expansion_series.jpg/250px-BEAM_module_expansion_series.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="169" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/BEAM_module_expansion_series.jpg/500px-BEAM_module_expansion_series.jpg 2x" data-file-width="698" data-file-height="472" /></a><figcaption>Progression of the expansion of BEAM</figcaption></figure>
<p>The <a href="/wiki/Bigelow_Expandable_Activity_Module" title="Bigelow Expandable Activity Module">Bigelow Expandable Activity Module</a> (BEAM) is an experimental <a href="/wiki/Inflatable_space_habitat" title="Inflatable space habitat">expandable</a> <a href="#Pressurised_modules">space station module</a> developed by <a href="/wiki/Bigelow_Aerospace" title="Bigelow Aerospace">Bigelow Aerospace</a>, under contract to NASA, for testing as a temporary module on the International Space Station (ISS) from 2016 to at least 2020. It arrived at the ISS on 10&#160;April 2016,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-space20160410&#95;153-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-space20160410-153"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>148<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> was berthed to the station on 16&#160;April at Tranquility Node 3, and was expanded and pressurized on 28&#160;May 2016. In December 2021, Bigelow Aerospace conveyed ownership of the module to NASA, as a result of Bigelow's cessation of activity.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-154" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-154"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>149<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="International_Docking_Adapters">International Docking Adapters</h4></div>
<p>The <a href="/wiki/International_Docking_Adapter" title="International Docking Adapter">International Docking Adapter</a> (IDA) is a <a href="/wiki/Docking_and_berthing_of_spacecraft#Adapters" title="Docking and berthing of spacecraft">spacecraft docking system adapter</a> developed to convert <a href="/wiki/APAS-95" class="mw-redirect" title="APAS-95">APAS-95</a> to the <a href="/wiki/NASA_Docking_System" title="NASA Docking System">NASA Docking System</a> (NDS). An IDA is placed on each of the ISS's two open <a href="/wiki/Pressurized_Mating_Adapters" class="mw-redirect" title="Pressurized Mating Adapters">Pressurized Mating Adapters</a> (PMAs), both of which are connected to the <i>Harmony</i> module.
</p><p>Two International Docking Adapters are currently installed aboard the Station. Originally, <a href="/wiki/International_Docking_Adapter" title="International Docking Adapter">IDA-1</a> was planned to be installed on PMA-2, located at <i>Harmony</i><span class="nowrap" style="padding-left:0.1em;">&#39;</span>s forward port, and <a href="/wiki/International_Docking_Adapter#IDA-2" title="International Docking Adapter">IDA-2</a> would be installed on PMA-3 at <i>Harmony</i><span class="nowrap" style="padding-left:0.1em;">&#39;</span>s zenith. After IDA 1 was destroyed in <a href="/wiki/SpaceX_CRS-7" title="SpaceX CRS-7">a launch incident</a>, <a href="/wiki/International_Docking_Adapter#IDA-2" title="International Docking Adapter">IDA-2</a> was installed on PMA-2 on 19 August 2016,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-sfn-20160819&#95;155-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-sfn-20160819-155"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>150<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> while <a href="/wiki/International_Docking_Adapter#IDA-3" title="International Docking Adapter">IDA-3</a> was later installed on PMA-3 on 21 August 2019.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-156" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-156"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>151<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Bishop_Airlock_Module">Bishop Airlock Module</h4></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Nanoracks_Bishop_Airlock" title="Nanoracks Bishop Airlock">Nanoracks Bishop Airlock</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:ISS-64_NanoRacks_Bishop_airlock_after_installation.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/ISS-64_NanoRacks_Bishop_airlock_after_installation.jpg/250px-ISS-64_NanoRacks_Bishop_airlock_after_installation.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="140" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/ISS-64_NanoRacks_Bishop_airlock_after_installation.jpg/500px-ISS-64_NanoRacks_Bishop_airlock_after_installation.jpg 2x" data-file-width="4928" data-file-height="2768" /></a><figcaption>NanoRacks Bishop airlock module installed on the ISS</figcaption></figure>
<p>The NanoRacks Bishop Airlock Module is a <a href="/wiki/Commercial_use_of_space" title="Commercial use of space">commercially</a> funded <a href="/wiki/Airlock" title="Airlock">airlock</a> module launched to the ISS on <a href="/wiki/SpaceX_CRS-21" title="SpaceX CRS-21">SpaceX CRS-21</a> on 6 December 2020.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-thales-milestone&#95;157-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-thales-milestone-157"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>152<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-sfn-20190802&#95;158-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-sfn-20190802-158"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>153<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The module was built by <a href="/wiki/NanoRacks" class="mw-redirect" title="NanoRacks">NanoRacks</a>, <a href="/wiki/Thales_Alenia_Space" title="Thales Alenia Space">Thales Alenia Space</a>, and Boeing.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-NanoRacks&#95;159-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-NanoRacks-159"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>154<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> It will be used to deploy <a href="/wiki/CubeSat" title="CubeSat">CubeSats</a>, <a href="/wiki/Small_satellite" title="Small satellite">small satellites</a>, and other external payloads for NASA, <a href="/wiki/CASIS" class="mw-redirect" title="CASIS">CASIS</a>, and other commercial and governmental customers.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-NASA&#95;Announcement&#95;160-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-NASA_Announcement-160"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>155<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Nauka"><i>Nauka</i></h4></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Russian_Spacewalkers_dwarfed_by_the_Nauka_and_Prichal_modules_2_(cropped).jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/Russian_Spacewalkers_dwarfed_by_the_Nauka_and_Prichal_modules_2_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Russian_Spacewalkers_dwarfed_by_the_Nauka_and_Prichal_modules_2_%28cropped%29.jpg" decoding="async" width="190" height="285" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/Russian_Spacewalkers_dwarfed_by_the_Nauka_and_Prichal_modules_2_%28cropped%29.jpg/500px-Russian_Spacewalkers_dwarfed_by_the_Nauka_and_Prichal_modules_2_%28cropped%29.jpg 2x" data-file-width="2724" data-file-height="4082" /></a><figcaption><i>Nauka</i> and <i>Prichal</i> docked to ISS</figcaption></figure>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Nauka_(ISS_module)" title="Nauka (ISS module)">Nauka (ISS module)</a></div>
<p><i>Nauka</i> (Russian: <span lang="ru">Наука</span>, <small><abbr title="Literal translation">lit.</abbr>&#8201;</small><span class="gloss-quot">'</span><span class="gloss-text">Science</span><span class="gloss-quot">'</span>), also known as the <i>Multipurpose Laboratory Module, Upgrade</i> (Russian: <span lang="ru">Многоцелевой лабораторный модуль, усоверше́нствованный</span>), is a Roscosmos-funded component of the ISS that was launched on 21&#160;July 2021, 14:58 UTC. In the original ISS plans, <i>Nauka</i> was to use the location of the <a href="/wiki/Rassvet_(ISS_module)#Initial_planning" title="Rassvet (ISS module)">Docking and Stowage Module</a> (DSM), but the DSM was later replaced by the <i>Rassvet</i> module and moved to <i>Zarya</i><span class="nowrap" style="padding-left:0.1em;">&#39;</span>s nadir port. <i>Nauka</i> was successfully docked to <i>Zvezda</i><span class="nowrap" style="padding-left:0.1em;">&#39;</span>s nadir port on 29&#160;July 2021, 13:29 UTC, replacing the <i>Pirs</i> module.
</p><p>It had a temporary docking adapter on its nadir port for crewed and uncrewed missions until Prichal arrival, where just before its arrival it was removed by a departing Progress spacecraft.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-russianspaceweb-ms17&#95;161-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-russianspaceweb-ms17-161"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>156<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Prichal"><i>Prichal</i></h4></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Prichal_(ISS_module)" title="Prichal (ISS module)">Prichal (ISS module)</a></div>
<p><i>Prichal</i> (Russian: <span lang="ru">Причал</span>, <small><abbr title="Literal translation">lit.</abbr>&#8201;</small><span class="gloss-quot">'</span><span class="gloss-text">pier</span><span class="gloss-quot">'</span>) is a 4-tonne (8,800&#160;lb) spherical module that serves as a docking hub for the Russian segment of the ISS. Launched in November 2021, Prichal provides additional docking ports for Soyuz and Progress spacecraft, as well as potential future modules. <i>Prichal</i> features six docking ports: forward, aft, port, starboard, zenith, and nadir. One of these ports, equipped with an active hybrid docking system, enabled it to dock with the Nauka module. The remaining five ports are passive hybrids, allowing for docking of Soyuz, Progress, and heavier modules, as well as future spacecraft with modified docking systems. As of 2024, the forward, aft, port and starboard docking ports remain covered. <i>Prichal</i> was initially intended to be an element of the now canceled <a href="/wiki/Orbital_Piloted_Assembly_and_Experiment_Complex" title="Orbital Piloted Assembly and Experiment Complex">Orbital Piloted Assembly and Experiment Complex</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-rsw-um&#95;162-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-rsw-um-162"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>157<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-:0&#95;163-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:0-163"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>158<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-energia-20110113&#95;164-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-energia-20110113-164"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>159<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-NSF20200819&#95;165-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-NSF20200819-165"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>160<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Unpressurised_elements">Unpressurised elements</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1273380762/mw-parser-output/.tmulti" /><div class="thumb tmulti tright"><div class="thumbinner multiimageinner" style="width:392px;max-width:392px"><div class="trow"><div class="tsingle" style="width:194px;max-width:194px"><div class="thumbimage" style="height:126px;overflow:hidden"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:Truss_breakdown.png" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/Truss_breakdown.png/250px-Truss_breakdown.png" decoding="async" width="192" height="126" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/Truss_breakdown.png/500px-Truss_breakdown.png 2x" data-file-width="2513" data-file-height="1650" /></a></span></div><div class="thumbcaption">ISS Truss Components breakdown showing Trusses and all ORUs in situ</div></div><div class="tsingle" style="width:194px;max-width:194px"><div class="thumbimage" style="height:126px;overflow:hidden"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:STS-116_spacewalk_1.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/STS-116_spacewalk_1.jpg/250px-STS-116_spacewalk_1.jpg" decoding="async" width="192" height="127" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/STS-116_spacewalk_1.jpg/500px-STS-116_spacewalk_1.jpg 2x" data-file-width="3032" data-file-height="2000" /></a></span></div><div class="thumbcaption">Construction of the <a href="/wiki/Integrated_Truss_Structure" title="Integrated Truss Structure">Integrated Truss Structure</a> over New Zealand</div></div></div></div></div>
<p>The ISS has a large number of external components that do not require pressurisation. The largest of these is the <a href="/wiki/Integrated_Truss_Structure" title="Integrated Truss Structure">Integrated Truss Structure</a> (ITS), to which the station's main <a href="/wiki/Photovoltaic_system" title="Photovoltaic system">solar arrays</a> and <a href="/wiki/External_Active_Thermal_Control_System" title="External Active Thermal Control System">thermal radiators</a> are mounted.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Arrays&#95;166-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Arrays-166"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>161<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The ITS consists of ten separate segments forming a structure 108.5 metres (356&#160;ft) long.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-OnOrbit&#95;12-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-OnOrbit-12"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>10<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The station was intended to have several smaller external components, such as six robotic arms, three <a href="/wiki/External_Stowage_Platform" class="mw-redirect" title="External Stowage Platform">External Stowage Platforms</a> (ESPs) and four <a href="/wiki/ExPRESS_Logistics_Carrier" title="ExPRESS Logistics Carrier">ExPRESS Logistics Carriers</a> (ELCs).<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Manifest&#95;167-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Manifest-167"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>162<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-nasa-factsheet-expressracks&#95;168-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-nasa-factsheet-expressracks-168"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>163<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> While these platforms allow experiments (including <a href="/wiki/Materials_International_Space_Station_Experiment" title="Materials International Space Station Experiment">MISSE</a>, the STP-H3 and the <a href="/wiki/Robotic_Refueling_Mission" title="Robotic Refueling Mission">Robotic Refueling Mission</a>) to be deployed and conducted in the vacuum of space by providing electricity and processing experimental data locally, their primary function is to store spare <a href="/wiki/Orbital_replacement_unit" title="Orbital replacement unit">Orbital Replacement Units</a> (ORUs). ORUs are parts that can be replaced when they fail or pass their design life, including pumps, storage tanks, antennas, and battery units. Such units are replaced either by astronauts during EVA or by robotic arms.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-nsf-20111223&#95;169-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-nsf-20111223-169"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>164<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Several shuttle missions were dedicated to the delivery of ORUs, including <a href="/wiki/STS-129" title="STS-129">STS-129</a>,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-EVA129&#95;170-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-EVA129-170"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>165<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> STS-133<sup id="cite&#95;ref-sts-133-press-kit&#95;171-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-sts-133-press-kit-171"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>166<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and STS-134.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-sts-134-press-kit&#95;172-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-sts-134-press-kit-172"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>167<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> As of January&#160;2011<sup class="plainlinks noexcerpt noprint asof-tag update" style="display:none;"><a class="external text" href="https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=International_Space_Station&amp;action=edit">&#91;update&#93;</a></sup>, only one other mode of transportation of ORUs had been used&#160;&#8211;&#32;the Japanese cargo vessel <a href="/wiki/H-II_Transfer_Vehicle" title="H-II Transfer Vehicle">HTV-2</a>&#160;&#8211;&#32;which delivered an FHRC and CTC-2 via its Exposed Pallet (EP).<sup id="cite&#95;ref-HTV2&#95;173-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-HTV2-173"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>168<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup class="noprint Inline-Template" style="margin-left:0.1em; white-space:nowrap;">&#91;<i><a href="/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items" title="Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers"><span title="The text near this tag is dated. (January 2013)">needs update</span></a></i>&#93;</sup>
</p><p>There are also smaller exposure facilities mounted directly to laboratory modules; the <i>Kibō</i> <a href="/wiki/Kibo_(ISS_module)" class="mw-redirect" title="Kibo (ISS module)">Exposed Facility</a> serves as an external "<a href="/wiki/Porch" title="Porch">porch</a>" for the <i>Kibō</i> complex,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-174" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-174"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>169<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and a facility on the European <i>Columbus</i> laboratory provides power and data connections for experiments such as the <a href="/wiki/European_Technology_Exposure_Facility" title="European Technology Exposure Facility">European Technology Exposure Facility</a><sup id="cite&#95;ref-NASA&#95;175-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-NASA-175"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>170<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-ESA&#95;176-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-ESA-176"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>171<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and the <a href="/wiki/Atomic_Clock_Ensemble_in_Space" title="Atomic Clock Ensemble in Space">Atomic Clock Ensemble in Space</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-177" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-177"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>172<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> A <a href="/wiki/Remote_sensing" title="Remote sensing">remote sensing</a> instrument, <a href="/wiki/SAGE_III_on_ISS" title="SAGE III on ISS">SAGE III-ISS</a>, was delivered to the station in February 2017 aboard <a href="/wiki/SpaceX_CRS-10" title="SpaceX CRS-10">CRS-10</a>,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-nsf20170310&#95;178-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-nsf20170310-178"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>173<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and the <a href="/wiki/Neutron_Star_Interior_Composition_Explorer" title="Neutron Star Interior Composition Explorer">NICER</a> experiment was delivered aboard <a href="/wiki/SpaceX_CRS-11" title="SpaceX CRS-11">CRS-11</a> in June 2017.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-nsf20170603&#95;179-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-nsf20170603-179"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>174<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The largest scientific payload externally mounted to the ISS is the <a href="/wiki/Alpha_Magnetic_Spectrometer" title="Alpha Magnetic Spectrometer">Alpha Magnetic Spectrometer</a> (AMS), a particle physics experiment launched on STS-134 in May 2011, and mounted externally on the ITS. The AMS measures cosmic rays to look for evidence of dark matter and antimatter.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-ams-cern&#95;180-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-ams-cern-180"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>175<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-nsf20130404&#95;181-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-nsf20130404-181"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>176<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The commercial <i><a href="/wiki/Columbus_%E2%80%93_External_Payload_Facility#Bartolomeo" class="mw-redirect" title="Columbus – External Payload Facility">Bartolomeo</a></i> External Payload Hosting Platform, manufactured by Airbus, was launched on 6 March 2020 aboard <a href="/wiki/SpaceX_CRS-20" title="SpaceX CRS-20">CRS-20</a> and attached to the European <i>Columbus</i> module. It will provide an additional 12 external payload slots, supplementing the eight on the <a href="/wiki/ExPRESS_Logistics_Carrier" title="ExPRESS Logistics Carrier">ExPRESS Logistics Carriers</a>, ten on <i>Kibō</i>, and four on <i>Columbus</i>. The system is designed to be robotically serviced and will require no astronaut intervention. It is named after Christopher Columbus's younger brother.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-spacedaily-20180209&#95;182-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-spacedaily-20180209-182"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>177<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-aerospacetech-20180208&#95;183-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-aerospacetech-20180208-183"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>178<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-eoportal-iss-bartolomeo&#95;184-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-eoportal-iss-bartolomeo-184"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>179<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
