<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="MLM_outfittings">MLM outfittings</h4></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1273380762/mw-parser-output/.tmulti" /><div class="thumb tmulti tright"><div class="thumbinner multiimageinner" style="width:392px;max-width:392px"><div class="trow"><div class="tsingle" style="width:219px;max-width:219px"><div class="thumbimage" style="height:111px;overflow:hidden"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:MRM1_Rassvet.png" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/MRM1_Rassvet.png/250px-MRM1_Rassvet.png" decoding="async" width="217" height="111" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/MRM1_Rassvet.png/500px-MRM1_Rassvet.png 2x" data-file-width="1022" data-file-height="525" /></a></span></div><div class="thumbcaption">MLM outfittings on Rassvet</div></div><div class="tsingle" style="width:169px;max-width:169px"><div class="thumbimage" style="height:111px;overflow:hidden"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:ISS-65_Nauka_and_Soyuz_MS-18_docked_to_the_International_Space_Station_(1).jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/ISS-65_Nauka_and_Soyuz_MS-18_docked_to_the_International_Space_Station_%281%29.jpg/250px-ISS-65_Nauka_and_Soyuz_MS-18_docked_to_the_International_Space_Station_%281%29.jpg" decoding="async" width="167" height="111" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/ISS-65_Nauka_and_Soyuz_MS-18_docked_to_the_International_Space_Station_%281%29.jpg/500px-ISS-65_Nauka_and_Soyuz_MS-18_docked_to_the_International_Space_Station_%281%29.jpg 2x" data-file-width="5568" data-file-height="3712" /></a></span></div><div class="thumbcaption">A wide-angle view of the new module (behind <i>Rassvet</i>) attached to the <a href="/wiki/Russian_Orbital_Segment" title="Russian Orbital Segment">ROS</a> as seen from the cupola</div></div></div></div></div>
<p>In May 2010, equipment for <i>Nauka</i> was launched on STS-132 (as part of an agreement with NASA) and delivered by Space Shuttle <i>Atlantis</i>. Weighing 1.4 metric tons, the equipment was attached to the outside of <i>Rassvet</i> (MRM-1). It included a spare elbow joint for the <a href="/wiki/European_Robotic_Arm" title="European Robotic Arm">European Robotic Arm</a> (ERA) (which was launched with <i>Nauka</i>) and an ERA-portable workpost used during EVAs, as well as RTOd add-on heat radiator and internal hardware alongside the pressurized experiment airlock.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Roscosmos1&#95;185-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Roscosmos1-185"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>180<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The RTOd radiator adds additional cooling capability to <i>Nauka</i>, which enables the module to host more scientific experiments.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Roscosmos1&#95;185-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Roscosmos1-185"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>180<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The ERA was used to remove the RTOd radiator from <i>Rassvet</i> and transferred over to <i>Nauka</i> during VKD-56 spacewalk. Later it was activated and fully deployed on VKD-58 spacewalk.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-garcia-20230512&#95;186-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-garcia-20230512-186"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>181<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> This process took several months. A portable work platform was also transferred over in August 2023 during VKD-60 spacewalk, which can attach to the end of the ERA to allow cosmonauts to "ride" on the end of the arm during spacewalks.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-esa-arm-brochure&#95;187-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-esa-arm-brochure-187"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>182<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-sfn-20230809&#95;188-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-sfn-20230809-188"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>183<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> However, even after several months of outfitting EVAs and RTOd heat radiator installation, six months later, the RTOd radiator malfunctioned before active use of Nauka (the purpose of RTOd installation is to radiate heat from Nauka experiments). The malfunction, a leak, rendered the RTOd radiator unusable for Nauka. This is the third ISS radiator leak after <a href="/wiki/Soyuz_MS-22" title="Soyuz MS-22">Soyuz MS-22</a> and <a href="/wiki/Progress_MS-21" title="Progress MS-21">Progress MS-21</a> radiator leaks. If a spare RTOd is not available, Nauka experiments will have to rely on Nauka's main launch radiator and the module could never be used to its full capacity.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-sfn-20231009&#95;189-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-sfn-20231009-189"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>184<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-190" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-190"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>185<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Another MLM outfitting is a 4 segment external payload interface called means of attachment of large payloads (Sredstva Krepleniya Krupnogabaritnykh Obyektov, SKKO).<sup id="cite&#95;ref-191" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-191"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>186<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Delivered in two parts to Nauka by <a href="/wiki/Progress_MS-18" title="Progress MS-18">Progress MS-18</a> (LCCS part) and <a href="/wiki/Progress_MS-21" title="Progress MS-21">Progress MS-21</a> (SCCCS part) as part of the module activation outfitting process.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-192" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-192"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>187<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-193" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-193"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>188<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-194" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-194"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>189<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-russianspaceweb-fgb2-mlm&#95;195-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-russianspaceweb-fgb2-mlm-195"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>190<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> It was taken outside and installed on the ERA aft facing base point on Nauka during the VKD-55 spacewalk.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-garcia-20221116&#95;196-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-garcia-20221116-196"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>191<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-lavelle-20221117&#95;197-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-lavelle-20221117-197"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>192<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-garcia-20221117&#95;198-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-garcia-20221117-198"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>193<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-spacecom-20221117&#95;199-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-spacecom-20221117-199"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>194<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Robotic_arms_and_cargo_cranes">Robotic arms and cargo cranes</h4></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1273380762/mw-parser-output/.tmulti" /><div class="thumb tmulti tright"><div class="thumbinner multiimageinner" style="width:392px;max-width:392px"><div class="trow"><div class="tsingle" style="width:194px;max-width:194px"><div class="thumbimage" style="height:127px;overflow:hidden"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:Iss017e011097.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/66/Iss017e011097.jpg/250px-Iss017e011097.jpg" decoding="async" width="192" height="127" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/66/Iss017e011097.jpg/500px-Iss017e011097.jpg 2x" data-file-width="3032" data-file-height="2012" /></a></span></div><div class="thumbcaption">Commander <a href="/wiki/Sergey_Volkov_(cosmonaut)" title="Sergey Volkov (cosmonaut)">Volkov</a> stands on <i>Pirs</i> with his back to the <a href="/wiki/Soyuz_spacecraft" class="mw-redirect" title="Soyuz spacecraft">Soyuz</a> whilst operating the manual<br /><a href="/wiki/Strela_(crane)" title="Strela (crane)">Strela crane</a> (which is holding photographer <a href="/wiki/Oleg_Kononenko" title="Oleg Kononenko">Oleg Kononenko</a>).</div></div><div class="tsingle" style="width:194px;max-width:194px"><div class="thumbimage" style="height:127px;overflow:hidden"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:Dextrereallyhasnohead.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/Dextrereallyhasnohead.jpg/250px-Dextrereallyhasnohead.jpg" decoding="async" width="192" height="127" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/Dextrereallyhasnohead.jpg/500px-Dextrereallyhasnohead.jpg 2x" data-file-width="3032" data-file-height="2009" /></a></span></div><div class="thumbcaption"><a href="/wiki/Dextre" title="Dextre">Dextre</a>, like many of the station's experiments and robotic arms, can be operated from Earth, allowing tasks to be performed while the crew sleeps.</div></div></div></div></div>
<p>The Integrated Truss Structure (ITS) serves as a base for the station's primary remote manipulator system, the <a href="/wiki/Mobile_Servicing_System" title="Mobile Servicing System">Mobile Servicing System</a> (MSS), which is composed of three main components:
</p>
<ul><li><a href="/wiki/Canadarm2" class="mw-redirect" title="Canadarm2">Canadarm2</a>, the largest robotic arm on the ISS, has a mass of 1,800 kilograms (4,000&#160;lb) and is used to: dock and manipulate spacecraft and modules on the USOS; hold crew members and equipment in place during EVAs; and move Dextre to perform tasks.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-200" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-200"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>195<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></li>
<li><a href="/wiki/Dextre" title="Dextre">Dextre</a> is a 1,560&#160;kg (3,440&#160;lb) robotic manipulator that has two arms and a rotating torso, with power tools, lights, and video for replacing <a href="/wiki/Orbital_replacement_unit" title="Orbital replacement unit">orbital replacement units</a> (ORUs) and performing other tasks requiring fine control.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-csa-dextre&#95;201-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-csa-dextre-201"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>196<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></li>
<li>The <a href="/wiki/Mobile_Base_System" class="mw-redirect" title="Mobile Base System">Mobile Base System</a> (MBS) is a platform that rides on rails along the length of the station's main truss, which serves as a mobile base for Canadarm2 and Dextre, allowing the robotic arms to reach all parts of the USOS.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-csa-mbs&#95;202-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-csa-mbs-202"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>197<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></li></ul>
<p>A <a href="/wiki/Grapple_Fixture" class="mw-redirect" title="Grapple Fixture">grapple fixture</a> was added to <i>Zarya</i> on STS-134 to enable Canadarm2 to inchworm itself onto the ROS.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-sts-134-press-kit&#95;172-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-sts-134-press-kit-172"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>167<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Also installed during STS-134 was the 15&#160;m (50&#160;ft) <a href="/wiki/Orbiter_Boom_Sensor_System" title="Orbiter Boom Sensor System">Orbiter Boom Sensor System</a> (OBSS), which had been used to inspect heat shield tiles on Space Shuttle missions and which can be used on the station to increase the reach of the MSS.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-sts-134-press-kit&#95;172-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-sts-134-press-kit-172"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>167<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Staff on Earth or the ISS can operate the MSS components using remote control, performing work outside the station without the need for space walks.
</p><p>Japan's <a href="/wiki/Japanese_Remote_Manipulator_System" class="mw-redirect" title="Japanese Remote Manipulator System">Remote Manipulator System</a>, which services the <i>Kibō</i> Exposed Facility,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-203" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-203"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>198<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> was launched on <a href="/wiki/STS-124" title="STS-124">STS-124</a> and is attached to the <i>Kibō</i> Pressurised Module.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-204" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-204"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>199<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The arm is similar to the Space Shuttle arm as it is permanently attached at one end and has a latching end effector for standard grapple fixtures at the other.
</p><p>The <a href="/wiki/European_Robotic_Arm" title="European Robotic Arm">European Robotic Arm</a>, which will service the ROS, was launched alongside the <a href="/wiki/Nauka_(ISS_module)" title="Nauka (ISS module)"><i>Nauka</i></a> module.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-tass-20200402&#95;205-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-tass-20200402-205"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>200<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The ROS does not require spacecraft or modules to be manipulated, as all spacecraft and modules dock automatically and may be discarded the same way. Crew use the two <i><a href="/wiki/Strela_(crane)" title="Strela (crane)">Strela</a></i> (<a href="/wiki/Russian_language" title="Russian language">Russian</a>: <span lang="ru">Стрела́</span>, <small><a href="/wiki/Literal_translation" title="Literal translation">lit.</a>&#8201;</small><span class="gloss-quot">'</span><span class="gloss-text">Arrow</span><span class="gloss-quot">'</span>) cargo cranes during EVAs for moving crew and equipment around the ROS. Each Strela crane has a mass of 45&#160;kg (99&#160;lb).
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Former_module">Former module</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1273380762/mw-parser-output/.tmulti" /><div class="thumb tmulti tright"><div class="thumbinner multiimageinner" style="width:392px;max-width:392px"><div class="trow"><div class="tsingle" style="width:177px;max-width:177px"><div class="thumbimage" style="height:116px;overflow:hidden"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:Sts110-363-001.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/Sts110-363-001.jpg/250px-Sts110-363-001.jpg" decoding="async" width="175" height="116" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/Sts110-363-001.jpg/500px-Sts110-363-001.jpg 2x" data-file-width="2764" data-file-height="1837" /></a></span></div><div class="thumbcaption">The <i>Pirs</i> module attached to the ISS</div></div><div class="tsingle" style="width:211px;max-width:211px"><div class="thumbimage" style="height:116px;overflow:hidden"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:ISS-65_Pirs_docking_compartment_separates_from_the_Space_Station.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/ISS-65_Pirs_docking_compartment_separates_from_the_Space_Station.jpg/250px-ISS-65_Pirs_docking_compartment_separates_from_the_Space_Station.jpg" decoding="async" width="209" height="117" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/ISS-65_Pirs_docking_compartment_separates_from_the_Space_Station.jpg/500px-ISS-65_Pirs_docking_compartment_separates_from_the_Space_Station.jpg 2x" data-file-width="1279" data-file-height="715" /></a></span></div><div class="thumbcaption">ISS-65 Pirs docking compartment separates from the International Space Station.</div></div></div></div></div>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Pirs"><i>Pirs</i></h4></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Pirs_(ISS_module)" title="Pirs (ISS module)">Pirs (ISS module)</a></div>
<p>Pirs (Russian: Пирс, lit. 'Pier') was launched on 14&#160;September 2001, as ISS Assembly Mission 4R, on a Russian Soyuz-U rocket, using a modified <a href="/wiki/Progress_spacecraft" class="mw-redirect" title="Progress spacecraft">Progress spacecraft</a>, <a href="/wiki/Progress_M-SO1" class="mw-redirect" title="Progress M-SO1">Progress M-SO1</a>, as an upper stage. Pirs was undocked by <a href="/wiki/Progress_MS-16" title="Progress MS-16">Progress MS-16</a> on 26&#160;July 2021, 10:56 UTC, and deorbited on the same day at 14:51 UTC to make room for the <a href="/wiki/Nauka_(ISS_module)" title="Nauka (ISS module)"><i>Nauka</i></a> module to be attached to the space station. Prior to its departure, Pirs served as the primary Russian airlock on the station, being used to store and refurbish the Russian Orlan spacesuits.
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Planned_components">Planned components</h3></div>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Axiom_segment">Axiom segment</h4></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Axiom_Station" title="Axiom Station">Axiom Station</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Axiom_modules_connected_to_ISS.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/15/Axiom_modules_connected_to_ISS.jpg/250px-Axiom_modules_connected_to_ISS.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="141" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/15/Axiom_modules_connected_to_ISS.jpg/500px-Axiom_modules_connected_to_ISS.jpg 2x" data-file-width="1089" data-file-height="614" /></a><figcaption>Early rendering of the <a href="/wiki/Axiom_Orbital_Segment" class="mw-redirect" title="Axiom Orbital Segment">Axiom Orbital Segment</a>, made prior to assembly plan changes, with now only one module, the <a href="/wiki/Axiom_Station#PPTM" title="Axiom Station">Payload Power Thermal Module (PPTM)</a>, being planned to dock with the ISS</figcaption></figure>
<p>In January 2020, NASA awarded <a href="/wiki/Axiom_Space" title="Axiom Space">Axiom Space</a> a contract to build a commercial module for the ISS. The contract is under the <a href="/wiki/Next_Space_Technologies_for_Exploration_Partnerships" title="Next Space Technologies for Exploration Partnerships">NextSTEP2</a> program. NASA negotiated with Axiom on a firm <a href="/wiki/Fixed-price_contract" title="Fixed-price contract">fixed-price contract</a> basis to build and deliver the module, which will attach to the forward port of the space station's <i>Harmony (Node 2)</i> module. Although NASA only commissioned one module, Axiom planned to build an entire segment (<a href="/wiki/Axiom_Orbital_Segment" class="mw-redirect" title="Axiom Orbital Segment">Axiom Orbital Segment</a>) consisting of five modules, including a node module, an orbital research and manufacturing facility, a crew habitat, and a "large-windowed Earth observatory". The Axiom segment was expected to greatly increase the capabilities and value of the space station, allowing for larger crews and private spaceflight by other organisations. Axiom planned to convert the segment into a stand-alone space station (<a href="/wiki/Axiom_Station" title="Axiom Station">Axiom Station</a>) once the ISS is decommissioned, with the intention that this would act as a successor to the ISS.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-sfn-20200128&#95;206-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-sfn-20200128-206"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>201<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-techcrunch-20200127&#95;207-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-techcrunch-20200127-207"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>202<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-geekwire-20200127&#95;208-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-geekwire-20200127-208"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>203<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Canadarm2" class="mw-redirect" title="Canadarm2">Canadarm2</a> is planned to continue its operations on Axiom Station after the retirement of ISS in 2030.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-axiom-assembly&#95;209-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-axiom-assembly-209"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>204<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In December 2024, Axiom Space revised their station assembly plans to require only one module to dock with the ISS before assembling Axiom Station in an independent orbit.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-sn-20241218&#95;210-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-sn-20241218-210"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>205<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The module docked to the ISS will feature storage for established ISS hardware to be transfered to and retained at the Axiom module and later station.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-v614&#95;211-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-v614-211"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>206<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>As of December 2024<sup class="plainlinks noexcerpt noprint asof-tag update" style="display:none;"><a class="external text" href="https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=International_Space_Station&amp;action=edit">&#91;update&#93;</a></sup>, Axiom Space expects to launch one module, the <a href="/wiki/Axiom_Station#PPTM" title="Axiom Station">Payload Power Thermal Module (PPTM)</a>, to the ISS no earlier than 2027.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-sn-20241218&#95;210-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-sn-20241218-210"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>205<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> PPTM is expected to remain at the ISS until the launch of Axiom's Habitat One (Hab-1) module about one year later, after which it will detach from the ISS to join with Hab-1.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-sn-20241218&#95;210-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-sn-20241218-210"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>205<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="US_Deorbit_Vehicle">US Deorbit Vehicle</h4></div>
<p>The <a href="/wiki/US_Deorbit_Vehicle" class="mw-redirect" title="US Deorbit Vehicle">US Deorbit Vehicle</a> (USDV) is a NASA-provided spacecraft intended to perform a controlled de-orbit and demise of the station after the end of its operational life in 2030. In June 2024, NASA awarded <a href="/wiki/SpaceX" title="SpaceX">SpaceX</a> a contract to build the Deorbit Vehicle.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-:2&#95;212-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:2-212"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>207<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> NASA plans to de-orbit ISS as soon as they have the "minimum capability" in orbit: "the USDV and at least one commercial station."<sup id="cite&#95;ref-213" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-213"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>208<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Cancelled_components">Cancelled components</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1273380762/mw-parser-output/.tmulti" /><div class="thumb tmulti tright"><div class="thumbinner multiimageinner" style="width:392px;max-width:392px"><div class="trow"><div class="tsingle" style="width:162px;max-width:162px"><div class="thumbimage" style="height:126px;overflow:hidden"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:ISS_Habitation_module.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/ISS_Habitation_module.jpg/250px-ISS_Habitation_module.jpg" decoding="async" width="160" height="126" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/ISS_Habitation_module.jpg/330px-ISS_Habitation_module.jpg 2x" data-file-width="1536" data-file-height="1212" /></a></span></div><div class="thumbcaption">The cancelled Habitation module under construction at Michoud in 1997</div></div><div class="tsingle" style="width:226px;max-width:226px"><div class="thumbimage" style="height:126px;overflow:hidden"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:Nautilus-X_demonstrator_docked_to_the_ISS_(side).webp" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/Nautilus-X_demonstrator_docked_to_the_ISS_%28side%29.webp/250px-Nautilus-X_demonstrator_docked_to_the_ISS_%28side%29.webp.png" decoding="async" width="224" height="126" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/Nautilus-X_demonstrator_docked_to_the_ISS_%28side%29.webp/500px-Nautilus-X_demonstrator_docked_to_the_ISS_%28side%29.webp.png 2x" data-file-width="995" data-file-height="559" /></a></span></div><div class="thumbcaption">Rendering of the Nautilus-X Centrifuge Demonstrator docked to the ISS (side)</div></div></div></div></div>
<p>Several modules developed or planned for the station were cancelled over the course of the ISS program. Reasons include budgetary constraints, the modules becoming unnecessary, and station redesigns after the 2003 <a href="/wiki/Space_Shuttle_Columbia_disaster" title="Space Shuttle Columbia disaster"><i>Columbia</i> disaster</a>. The US <a href="/wiki/Centrifuge_Accommodations_Module" title="Centrifuge Accommodations Module">Centrifuge Accommodations Module</a> would have hosted science experiments in varying levels of <a href="/wiki/Artificial_gravity" title="Artificial gravity">artificial gravity</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-214" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-214"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>209<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The US <a href="/wiki/Habitation_Module" title="Habitation Module">Habitation Module</a> would have served as the station's living quarters. Instead, the living quarters are now spread throughout the station.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-spacecom-20060214&#95;215-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-spacecom-20060214-215"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>210<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The US <a href="/wiki/Interim_Control_Module" title="Interim Control Module">Interim Control Module</a> and <a href="/wiki/ISS_Propulsion_Module" title="ISS Propulsion Module">ISS Propulsion Module</a> would have replaced the functions of <i>Zvezda</i> in case of a launch failure.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-navy-code8200-icm&#95;216-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-navy-code8200-icm-216"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>211<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Two <a href="/wiki/Russian_Research_Module" title="Russian Research Module">Russian Research Modules</a> were planned for scientific research.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-boeing-russian-modules&#95;217-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-boeing-russian-modules-217"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>212<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> They would have docked to a Russian <a href="/wiki/Universal_Docking_Module" title="Universal Docking Module">Universal Docking Module</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-russianspaceweb-iss-segment&#95;218-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-russianspaceweb-iss-segment-218"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>213<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The Russian <a href="/wiki/Science_Power_Platform" title="Science Power Platform">Science Power Platform</a> would have supplied power to the Russian Orbital Segment independent of the ITS solar arrays.
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Science_Power_Modules_1_and_2_(Repurposed_Components)"><span id="Science_Power_Modules_1_and_2_.28Repurposed_Components.29"></span>Science Power Modules 1 and 2 (Repurposed Components)</h4></div>
<p><b>Science Power Module 1</b> (<b>SPM-1</b>, also known as <b>NEM-1</b>) and <b>Science Power Module 2</b> (<b>SPM-2</b>, also known as <b>NEM-2</b>) are modules that were originally planned to arrive at the ISS no earlier than 2024, and dock to the <i>Prichal</i> module, which is docked to the <i>Nauka</i> module.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-NSF20200819&#95;165-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-NSF20200819-165"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>160<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-rsw-2024&#95;219-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-rsw-2024-219"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>214<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In April 2021, Roscosmos announced that NEM-1 would be repurposed to function as a core module of the proposed <a href="/wiki/Russian_Orbital_Service_Station" title="Russian Orbital Service Station">Russian Orbital Service Station</a> (ROS), launching no earlier than 2027<sup id="cite&#95;ref-tass-20230124&#95;220-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-tass-20230124-220"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>215<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and docking to the free-flying <i>Nauka</i> module.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-tass-20210719&#95;221-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-tass-20210719-221"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>216<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-russianspaceweb-ros&#95;222-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-russianspaceweb-ros-222"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>217<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> NEM-2 may be converted into another core "base" module, which would be launched in 2028.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-roscosmos-20210424&#95;223-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-roscosmos-20210424-223"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>218<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> As of December 2025<sup class="plainlinks noexcerpt noprint asof-tag update" style="display:none;"><a class="external text" href="https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=International_Space_Station&amp;action=edit">&#91;update&#93;</a></sup>, NEM-1—now referred to simply as <b>NEM</b>—is expected to be launched to the ISS in 2029, where it will dock with the Universal Node module replacing <i>Prichal</i> prior to the separation of the ROS modules around 2030.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-224" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-224"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>219<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="XBASE">XBASE</h4></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/B330" title="B330">B330</a></div>
<p>In August 2016, <a href="/wiki/Bigelow_Aerospace" title="Bigelow Aerospace">Bigelow Aerospace</a> negotiated an agreement with NASA to develop a full-size ground prototype Deep Space Habitation based on the B330 under the second phase of Next Space Technologies for Exploration Partnerships. The module was called the Expandable Bigelow Advanced Station Enhancement (XBASE), as Bigelow hoped to test the module by attaching it to the International Space Station. However, in March 2020, Bigelow laid off all 88 of its employees, and as of February&#160;2024<sup class="plainlinks noexcerpt noprint asof-tag update" style="display:none;"><a class="external text" href="https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=International_Space_Station&amp;action=edit">&#91;update&#93;</a></sup> the company remains dormant and is considered defunct,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-225" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-225"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>220<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-226" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-226"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>221<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> making it appear unlikely that the XBASE module will ever be launched.
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Nautilus-X_Centrifuge_Demonstration">Nautilus-X Centrifuge Demonstration</h4></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Nautilus-X" title="Nautilus-X">Nautilus-X</a></div>
<p>A proposal was put forward in 2011 for a first in-space demonstration of a sufficiently scaled centrifuge for artificial partial-g gravity effects. It was designed to become a sleep module for the ISS crew. The project was cancelled in favour of other projects due to budget constraints.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-m966&#95;227-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-m966-227"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>222<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Onboard_systems">Onboard systems</h2></div>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Life_support">Life support</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main articles: <a href="/wiki/ISS_ECLSS" title="ISS ECLSS">ISS ECLSS</a> and <a href="/wiki/Chemical_oxygen_generator" title="Chemical oxygen generator">Chemical oxygen generator</a></div>
<p>The critical systems are the atmosphere control system, the water supply system, the food supply facilities, the sanitation and hygiene equipment, and fire detection and suppression equipment. The Russian Orbital Segment's life-support systems are contained in the <i>Zvezda</i> service module. Some of these systems are supplemented by equipment in the USOS. The <i>Nauka</i> laboratory has a complete set of life-support systems.
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Atmospheric_control_systems">Atmospheric control systems</h4></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:SpaceStationCycle.svg" class="mw-file-description"><img alt="A flowchart diagram showing the components of the ISS life-support system." src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4d/SpaceStationCycle.svg/500px-SpaceStationCycle.svg.png" decoding="async" width="500" height="295" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4d/SpaceStationCycle.svg/1280px-SpaceStationCycle.svg.png 2x" data-file-width="512" data-file-height="302" /></a><figcaption>The interactions between the components of the ISS Environmental Control and Life-Support System (ECLSS)</figcaption></figure>
<p>The atmosphere on board the ISS is similar to <a href="/wiki/Atmosphere_of_Earth" title="Atmosphere of Earth">that of Earth</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-228" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-228"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>223<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Normal air pressure on the ISS is 101.3&#160;kPa (14.69&#160;psi), the same as at sea level on Earth.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-pressure&#95;229-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-pressure-229"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>224<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> While the crew would remain healthy at a lower pressure, some equipment is very pressure-sensitive.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-pressure&#95;229-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-pressure-229"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>224<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Earth-like atmospheric conditions have been maintained on all Russian and Soviet spacecraft,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-spacemed&#95;230-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-spacemed-230"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>225<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> while American spacecraft used pure oxygen atmospheres at 5 psi (0.3 atm) after launch.
</p><p>The <i><a href="/wiki/Elektron_(ISS)" class="mw-redirect" title="Elektron (ISS)">Elektron</a></i> system aboard <i>Zvezda</i> and a similar system in <i>Destiny</i> generate oxygen aboard the station.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-OGS&#95;231-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-OGS-231"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>226<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The crew has a backup option in the form of bottled oxygen and <a href="/wiki/Vika_oxygen_generator" title="Vika oxygen generator">Solid Fuel Oxygen Generation</a> (SFOG) canisters, a <a href="/wiki/Chemical_oxygen_generator" title="Chemical oxygen generator">chemical oxygen generator</a> system.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-breath&#95;easy&#95;232-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-breath_easy-232"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>227<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Carbon dioxide is removed from the air by the <a href="/wiki/ISS_ECLSS#Vozdukh" title="ISS ECLSS">Vozdukh</a> system in <i>Zvezda</i>. Other by-products of human metabolism, such as methane from the intestines and ammonia from sweat, are removed by <a href="/wiki/Activated_carbon" title="Activated carbon">activated charcoal</a> filters.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-breath&#95;easy&#95;232-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-breath_easy-232"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>227<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Part of the ROS atmosphere control system is the oxygen supply. Triple-redundancy is provided by the Elektron unit, solid fuel generators, and stored oxygen. The primary supply of oxygen is the Elektron unit which produces <style data-mw-deduplicate="TemplateStyles:r1123817410">.mw-parser-output .template-chem2-su{display:inline-block;font-size:80%;line-height:1;vertical-align:-0.35em}.mw-parser-output .template-chem2-su>span{display:block;text-align:left}.mw-parser-output sub.template-chem2-sub{font-size:80%;vertical-align:-0.35em}.mw-parser-output sup.template-chem2-sup{font-size:80%;vertical-align:0.65em}</style><span class="chemf nowrap">O<sub class="template-chem2-sub">2</sub></span> and <link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1123817410" /><span class="chemf nowrap">H<sub class="template-chem2-sub">2</sub></span> by <a href="/wiki/Electrolysis" title="Electrolysis">electrolysis</a> of water and vents <link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1123817410" /><span class="chemf nowrap">H<sub class="template-chem2-sub">2</sub></span> overboard. The 1&#160;kW (1.3&#160;hp) system uses approximately one litre of water per crew member per day. This water is either brought from Earth or recycled from other systems. <i>Mir</i> was the first spacecraft to use recycled water for oxygen production. The secondary oxygen supply is provided by burning oxygen-producing <a href="/wiki/Vika_oxygen_generator" title="Vika oxygen generator">Vika</a> cartridges (see also <a href="/wiki/ISS_ECLSS" title="ISS ECLSS">ISS ECLSS</a>). Each 'candle' takes 5–20 minutes to decompose at 450–500&#160;°C (842–932&#160;°F), producing 600 litres (130&#160;imp&#160;gal; 160&#160;US&#160;gal) of <link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1123817410" /><span class="chemf nowrap">O<sub class="template-chem2-sub">2</sub></span>. This unit is manually operated.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-233" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-233"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>228<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The US Orbital Segment (USOS) has redundant supplies of oxygen, from a pressurised storage tank on the <i>Quest</i> airlock module delivered in 2001, supplemented ten years later by ESA-built Advanced Closed-Loop System (ACLS) in the <i>Tranquility</i> module (Node 3), which produces <link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1123817410" /><span class="chemf nowrap">O<sub class="template-chem2-sub">2</sub></span> by electrolysis.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-nasa-20001113&#95;234-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-nasa-20001113-234"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>229<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Hydrogen produced is combined with carbon dioxide from the cabin atmosphere and converted to water and methane.
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Power_and_thermal_control">Power and thermal control</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main articles: <a href="/wiki/Electrical_system_of_the_International_Space_Station" title="Electrical system of the International Space Station">Electrical system of the International Space Station</a> and <a href="/wiki/External_Active_Thermal_Control_System" title="External Active Thermal Control System">External Active Thermal Control System</a></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1273380762/mw-parser-output/.tmulti" /><div class="thumb tmulti tright"><div class="thumbinner multiimageinner" style="width:392px;max-width:392px"><div class="trow"><div class="tsingle" style="width:121px;max-width:121px"><div class="thumbimage" style="height:79px;overflow:hidden"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:ROSSA.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/ROSSA.jpg/120px-ROSSA.jpg" decoding="async" width="119" height="79" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/ROSSA.jpg/250px-ROSSA.jpg 2x" data-file-width="3060" data-file-height="2032" /></a></span></div><div class="thumbcaption">Russian solar arrays, backlit by sunset</div></div><div class="tsingle" style="width:122px;max-width:122px"><div class="thumbimage" style="height:79px;overflow:hidden"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:P4_deployed.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/P4_deployed.jpg/120px-P4_deployed.jpg" decoding="async" width="120" height="79" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/P4_deployed.jpg/250px-P4_deployed.jpg 2x" data-file-width="3032" data-file-height="2000" /></a></span></div><div class="thumbcaption">One of the eight truss mounted pairs of USOS solar arrays</div></div><div class="tsingle" style="width:143px;max-width:143px"><div class="thumbimage" style="height:79px;overflow:hidden"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:ISS_new_iROSA_deployed.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9c/ISS_new_iROSA_deployed.jpg/250px-ISS_new_iROSA_deployed.jpg" decoding="async" width="141" height="79" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9c/ISS_new_iROSA_deployed.jpg/330px-ISS_new_iROSA_deployed.jpg 2x" data-file-width="4928" data-file-height="2768" /></a></span></div><div class="thumbcaption">ISS new roll out solar array as seen from a zoom camera on the P6 Truss</div></div></div></div></div>
<p>Double-sided <a href="/wiki/Solar_panel" title="Solar panel">solar arrays</a> provide <a href="/wiki/Electric_power" title="Electric power">electrical power</a> to the ISS. These bifacial cells collect direct sunlight on one side and light <a href="/wiki/Albedo" title="Albedo">reflected off</a> from the Earth on the other, and are more efficient and operate at a lower temperature than single-sided cells commonly used on Earth.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-andreas-2005&#95;235-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-andreas-2005-235"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>230<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> 
</p><p>The Russian segment of the station, like most spacecraft, uses 28&#160;<a href="/wiki/Volt" title="Volt">V</a>&#160;<a href="/wiki/Extra-low_voltage" title="Extra-low voltage">low voltage</a> <a href="/wiki/Direct_current" title="Direct current">DC</a> from two rotating solar arrays mounted on <i>Zvezda</i>. The USOS uses 130–180&#160;V&#160;DC from the USOS&#160;PV array. Power is stabilised and distributed at 160&#160;V&#160;DC and converted to the user-required 124&#160;V&#160;DC. The <a href="/wiki/High_voltage" title="High voltage">higher distribution voltage</a> allows smaller, lighter conductors, at the expense of crew safety. The two station segments share power with converters.
</p><p>The USOS solar arrays are arranged as four wing pairs, for a total production of 75 to 90 kilowatts.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-ISS&#95;stats&#95;7-4" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-ISS_stats-7"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>5<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> These arrays normally track the Sun to maximise power generation. Each array is about 375&#160;m<sup>2</sup> (4,036&#160;sq&#160;ft) in area and 58&#160;m (190&#160;ft) long. In the complete configuration, the solar arrays track the Sun by rotating the <i>alpha <a href="/wiki/Gimbal" title="Gimbal">gimbal</a></i> once per orbit; the <i>beta gimbal</i> follows slower changes in the angle of the Sun to the orbital plane. The <a href="/wiki/Night_Glider_mode" title="Night Glider mode">Night Glider mode</a> aligns the solar arrays parallel to the ground at night to reduce the significant aerodynamic drag at the station's relatively low orbital altitude.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Glider&#95;236-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Glider-236"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>231<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The station originally used rechargeable <a href="/wiki/Nickel%E2%80%93hydrogen_battery" title="Nickel–hydrogen battery">nickel–hydrogen batteries</a> (<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1123817410" /><span class="chemf nowrap">NiH<sub class="template-chem2-sub">2</sub></span>) for continuous power during the 45 minutes of every 90-minute orbit that it is eclipsed by the Earth. The batteries are recharged on the day side of the orbit. They had a 6.5-year lifetime (over 37,000 charge/discharge cycles) and were regularly replaced over the anticipated 20-year life of the station.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-237" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-237"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>232<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Starting in 2016, the nickel–hydrogen batteries were replaced by <a href="/wiki/Lithium-ion_battery" title="Lithium-ion battery">lithium-ion batteries</a>, which are expected to last until the end of the ISS program.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-sfn-20161213&#95;238-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-sfn-20161213-238"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>233<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The station's large solar panels generate a high potential voltage difference between the station and the ionosphere. This could cause arcing through insulating surfaces and sputtering of conductive surfaces as ions are accelerated by the spacecraft plasma sheath. To mitigate this, <a href="/wiki/Plasma_contactor" title="Plasma contactor">plasma contactor</a> units create current paths between the station and the ambient space plasma.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-239" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-239"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>234<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:EATCS.png" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/e/e6/EATCS.png" decoding="async" width="550" height="345" class="mw-file-element" data-file-width="786" data-file-height="493" /></a><figcaption>ISS External Active Thermal Control System (EATCS) diagram</figcaption></figure>
<p>The station's systems and experiments consume a large amount of electrical power, almost all of which is converted to heat. To keep the internal temperature within workable limits, a passive thermal control system (PTCS) is made of external surface materials, insulation such as MLI, and heat pipes. If the PTCS cannot keep up with the heat load, an External Active Thermal Control System (EATCS) maintains the temperature. The EATCS consists of an internal, non-toxic, water coolant loop used to cool and dehumidify the atmosphere, which transfers collected heat into an external liquid <a href="/wiki/Ammonia" title="Ammonia">ammonia</a> loop. From the heat exchangers, ammonia is pumped into external radiators that emit heat as infrared radiation, then the ammonia is cycled back to the station.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-nasa-stayingcool&#95;240-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-nasa-stayingcool-240"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>235<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The EATCS provides cooling for all the US pressurised modules, including <i>Kibō</i> and <i>Columbus</i>, as well as the main power distribution electronics of the S0, S1 and P1 trusses. It can reject up to 70&#160;kW. This is much more than the 14&#160;kW of the Early External Active Thermal Control System (EEATCS) via the Early Ammonia Servicer (EAS), which was launched on <a href="/wiki/STS-105" title="STS-105">STS-105</a> and installed onto the P6 Truss.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-acts-overview&#95;241-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-acts-overview-241"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>236<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Communications_and_computers">Communications and computers</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main articles: <a href="/wiki/Tracking_and_Data_Relay_Satellite" class="mw-redirect" title="Tracking and Data Relay Satellite">Tracking and Data Relay Satellite</a> and <a href="/wiki/Luch_(satellite)" title="Luch (satellite)">Luch (satellite)</a></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">See also: <a href="/wiki/ThinkPad#Use_in_space" title="ThinkPad">ThinkPad §&#160;Use in space</a></div><p>The ISS relies on various radio communication systems to provide <a href="/wiki/Telemetry" title="Telemetry">telemetry</a> and scientific data links between the station and <a href="/wiki/Mission_control_center" title="Mission control center">mission control centers</a>. Radio links are also used during <a href="/wiki/Space_rendezvous" title="Space rendezvous">rendezvous and docking procedures</a> and for audio and video communication between crew members, flight controllers and family members. As a result, the ISS is equipped with internal and external communication systems used for different purposes.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-BoeingComm&#95;242-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-BoeingComm-242"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>237<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The Russian Orbital Segment primarily uses the <i><a href="/wiki/Lira_(ISS)" title="Lira (ISS)">Lira</a></i> antenna mounted on <i>Zvezda</i> for direct ground communication.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-ISSRG&#95;75-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-ISSRG-75"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>73<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-243" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-243"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>238<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> It also had the capability to utilize the <i><a href="/wiki/Luch_(satellite)" title="Luch (satellite)">Luch</a></i> data relay satellite system,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-ISSRG&#95;75-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-ISSRG-75"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>73<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> which was in a state of disrepair when the station was built,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-ISSRG&#95;75-3" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-ISSRG-75"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>73<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-SSSM&#95;244-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-SSSM-244"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>239<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-Harvey&#95;245-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Harvey-245"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>240<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> but was restored to operational status in 2011 and 2012 with the launch of Luch-5A and Luch-5B.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-246" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-246"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>241<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Additionally, the <a href="/wiki/Voskhod-M" title="Voskhod-M">Voskhod-M</a> system provides internal telephone communications and VHF radio links to ground control.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-247" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-247"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>242<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The <a href="/wiki/US_Orbital_Segment" title="US Orbital Segment">US Orbital Segment</a> (USOS) makes use of two separate radio links: <a href="/wiki/S_band" title="S band">S band</a> (audio, telemetry, commanding – located on the P1/S1 truss) and <a href="/wiki/Ku_band" title="Ku band">K<sub>u</sub> band</a> (audio, video and data – located on the <a href="/wiki/Z1_truss" class="mw-redirect" title="Z1 truss">Z1 truss</a>) systems. These transmissions are routed via the United States <a href="/wiki/Tracking_and_Data_Relay_Satellite" class="mw-redirect" title="Tracking and Data Relay Satellite">Tracking and Data Relay Satellite</a> System (TDRSS) in <a href="/wiki/Geostationary_orbit" title="Geostationary orbit">geostationary orbit</a>, allowing for almost continuous real-time communications with <a href="/wiki/Christopher_C._Kraft_Jr._Mission_Control_Center" title="Christopher C. Kraft Jr. Mission Control Center">Christopher C. Kraft Jr. Mission Control Center</a> (MCC-H) in <a href="/wiki/Houston" title="Houston">Houston</a>, Texas.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-ISSRG&#95;75-4" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-ISSRG-75"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>73<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-ISSBook&#95;248-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-ISSBook-248"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>243<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-BoeingComm&#95;242-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-BoeingComm-242"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>237<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Data channels for the Canadarm2, European <i>Columbus</i> laboratory and Japanese <i>Kibō</i> modules were originally also routed via the S band and K<sub>u</sub> band systems, with the <a href="/wiki/European_Data_Relay_System" title="European Data Relay System">European Data Relay System</a> and a similar Japanese system intended to eventually complement the TDRSS in this role.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-ISSBook&#95;248-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-ISSBook-248"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>243<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-JAXA-MOU&#95;249-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-JAXA-MOU-249"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>244<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p><a href="/wiki/Ultra_high_frequency" title="Ultra high frequency">UHF radio</a> is used by astronauts and cosmonauts conducting EVAs and other spacecraft that dock to or undock from the station.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-ISSRG&#95;75-5" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-ISSRG-75"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>73<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Automated spacecraft are fitted with their own communications equipment; the ATV used a <a href="/wiki/Laser" title="Laser">laser</a> attached to the spacecraft and the Proximity Communications Equipment attached to <i>Zvezda</i> to accurately dock with the station.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-spaceref-20050228&#95;250-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-spaceref-20050228-250"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>245<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-nsf-20091110&#95;251-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-nsf-20091110-251"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>246<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1273380762/mw-parser-output/.tmulti" /><div class="thumb tmulti tright"><div class="thumbinner multiimageinner" style="width:591px;max-width:591px"><div class="trow"><div class="tsingle" style="width:195px;max-width:195px"><div class="thumbimage" style="height:128px;overflow:hidden"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:ISS-38_EVA-1_Laptops.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/ISS-38_EVA-1_Laptops.jpg/250px-ISS-38_EVA-1_Laptops.jpg" decoding="async" width="193" height="128" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/ISS-38_EVA-1_Laptops.jpg/500px-ISS-38_EVA-1_Laptops.jpg 2x" data-file-width="4288" data-file-height="2848" /></a></span></div><div class="thumbcaption">An array of laptops in the US lab</div></div><div class="tsingle" style="width:195px;max-width:195px"><div class="thumbimage" style="height:128px;overflow:hidden"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:STS-128_ISS-20_Destiny_Canadarm2.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/19/STS-128_ISS-20_Destiny_Canadarm2.jpg/250px-STS-128_ISS-20_Destiny_Canadarm2.jpg" decoding="async" width="193" height="128" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/19/STS-128_ISS-20_Destiny_Canadarm2.jpg/500px-STS-128_ISS-20_Destiny_Canadarm2.jpg 2x" data-file-width="3072" data-file-height="2040" /></a></span></div><div class="thumbcaption">Laptop computers surround the Canadarm2 console.</div></div><div class="tsingle" style="width:195px;max-width:195px"><div class="thumbimage" style="height:128px;overflow:hidden"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:ISS_laptop_hard_drive_failure_error_message.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/ISS_laptop_hard_drive_failure_error_message.jpg/250px-ISS_laptop_hard_drive_failure_error_message.jpg" decoding="async" width="193" height="128" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/ISS_laptop_hard_drive_failure_error_message.jpg/500px-ISS_laptop_hard_drive_failure_error_message.jpg 2x" data-file-width="4288" data-file-height="2848" /></a></span></div><div class="thumbcaption">An error message displays a problem with a hard drive on a laptop aboard the ISS.</div></div></div></div></div>
<p>The US Orbital Segment of the ISS is equipped with approximately 100 <a href="/wiki/Commercial_off-the-shelf" title="Commercial off-the-shelf">commercial off-the-shelf</a> laptops running Windows or Linux.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-techrepublic-20160523&#95;252-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-techrepublic-20160523-252"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>247<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> These devices are modified to use the station's 28V DC power system and with additional ventilation since heat generated by the devices can stagnate in the weightless environment. NASA prefers to keep a high commonality between laptops and spare parts are kept on the station so astronauts can repair laptops when needed.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-esa-20130813&#95;253-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-esa-20130813-253"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>248<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The laptops are divided into two groups: the Portable Computer System (PCS) and Station Support Computers (SSC).
</p><p>PCS laptops run Linux and are used for connecting to the station's primary Command &amp; Control computer (C&amp;C MDM), which runs on <a href="/wiki/Debian" title="Debian">Debian</a> Linux,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-register-20130510&#95;254-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-register-20130510-254"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>249<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> a switch made from Windows in 2013 for reliability and flexibility.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-255" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-255"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>250<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The primary computer supervises the critical systems that keep the station in orbit and supporting life.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-techrepublic-20160523&#95;252-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-techrepublic-20160523-252"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>247<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Since the primary computer has no display or keyboards, astronauts use a PCS laptop to connect as remote terminals via a USB to <a href="/wiki/MIL-STD-1553" title="MIL-STD-1553">1553</a> adapter.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-nasa-20190430&#95;256-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-nasa-20190430-256"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>251<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The primary computer experienced failures in 2001,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-roundup20010601&#95;257-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-roundup20010601-257"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>252<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> 2007,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-reuters20070613&#95;258-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-reuters20070613-258"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>253<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and 2017. The 2017 failure required a spacewalk to replace external components.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-huffpost20170522&#95;259-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-huffpost20170522-259"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>254<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>SSC laptops are used for everything else on the station, including reviewing procedures, managing scientific experiments, communicating over e-mail or video chat, and for entertainment during downtime.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-techrepublic-20160523&#95;252-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-techrepublic-20160523-252"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>247<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> SSC laptops connect to the station's <a href="/wiki/Wireless_LAN" title="Wireless LAN">wireless LAN</a> via <a href="/wiki/Wi-Fi" title="Wi-Fi">Wi-Fi</a>, which connects to the ground via the K<sub>u</sub> band. While originally this provided speeds of 10&#160;<a href="/wiki/Megabit_per_second" class="mw-redirect" title="Megabit per second">Mbit/s</a> download and 3&#160;Mbit/s upload from the station,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-tested20121019&#95;260-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-tested20121019-260"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>255<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> NASA upgraded the system in 2019 and increased the speeds to 600&#160;Mbit/s.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-universetoday-20190825&#95;261-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-universetoday-20190825-261"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>256<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> ISS crew members have access to the <a href="/wiki/Internet" title="Internet">internet</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Kuksov&#95;262-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Kuksov-262"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>257<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-ScienceAlert&#95;263-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-ScienceAlert-263"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>258<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Operations">Operations</h2></div>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Expeditions">Expeditions</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">See also: <a href="/wiki/List_of_International_Space_Station_expeditions" title="List of International Space Station expeditions">List of International Space Station expeditions</a></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1273380762/mw-parser-output/.tmulti" /><div class="thumb tmulti tright"><div class="thumbinner multiimageinner" style="width:392px;max-width:392px"><div class="trow"><div class="tsingle" style="width:170px;max-width:170px"><div class="thumbimage" style="height:141px;overflow:hidden"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:Sts088-703-019e.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/Sts088-703-019e.jpg/250px-Sts088-703-019e.jpg" decoding="async" width="168" height="142" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/Sts088-703-019e.jpg/500px-Sts088-703-019e.jpg 2x" data-file-width="1716" data-file-height="1448" /></a></span></div><div class="thumbcaption"><i>Zarya</i> and <i>Unity</i> were entered for the first time on 10 December 1998.</div></div><div class="tsingle" style="width:218px;max-width:218px"><div class="thumbimage" style="height:141px;overflow:hidden"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:Soyuz_tm-31_transported_to_launch_pad.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/Soyuz_tm-31_transported_to_launch_pad.jpg/250px-Soyuz_tm-31_transported_to_launch_pad.jpg" decoding="async" width="216" height="142" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/Soyuz_tm-31_transported_to_launch_pad.jpg/500px-Soyuz_tm-31_transported_to_launch_pad.jpg 2x" data-file-width="3000" data-file-height="1968" /></a></span></div><div class="thumbcaption">Soyuz TM-31 being prepared to bring the first resident crew to the station in October 2000</div></div></div></div></div>
<p>Each permanent crew is given an expedition number. Expeditions run up to six months, from launch until undocking, an 'increment' covers the same time period, but includes cargo spacecraft and all activities. Expeditions 1 to 6 consisted of three-person crews. After the destruction of NASA's Space Shuttle <i>Columbia</i>, Expeditions 7 to 12 were reduced to two-person "caretaker" crews who could maintain the station, because a larger crew could not be fully resupplied by the small Russian Progress cargo spacecraft.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-264" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-264"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>259<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> After the Shuttle fleet returned to flight, three person crews also returned to the ISS beginning with Expedition 13. As the Shuttle flights expanded the station, crew sizes also expanded, eventually reaching six around 2010.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-ISSEx&#95;265-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-ISSEx-265"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>260<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-current&#95;266-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-current-266"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>261<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> With the arrival of crew on larger US <a href="/wiki/Development_of_the_Commercial_Crew_Program" title="Development of the Commercial Crew Program">commercial spacecraft</a> beginning in 2020,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-bbc-20200119&#95;267-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-bbc-20200119-267"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>262<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> crew size has been increased to seven, the number for which ISS was originally designed.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-268" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-268"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>263<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-airandspace-201104&#95;269-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-airandspace-201104-269"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>264<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p><a href="/wiki/Oleg_Kononenko" title="Oleg Kononenko">Oleg Kononenko</a> of Roscosmos holds the record for the longest time spent in space and at the ISS, accumulating nearly 1,111 days in space over the course of five long-duration missions on the ISS (<a href="/wiki/Expedition_17" title="Expedition 17">Expedition 17</a>, <a href="/wiki/Expedition_30" title="Expedition 30">30</a>/<a href="/wiki/Expedition_31" title="Expedition 31">31</a>, <a href="/wiki/Expedition_44" title="Expedition 44">44</a>/<a href="/wiki/Expedition_45" title="Expedition 45">45</a>, <a href="/wiki/Expedition_57" title="Expedition 57">57</a>/<a href="/wiki/Expedition_58" title="Expedition 58">58</a>/<a href="/wiki/Expedition_59" title="Expedition 59">59</a> and <a href="/wiki/Expedition_69" title="Expedition 69">69</a>/<a href="/wiki/Expedition_70" title="Expedition 70">70</a>/<a href="/wiki/Expedition_71" title="Expedition 71">71</a>). He also served as commander three times (Expedition 31, 58/59 and 70/71).<sup id="cite&#95;ref-270" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-270"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>265<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p><a href="/wiki/Peggy_Whitson" title="Peggy Whitson">Peggy Whitson</a> of NASA and <a href="/wiki/Axiom_Space" title="Axiom Space">Axiom Space</a> has spent the most time in space of any American, accumulating over 675 days in space during her time on Expeditions <a href="/wiki/Expedition_5" title="Expedition 5">5</a>, <a href="/wiki/Expedition_16" title="Expedition 16">16</a>, and <a href="/wiki/Expedition_50" title="Expedition 50">50</a>/<a href="/wiki/Expedition_51" title="Expedition 51">51</a>/<a href="/wiki/Expedition_52" title="Expedition 52">52</a> and Axiom Missions <a href="/wiki/Axiom_Mission_2" title="Axiom Mission 2">2</a> and <a href="/wiki/Axiom_Mission_4" title="Axiom Mission 4">4</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-271" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-271"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>266<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-ax2commander&#95;272-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-ax2commander-272"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>267<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Private_flights">Private flights</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">See also: <a href="/wiki/Space_tourism" title="Space tourism">Space tourism</a></div>
<p>As of June 2023, 13 individuals have paid for their own travel to visit the ISS. In news coverage, such travellers are often referred to as "space tourists"; however, many have objected to the term, as they typically undergo professional training and conduct scientific, educational, or outreach activities while on orbit.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-278" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-278"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>f<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-rfe-20060915&#95;279-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-rfe-20060915-279"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>273<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Accordingly, Roscosmos and NASA classify them as <a href="/wiki/Spaceflight_participant" title="Spaceflight participant">spaceflight participants</a>.
</p><p>Initially, privately funded access to the ISS was provided exclusively by Roscosmos through seats on Soyuz spacecraft, either during crew rotations or on dedicated missions. These seats were marketed by Space Adventures at prices of about US$40&#160;million.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-space&#95;adventures&#95;280-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-space_adventures-280"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>274<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-tass20200219&#95;281-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-tass20200219-281"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>275<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> NASA and the ESA initially criticised the practice, and NASA resisted training <a href="/wiki/Dennis_Tito" title="Dennis Tito">Dennis Tito</a>, the first person to pay for an ISS stay.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-282" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-282"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>g<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Beginning in 2021, NASA also began authorizing commercially organized visits known as Private Astronaut Missions (PAMs). These flights are required to use a NASA-certified U.S. commercial vehicle and to include a mission commander who is a former NASA astronaut, responsible for spacecraft operations and oversight of the other spaceflight participants.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-283" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-283"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>276<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The first PAM, <a href="/wiki/Axiom_Mission_1" title="Axiom Mission 1">Axiom Mission 1</a>, launched in 2022 with one Axiom commander and three private passengers,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-284" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-284"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>277<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-axiom-first-private&#95;285-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-axiom-first-private-285"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>278<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-ax1overview&#95;286-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-ax1overview-286"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>279<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> followed in 2023 by <a href="/wiki/Axiom_Mission_2" title="Axiom Mission 2">Axiom Mission 2</a>, with one private passenger and two astronauts from the <a href="/wiki/Saudi_Space_Agency" title="Saudi Space Agency">Saudi Space Agency</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Sheetz&#95;2021&#95;287-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Sheetz_2021-287"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>280<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-Ax-2&#95;Overview&#95;288-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Ax-2_Overview-288"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>281<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> As of 2025, NASA offers up to two PAM opportunities per year.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-289" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-289"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>282<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In addition to private individuals, PAMs are frequently used by ESA and other national governments to fly astronauts for short-term missions.
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Fleet_operations">Fleet operations</h3></div>
<p>Various crewed and uncrewed spacecraft have supported the station's operations. Flights to the ISS have included 93 Progress,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-290" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-290"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>h<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> 73 Soyuz, 51 <a href="/wiki/SpaceX_Dragon" title="SpaceX Dragon">SpaceX Dragon</a>,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-291" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-291"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>i<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> 37 Space Shuttle, 21 <a href="/wiki/Cygnus_(spacecraft)" title="Cygnus (spacecraft)">Cygnus</a>, 10 <a href="/wiki/H-II_Transfer_Vehicle" title="H-II Transfer Vehicle">HTV</a>/<a href="/wiki/HTV-X" title="HTV-X">HTV-X</a>, 5 <a href="/wiki/Automated_Transfer_Vehicle" title="Automated Transfer Vehicle">ATV</a>, and 2 <a href="/wiki/Boeing_Starliner" title="Boeing Starliner">Boeing Starliner</a> missions.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-spacecom-20210810&#95;292-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-spacecom-20210810-292"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>283<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>There are currently eight docking ports for visiting spacecraft, with four additional ports installed but not yet put into service:<sup id="cite&#95;ref-her&#95;293-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-her-293"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>284<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<ol><li><i><a href="/wiki/Harmony_(ISS_module)" title="Harmony (ISS module)">Harmony</a></i> forward (with <a href="/wiki/Pressurized_Mating_Adapter" title="Pressurized Mating Adapter"><i>PMA 2</i></a> &amp; <a href="/wiki/International_Docking_Adapter" title="International Docking Adapter"><i>IDA 2</i></a>)</li>
<li><i>Harmony</i> zenith (with <a href="/wiki/Pressurized_Mating_Adapter" title="Pressurized Mating Adapter"><i>PMA 3</i></a> &amp; <a href="/wiki/International_Docking_Adapter" title="International Docking Adapter"><i>IDA 3</i></a>)</li>
<li><i>Harmony</i> nadir (<a href="/wiki/Common_Berthing_Mechanism" title="Common Berthing Mechanism">CBM</a> port)</li>
<li><i><a href="/wiki/Unity_(ISS_module)" title="Unity (ISS module)">Unity</a></i> nadir (<a href="/wiki/Common_Berthing_Mechanism" title="Common Berthing Mechanism">CBM</a> port)</li>
<li><i><a href="/wiki/Prichal_(ISS_module)" title="Prichal (ISS module)">Prichal</a></i> aft<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Prichal&#95;294-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Prichal-294"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>j<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></li>
<li><i>Prichal</i> forward<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Prichal&#95;294-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Prichal-294"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>j<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></li>
<li><i>Prichal</i> nadir</li>
<li><i>Prichal</i> port<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Prichal&#95;294-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Prichal-294"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>j<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></li>
<li><i>Prichal</i> starboard<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Prichal&#95;294-3" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Prichal-294"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>j<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></li>
<li><i><a href="/wiki/Poisk_(ISS_module)" title="Poisk (ISS module)">Poisk</a></i> zenith</li>
<li><i><a href="/wiki/Rassvet_(ISS_module)" title="Rassvet (ISS module)">Rassvet</a></i> nadir</li>
<li><i><a href="/wiki/Zvezda_(ISS_module)" title="Zvezda (ISS module)">Zvezda</a></i> aft</li></ol>
<p><a href="/wiki/List_of_ship_directions" title="List of ship directions">Forward</a> ports are at the front of the station in its usual orientation and direction of travel. <a href="/wiki/List_of_ship_directions" title="List of ship directions">Aft</a> is the opposite, at the rear. <a href="/wiki/Nadir" title="Nadir">Nadir</a> points toward Earth, while <a href="/wiki/Zenith" title="Zenith">zenith</a> points away from it. <a href="/wiki/Port_and_starboard" title="Port and starboard">Port</a> is to the left and <a href="/wiki/Port_and_starboard" title="Port and starboard">starboard</a> to the right when one's feet are toward Earth and one is facing forward, in the direction of travel.
</p><p>Cargo spacecraft that will perform an orbital re-boost of the station will typically dock at an aft, forward or nadir-facing port.
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Crewed">Crewed</h4></div>
