<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/List_of_human_spaceflights_to_the_International_Space_Station" title="List of human spaceflights to the International Space Station">List of human spaceflights to the International Space Station</a></div>
<p>As of 14&#160;February&#160;2026<sup class="plainlinks noexcerpt noprint asof-tag ref" style="display:none;"><a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://www.nasa.gov/international-space-station/space-station-visitors-by-country/">&#91;ref&#93;</a></sup>, a total of 294 individuals from 26 countries have visited the ISS, including both government-sponsored astronauts and privately funded spaceflight participants. The United States accounted for 172 of these visitors, followed by Russia with 65, Japan with 11, and <a href="/wiki/Canada" title="Canada">Canada</a> with 9. <a href="/wiki/Italy" title="Italy">Italy</a> had 6 visitors, <a href="/wiki/France" title="France">France</a> had 5 and <a href="/wiki/Germany" title="Germany">Germany</a> had 4. <a href="/wiki/Saudi_Arabia" title="Saudi Arabia">Saudi Arabia</a>, <a href="/wiki/Sweden" title="Sweden">Sweden</a>, and the <a href="/wiki/United_Arab_Emirates" title="United Arab Emirates">United Arab Emirates</a> each had 2 individuals visit the ISS. One person has traveled to the ISS from each of the following countries: <a href="/wiki/Belarus" title="Belarus">Belarus</a>, <a href="/wiki/Belgium" title="Belgium">Belgium</a>, <a href="/wiki/Brazil" title="Brazil">Brazil</a>, <a href="/wiki/Denmark" title="Denmark">Denmark</a>, <a href="/wiki/Hungary" title="Hungary">Hungary</a>, <a href="/wiki/India" title="India">India</a>, <a href="/wiki/Israel" title="Israel">Israel</a>, <a href="/wiki/Kazakhstan" title="Kazakhstan">Kazakhstan</a>, <a href="/wiki/Malaysia" title="Malaysia">Malaysia</a>, the <a href="/wiki/Netherlands" title="Netherlands">Netherlands</a>, <a href="/wiki/Poland" title="Poland">Poland</a>, <a href="/wiki/South_Africa" title="South Africa">South Africa</a>, <a href="/wiki/South_Korea" title="South Korea">South Korea</a>, <a href="/wiki/Spain" title="Spain">Spain</a>, <a href="/wiki/Turkey" title="Turkey">Turkey</a>, and the <a href="/wiki/United_Kingdom" title="United Kingdom">United Kingdom</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-NASA&#95;Visitor&#95;Count&#95;2023&#95;295-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-NASA_Visitor_Count_2023-295"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>285<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>

<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Uncrewed">Uncrewed</h4></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Uncrewed_spaceflights_to_the_International_Space_Station" title="Uncrewed spaceflights to the International Space Station">Uncrewed spaceflights to the International Space Station</a></div>
<p>Uncrewed spaceflights are primarily used to deliver cargo to the station including crew supplies, scientific investigations, spacewalk equipment, vehicle hardware, propellant, water, and gases. Cargo resupply missions have typically used Russian Progress spacecraft, the now-retired European ATV, the Japanese HTV, and American Dragon and Cygnus spacecraft. Additionally, several Russian modules have been launched on uncrewed rockets and autonomously docked with the station.
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Currently_docked/berthed"><span id="Currently_docked.2Fberthed"></span>Currently docked/berthed</h4></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:December_1,_2025_International_Space_Station_Configuration.png" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dd/December_1%2C_2025_International_Space_Station_Configuration.png/250px-December_1%2C_2025_International_Space_Station_Configuration.png" decoding="async" width="250" height="141" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dd/December_1%2C_2025_International_Space_Station_Configuration.png/500px-December_1%2C_2025_International_Space_Station_Configuration.png 2x" data-file-width="1280" data-file-height="720" /></a><figcaption>Rendering of the ISS and visiting vehicles as of 1&#160;December&#160;2025<sup class="plainlinks noexcerpt noprint asof-tag update" style="display:none;"><a class="external text" href="https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=International_Space_Station&amp;action=edit">&#91;update&#93;</a></sup>. <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://www.nasa.gov/international-space-station/space-station-visiting-vehicles/">Live link at nasa.gov</a>.</figcaption></figure>
<p><i>All dates are <a href="/wiki/UTC" class="mw-redirect" title="UTC">UTC</a>. Departure dates are the earliest possible (<abbr title="not earlier than">NET</abbr>) and may change.</i>
</p>

</td></tr></tbody></table>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Docking_and_berthing_of_spacecraft">Docking and berthing of spacecraft</h4></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">See also: <a href="/wiki/Docking_and_berthing_of_spacecraft" title="Docking and berthing of spacecraft">Docking and berthing of spacecraft</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Progress_M-14M.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/Progress_M-14M.jpg/250px-Progress_M-14M.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="166" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/Progress_M-14M.jpg/500px-Progress_M-14M.jpg 2x" data-file-width="3904" data-file-height="2600" /></a><figcaption>The <a href="/wiki/Progress_M-14M" title="Progress M-14M">Progress M-14M</a> resupply vehicle approaching the ISS in 2012. Nearly 100 unpiloted <a href="/wiki/Progress_(spacecraft)" title="Progress (spacecraft)">Progress</a> spacecraft have delivered supplies during the lifetime of the station.</figcaption></figure>
<p>Russian spacecraft can autonomously rendezvous and dock with the station without human intervention. Once within about 200 kilometres (120&#160;mi) the spacecraft activates its <a href="/wiki/Kurs_(docking_navigation_system)" title="Kurs (docking navigation system)">Kurs docking navigation system</a>, exchanging radio signals with the station's beacon to guide orbital manoeuvres. As it closes in, more accurate transceivers align the craft with the docking port and control the final approach. The crew supervises the procedure and can intervene using the <a href="/wiki/TORU" title="TORU">TORU</a> (Tele-robotically Operated Rendezvous Unit) system if required.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-297" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-297"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>287<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Automated docking has been used by the Soviet program since 1967, with Kurs introduced on <i>Mir</i> in 1986 and refined since. Though costly to develop, its reliability and standardised components have delivered significant long-term savings.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-298" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-298"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>288<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The American <a href="/wiki/SpaceX_Dragon_2" title="SpaceX Dragon 2">SpaceX Dragon 2</a> cargo and crewed spacecraft can autonomously rendezvous and dock with the station without human intervention. However, on crewed Dragon missions, the astronauts have the capability to intervene and fly the vehicle manually.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-299" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-299"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>289<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:ISS-36_HTV-4_berthing_2.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/ISS-36_HTV-4_berthing_2.jpg/250px-ISS-36_HTV-4_berthing_2.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="187" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/ISS-36_HTV-4_berthing_2.jpg/500px-ISS-36_HTV-4_berthing_2.jpg 2x" data-file-width="2592" data-file-height="1936" /></a><figcaption>Japan's <a href="/wiki/Kounotori_4" title="Kounotori 4">Kounotori 4</a> berthing</figcaption></figure>
<p>Other <a href="/wiki/Automated_cargo_spacecraft" class="mw-redirect" title="Automated cargo spacecraft">automated cargo spacecraft</a> typically use a semi-automated process when arriving and departing from the station. These spacecraft are instructed to approach and park near the station. Once the crew on board the station is ready, the spacecraft is commanded to come close to the station, so that it can be grappled by an astronaut using the <a href="/wiki/Mobile_Servicing_System" title="Mobile Servicing System">Mobile Servicing System</a> robotic arm. The final mating of the spacecraft to the station is achieved using the robotic arm (a process known as berthing). Spacecraft using this semi-automated process include the American <a href="/wiki/Cygnus_(spacecraft)" title="Cygnus (spacecraft)">Cygnus</a> and the Japanese <a href="/wiki/HTV-X" title="HTV-X">HTV-X</a>. The now-retired American <a href="/wiki/SpaceX_Dragon_1" title="SpaceX Dragon 1">SpaceX Dragon 1</a>, European <a href="/wiki/Automated_Transfer_Vehicle" title="Automated Transfer Vehicle">ATV</a> and Japanese <a href="/wiki/H-II_Transfer_Vehicle" title="H-II Transfer Vehicle">HTV</a> also used this process.
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Launch_and_docking_windows">Launch and docking windows</h4></div>
<p>Prior to a spacecraft's docking to the ISS, navigation and attitude control (<a href="/wiki/Guidance,_navigation,_and_control" title="Guidance, navigation, and control">GNC</a>) is handed over to the ground control of the spacecraft's country of origin. GNC is set to allow the station to drift in space, rather than fire its thrusters or turn using gyroscopes. The solar panels of the station are turned edge-on to the incoming spacecraft, so residue from its thrusters does not damage the cells. Before its retirement, Shuttle launches were often given priority over Soyuz, with occasional priority given to Soyuz arrivals carrying crew and time-critical cargoes, such as biological experiment materials.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-300" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-300"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>290<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Repairs">Repairs</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Maintenance_of_the_International_Space_Station" title="Maintenance of the International Space Station">Maintenance of the International Space Station</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:ISS_Unpressurized_Platforms.png" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/ISS_Unpressurized_Platforms.png/500px-ISS_Unpressurized_Platforms.png" decoding="async" width="450" height="200" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/ISS_Unpressurized_Platforms.png/960px-ISS_Unpressurized_Platforms.png 2x" data-file-width="1112" data-file-height="493" /></a><figcaption>Spare parts are called <a href="/wiki/Orbital_replacement_unit" title="Orbital replacement unit">ORUs</a>; some are externally stored on pallets called <a href="/wiki/ExPRESS_Logistics_Carrier" title="ExPRESS Logistics Carrier">ELCs</a> and <a href="/wiki/External_stowage_platform" title="External stowage platform">ESPs</a>.</figcaption></figure>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:STS-120_EVA_Scott_Parazynski.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img alt="Two black and orange solar arrays, shown uneven and with a large tear visible. A crew member in a spacesuit, attached to the end of a robotic arm, holds a latticework between two solar sails." src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/STS-120_EVA_Scott_Parazynski.jpg/250px-STS-120_EVA_Scott_Parazynski.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="166" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/STS-120_EVA_Scott_Parazynski.jpg/500px-STS-120_EVA_Scott_Parazynski.jpg 2x" data-file-width="3032" data-file-height="2008" /></a><figcaption>While anchored on the end of the <a href="/wiki/Orbiter_Boom_Sensor_System" title="Orbiter Boom Sensor System">Orbiter Boom Sensor System</a> during <a href="/wiki/STS-120" title="STS-120">STS-120</a>, astronaut <a href="/wiki/Scott_Parazynski" class="mw-redirect" title="Scott Parazynski">Scott Parazynski</a> performs makeshift repairs to a US solar array that was damaged during unfolding</figcaption></figure>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Astronaut_Mike_Hopkins_on_Dec._24_Spacewalk.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/Astronaut_Mike_Hopkins_on_Dec._24_Spacewalk.jpg/250px-Astronaut_Mike_Hopkins_on_Dec._24_Spacewalk.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="166" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/Astronaut_Mike_Hopkins_on_Dec._24_Spacewalk.jpg/500px-Astronaut_Mike_Hopkins_on_Dec._24_Spacewalk.jpg 2x" data-file-width="4288" data-file-height="2848" /></a><figcaption><a href="/wiki/Michael_S._Hopkins" class="mw-redirect" title="Michael S. Hopkins">Mike Hopkins</a> during a spacewalk</figcaption></figure>
<p><a href="/wiki/Orbital_Replacement_Unit" class="mw-redirect" title="Orbital Replacement Unit">Orbital Replacement Units</a> (ORUs) are spare parts that can be readily replaced when a unit either passes its design life or fails. Examples of ORUs are pumps, storage tanks, controller boxes, antennas, and battery units. Some units can be replaced using robotic arms. Most are stored outside the station, either on small pallets called <a href="/wiki/ExPRESS_Logistics_Carrier" title="ExPRESS Logistics Carrier">ExPRESS Logistics Carriers</a> (ELCs) or share larger platforms called <a href="/wiki/External_Stowage_Platform" class="mw-redirect" title="External Stowage Platform">External Stowage Platforms</a> (ESPs) which also hold science experiments. Both kinds of pallets provide electricity for many parts that could be damaged by the cold of space and require heating. The larger logistics carriers also have local area network (LAN) connections for telemetry to connect experiments. A heavy emphasis on stocking the USOS with ORU's occurred around 2011, before the end of the NASA shuttle program, as its commercial replacements, Cygnus and Dragon, carry one tenth to one quarter the payload.
</p><p>Unexpected problems and failures have impacted the station's assembly time-line and work schedules leading to periods of reduced capabilities and, in some cases, could have forced abandonment of the station for safety reasons. Serious problems include an air leak from the USOS in 2004,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-nbc-20040106&#95;301-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-nbc-20040106-301"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>291<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> the venting of fumes from an <i><a href="/wiki/Elektron_(ISS)" class="mw-redirect" title="Elektron (ISS)">Elektron</a></i> oxygen generator in 2006,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-sfn-20060918&#95;302-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-sfn-20060918-302"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>292<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and the failure of the computers in the ROS in 2007 during <a href="/wiki/STS-117" title="STS-117">STS-117</a> that left the station without thruster, <i>Elektron</i>, <i><a href="/wiki/ISS_ECLSS#Vozdukh" title="ISS ECLSS">Vozdukh</a></i> and other environmental control system operations. In the latter case, the root cause was found to be condensation inside electrical connectors leading to a short circuit.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-toledo-blade-20081004&#95;303-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-toledo-blade-20081004-303"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>293<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>During STS-120 in 2007 and following the relocation of the P6 truss and solar arrays, it was noted during unfurling that the solar array had torn and was not deploying properly.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Astronauts&#95;notice&#95;tear&#95;in&#95;solar&#95;panel&#95;304-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Astronauts_notice_tear_in_solar_panel-304"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>294<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> An EVA was carried out by <a href="/wiki/Scott_E._Parazynski" title="Scott E. Parazynski">Scott Parazynski</a>, assisted by <a href="/wiki/Douglas_H._Wheelock" title="Douglas H. Wheelock">Douglas Wheelock</a>. Extra precautions were taken to reduce the risk of electric shock, as the repairs were carried out with the solar array exposed to sunlight.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Space&#95;Station&#95;305-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Space_Station-305"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>295<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The issues with the array were followed in the same year by problems with the starboard Solar Alpha Rotary Joint (SARJ), which rotates the arrays on the starboard side of the station. Excessive vibration and high-current spikes in the array drive motor were noted, resulting in a decision to substantially curtail motion of the starboard SARJ until the cause was understood. Inspections during EVAs on STS-120 and <a href="/wiki/STS-123" title="STS-123">STS-123</a> showed extensive contamination from metallic shavings and debris in the large drive gear and confirmed damage to the large metallic bearing surfaces, so the joint was locked to prevent further damage.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-joint-update&#95;306-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-joint-update-306"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>296<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-iss-joint-investigation&#95;307-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-iss-joint-investigation-307"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>297<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Repairs to the joints were carried out during <a href="/wiki/STS-126" title="STS-126">STS-126</a> with lubrication and the replacement of 11 out of 12 trundle bearings on the joint.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-308" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-308"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>298<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-sfn-20081118&#95;309-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-sfn-20081118-309"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>299<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>In September 2008, damage to the S1 radiator was first noticed in Soyuz imagery. The problem was initially not thought to be serious.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Radiator&#95;310-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Radiator-310"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>300<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The imagery showed that the surface of one sub-panel had peeled back from the underlying central structure, possibly because of micro-meteoroid or debris impact. On 15 May 2009, the damaged radiator panel's ammonia tubing was mechanically shut off from the rest of the cooling system by the computer-controlled closure of a valve. The same valve was then used to vent the ammonia from the damaged panel, eliminating the possibility of an ammonia leak.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Radiator&#95;310-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Radiator-310"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>300<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> It is also known that a Service Module thruster cover struck the S1 radiator after being jettisoned during an EVA in 2008, but its effect, if any, has not been determined.
</p><p>In the early hours of 1 August 2010, a failure in cooling Loop A (starboard side), one of two external cooling loops, left the station with only half of its normal cooling capacity and zero redundancy in some systems.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-SpaceFlightNow1007&#95;31&#95;311-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-SpaceFlightNow1007_31-311"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>301<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-iss-report-20100801&#95;312-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-iss-report-20100801-312"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>302<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-313" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-313"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>303<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The problem appeared to be in the ammonia pump module that circulates the ammonia cooling fluid. Several subsystems, including two of the four CMGs, were shut down.
</p><p>Planned operations on the ISS were interrupted through a series of EVAs to address the cooling system issue. A first EVA on 7 August 2010, to replace the failed pump module, was not fully completed because of an ammonia leak in one of four quick-disconnects. A second EVA on 11 August removed the failed pump module.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-sfn-20100810&#95;314-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-sfn-20100810-314"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>304<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-nsf-20100811&#95;315-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-nsf-20100811-315"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>305<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> A third EVA was required to restore Loop A to normal functionality.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-sfn-20100811&#95;316-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-sfn-20100811-316"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>306<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-nsf-20100818&#95;317-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-nsf-20100818-317"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>307<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The USOS's cooling system is largely built by the US company Boeing,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-spacecom-20100802&#95;318-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-spacecom-20100802-318"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>308<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> which is also the manufacturer of the failed pump.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-SpaceFlightNow1007&#95;31&#95;311-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-SpaceFlightNow1007_31-311"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>301<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The four Main Bus Switching Units (MBSUs, located in the S0 truss), control the routing of power from the four solar array wings to the rest of the ISS. Each MBSU has two power channels that feed 160V DC from the arrays to two DC-to-DC power converters (DDCUs) that supply the 124V power used in the station. In late 2011, MBSU-1 ceased responding to commands or sending data confirming its health. While still routing power correctly, it was scheduled to be swapped out at the next available EVA. A spare MBSU was already on board, but a 30 August 2012 EVA failed to be completed when a bolt being tightened to finish installation of the spare unit jammed before the electrical connection was secured.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-nsf-20120830&#95;319-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-nsf-20120830-319"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>309<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The loss of MBSU-1 limited the station to 75% of its normal power capacity, requiring minor limitations in normal operations until the problem could be addressed.
</p><p>On 5 September 2012, in a second six-hour EVA, astronauts Sunita Williams and Akihiko Hoshide successfully replaced MBSU-1 and restored the ISS to 100% power.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-spaceref-20120905&#95;320-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-spaceref-20120905-320"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>310<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>On 24 December 2013, astronauts installed a new ammonia pump for the station's cooling system. The faulty cooling system had failed earlier in the month, halting many of the station's science experiments. Astronauts had to brave a "mini blizzard" of ammonia while installing the new pump. It was only the second Christmas Eve spacewalk in NASA history.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-321" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-321"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>311<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Mission_control_centers">Mission control centers</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/International_Space_Station_program#Mission_control_centers" title="International Space Station program">International Space Station program §&#160;Mission control centers</a></div>
<p>The components of the ISS are operated and monitored by their respective space agencies at <a href="/wiki/Mission_control_center" title="Mission control center">mission control centers</a> across the globe, primarily the <a href="/wiki/Christopher_C._Kraft_Jr._Mission_Control_Center" title="Christopher C. Kraft Jr. Mission Control Center">Christopher C. Kraft Jr. Mission Control Center</a> in Houston and the <a href="/wiki/RKA_Mission_Control_Center" title="RKA Mission Control Center">RKA Mission Control Center</a> (TsUP) in Moscow, with support from <a href="/wiki/Tsukuba_Space_Center" title="Tsukuba Space Center">Tsukuba Space Center</a> in Japan, <a href="/wiki/Payload_Operations_and_Integration_Center" title="Payload Operations and Integration Center">Payload Operations and Integration Center</a> in Huntsville, Alabama, U.S., <a href="/wiki/Columbus_Control_Center" class="mw-redirect" title="Columbus Control Center">Columbus Control Center</a> in Munich, Germany and <a href="/wiki/Mobile_Servicing_System" title="Mobile Servicing System">Mobile Servicing System</a> Control at the <a href="/wiki/Canadian_Space_Agency" title="Canadian Space Agency">Canadian Space Agency</a>'s headquarters in <a href="/wiki/Saint-Hubert,_Quebec" title="Saint-Hubert, Quebec">Saint-Hubert, Quebec</a>.
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Orbit,_debris_and_visibility"><span id="Orbit.2C_debris_and_visibility"></span>Orbit, debris and visibility</h2></div>
<p><span class="anchor" id="Orbit"></span>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Altitude_and_orbital_inclination">Altitude and orbital inclination</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1273380762/mw-parser-output/.tmulti" /><div class="thumb tmulti tright"><div class="thumbinner multiimageinner" style="width:392px;max-width:392px"><div class="trow"><div class="tsingle" style="width:201px;max-width:201px"><div class="thumbimage" style="height:149px;overflow:hidden"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:Animation_of_International_Space_Station_trajectory.gif" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c8/Animation_of_International_Space_Station_trajectory.gif/250px-Animation_of_International_Space_Station_trajectory.gif" decoding="async" width="199" height="149" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c8/Animation_of_International_Space_Station_trajectory.gif/500px-Animation_of_International_Space_Station_trajectory.gif 2x" data-file-width="560" data-file-height="420" /></a></span></div><div class="thumbcaption">Animation of ISS orbit from 14 September 2018 to 14 November 2018. Earth is not shown.</div></div><div class="tsingle" style="width:187px;max-width:187px"><div class="thumbimage" style="height:149px;overflow:hidden"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:Altitude_of_International_Space_Station.svg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/Altitude_of_International_Space_Station.svg/250px-Altitude_of_International_Space_Station.svg.png" decoding="async" width="185" height="149" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/Altitude_of_International_Space_Station.svg/500px-Altitude_of_International_Space_Station.svg.png 2x" data-file-width="807" data-file-height="649" /></a></span></div><div class="thumbcaption">Graph showing the changing altitude of the ISS from November 1998 until November 2018</div></div></div></div></div>
<p>The ISS is currently maintained in a nearly circular orbit with an <a href="/wiki/Eccentricity" class="mw-disambig" title="Eccentricity">eccentricity</a> of 0.0002267<sup id="cite&#95;ref-322" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-322"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>312<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup class="noprint Inline-Template" style="white-space:nowrap;">&#91;<i><a href="/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items" title="Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers"><span title="This information is in need of an update. (June 2025)">needs update</span></a></i>&#93;</sup> and at an <a href="/wiki/Inclination" class="mw-redirect" title="Inclination">inclination</a> of 51.6 degrees to Earth's equator.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-323" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-323"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>313<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> This orbit was selected because it is the lowest inclination that can be directly reached by Russian Soyuz and Progress spacecraft launched from <a href="/wiki/Baikonur_Cosmodrome" title="Baikonur Cosmodrome">Baikonur Cosmodrome</a> at 46° N latitude without overflying China or dropping spent rocket stages in inhabited areas.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-MCC&#95;Answer&#95;324-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-MCC_Answer-324"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>314<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-325" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-325"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>315<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> As a result, it makes more polar latitudes accessible and observable, for example the <a href="/wiki/Chinese_Space_Station" class="mw-redirect" title="Chinese Space Station">Chinese Space Station</a> orbits more equatorial with an inclination of 41.47°. The ISS travels <a href="/wiki/Retrograde_and_prograde_motion" title="Retrograde and prograde motion">prograde</a> (with Earth's rotation, west to east) at an average speed of 28,000 kilometres per hour (17,000&#160;mph), and completes 15.5 orbits per day (93 minutes per orbit).<sup id="cite&#95;ref-heavens-above&#95;8-5" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-heavens-above-8"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>6<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-tracking&#95;326-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-tracking-326"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>316<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The station orbits in <a href="/wiki/Low_Earth_Orbit" class="mw-redirect" title="Low Earth Orbit">Low Earth Orbit</a> (LEO) at a minimum mean altitude of 370&#160;km (230&#160;mi) and a maximum of 460&#160;km (290&#160;mi),<sup id="cite&#95;ref-nasa-iss-overview&#95;327-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-nasa-iss-overview-327"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>317<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> in the center of the <a href="/wiki/Thermosphere" title="Thermosphere">thermosphere</a>. The altitude was in the past allowed to fall around the time of each NASA <a href="/wiki/Space_Shuttle" title="Space Shuttle">Space Shuttle</a> flight to permit heavier loads to be transferred to the station. After the retirement of the shuttle, the nominal orbit of the space station was raised in altitude (from about 350&#160;km to about 400&#160;km).<sup id="cite&#95;ref-nsf-20110620&#95;328-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-nsf-20110620-328"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>318<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-Popular&#95;Mechanics&#95;329-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Popular_Mechanics-329"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>319<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Other, more frequent supply spacecraft do not require this adjustment as they are substantially higher performance vehicles.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Worldbook&#95;at&#95;NASA&#95;41-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Worldbook_at_NASA-41"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>39<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-nasa.gov-iss-environment&#95;330-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-nasa.gov-iss-environment-330"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>320<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Atmospheric drag reduces the altitude by about 2&#160;km a month on average. To reduce drag the ISS puts its solar arrays while in the dark of Earth's night side edge on toward the flight direction into <a href="/wiki/Night_Glider_mode" title="Night Glider mode">Night Glider mode</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Glider&#95;236-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Glider-236"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>231<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> For <a href="/wiki/Orbital_station-keeping" title="Orbital station-keeping">station keeping</a>, to bring the station back to a higher altitude, a so-called <a href="/wiki/Reboost" title="Reboost">reboost</a> is performed, which takes approximately two orbits (three hours) to be completed.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-nasa.gov-iss-environment&#95;330-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-nasa.gov-iss-environment-330"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>320<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Maintaining ISS altitude uses about 7.5 tonnes of chemical fuel per annum<sup id="cite&#95;ref-newscientist.com&#95;331-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-newscientist.com-331"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>321<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> at an annual cost of about $210&#160;million.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-aaESummary20100124&#95;332-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-aaESummary20100124-332"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>322<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Orbital boosting can be performed by the station's two main engines on the <i>Zvezda</i> service module, or Russian or European spacecraft docked to <i>Zvezda</i><span class="nowrap" style="padding-left:0.1em;">&#39;s</span> aft port. The Automated Transfer Vehicle is constructed with the possibility of adding a <a href="/wiki/Automated_Transfer_Vehicle#ATV_evolution_proposals" title="Automated Transfer Vehicle">second docking port</a> to its aft end, allowing other craft to dock and boost the station.
</p>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:ISS_orbits_04132013.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/ISS_orbits_04132013.jpg/330px-ISS_orbits_04132013.jpg" decoding="async" width="330" height="213" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/ISS_orbits_04132013.jpg/960px-ISS_orbits_04132013.jpg 2x" data-file-width="1010" data-file-height="653" /></a><figcaption>Orbits of the ISS, shown in April 2013</figcaption></figure>
<p>The Russian Orbital Segment contains the Data Management System, which handles Guidance, Navigation and Control (ROS GNC) for the entire station.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Navigation&#95;333-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Navigation-333"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>323<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Initially, <i>Zarya</i>, the first module of the station, controlled the station until a short time after the Russian service module <i>Zvezda</i> docked and was transferred control. <i>Zvezda</i> contains the ESA built DMS-R Data Management System.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-EsaComputer&#95;334-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-EsaComputer-334"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>324<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Using two fault-tolerant computers (FTC), <i>Zvezda</i> computes the station's position and orbital trajectory using redundant Earth horizon sensors, Solar horizon sensors as well as Sun and star trackers. The FTCs each contain three identical processing units working in parallel and provide advanced fault-masking by majority voting.
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Orientation">Orientation</h3></div>
<p><i>Zvezda</i> uses gyroscopes (<a href="/wiki/Reaction_wheel" title="Reaction wheel">reaction wheels</a>) and thrusters to turn itself. Gyroscopes do not require propellant; instead they use electricity to 'store' momentum in flywheels by turning in the opposite direction to the station's movement. The USOS has its own computer-controlled gyroscopes to handle its extra mass. When gyroscopes <a href="/wiki/Control_moment_gyroscope#Saturation" title="Control moment gyroscope">'saturate'</a>, thrusters are used to cancel out the stored momentum. In February 2005, during Expedition 10, an incorrect command was sent to the station's computer, using about 14&#160;kilograms of propellant before the fault was noticed and fixed. When attitude control computers in the ROS and USOS fail to communicate properly, this can result in a rare 'force fight' where the ROS GNC computer must ignore the USOS counterpart, which itself has no thrusters.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-335" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-335"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>325<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-iss-report-05-07&#95;336-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-iss-report-05-07-336"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>326<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-nasa-dynamics-report&#95;337-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-nasa-dynamics-report-337"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>327<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Docked spacecraft can also be used to maintain station attitude, such as for troubleshooting or during the <a href="/wiki/STS-117" title="STS-117">installation of the S3/S4 truss</a>, which provides electrical power and data interfaces for the station's electronics.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-nsf-20070614&#95;338-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-nsf-20070614-338"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>328<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Orbital_debris_threats">Orbital debris threats</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Space_debris" title="Space debris">Space debris</a></div>
<p>The low altitudes at which the ISS orbits are also home to a variety of space debris,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-339" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-339"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>329<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> including spent rocket stages, defunct satellites, explosion fragments (including materials from <a href="/wiki/Anti-satellite_weapon" title="Anti-satellite weapon">anti-satellite weapon</a> tests), paint flakes, slag from solid rocket motors, and coolant released by <a href="/wiki/US-A" title="US-A">US-A</a> nuclear-powered satellites. These objects, in addition to natural <a href="/wiki/Micrometeoroid" title="Micrometeoroid">micrometeoroids</a>,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-340" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-340"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>330<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> are a significant threat. Objects large enough to destroy the station can be tracked, and therefore are not as dangerous as smaller debris.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-NSFdebris&#95;341-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-NSFdebris-341"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>331<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-nasa-micrometeoroid-report&#95;342-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-nasa-micrometeoroid-report-342"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>332<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Objects too small to be detected by optical and radar instruments, from approximately 1&#160;cm down to microscopic size, number in the trillions. Despite their small size, some of these objects are a threat because of their <a href="/wiki/Kinetic_energy" title="Kinetic energy">kinetic energy</a> and direction in relation to the station. Spacewalking crew in spacesuits are also at risk of suit damage and consequent <a href="/wiki/Space_exposure" class="mw-redirect" title="Space exposure">exposure to vacuum</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-debrisdecomp&#95;343-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-debrisdecomp-343"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>333<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Ballistic panels, also called micrometeorite shielding, are incorporated into the station to protect pressurised sections and critical systems. The type and thickness of these panels depend on their predicted exposure to damage. The station's shields and structure have different designs on the ROS and the USOS. On the USOS, <a href="/wiki/Whipple_Shield" class="mw-redirect" title="Whipple Shield">Whipple Shields</a> are used. The US segment modules consist of an inner layer made from 1.5–5.0&#160;cm-thick (0.59–1.97&#160;in) <a href="/wiki/2219_aluminium_alloy" title="2219 aluminium alloy">aluminium</a>, a 10&#160;cm-thick (3.9&#160;in) intermediate layers of <a href="/wiki/Kevlar" title="Kevlar">Kevlar</a> and Nextel (a ceramic fabric),<sup id="cite&#95;ref-nasa-20040716&#95;344-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-nasa-20040716-344"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>334<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and an outer layer of <a href="/wiki/Stainless_steel" title="Stainless steel">stainless steel</a>, which causes objects to shatter into a cloud before hitting the hull, thereby spreading the energy of impact. On the ROS, a <a href="/wiki/Carbon_fibre_reinforced_polymer" class="mw-redirect" title="Carbon fibre reinforced polymer">carbon fibre reinforced polymer</a> honeycomb screen is spaced from the hull, an aluminium honeycomb screen is spaced from that, with a screen-vacuum thermal insulation covering, and glass cloth over the top.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-roscomos-iss&#95;345-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-roscomos-iss-345"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>335<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Space debris is tracked remotely from the ground, and the station crew can be notified.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-nasa-debris-package&#95;346-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-nasa-debris-package-346"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>336<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> If necessary, thrusters on the Russian Orbital Segment can alter the station's orbital altitude, avoiding the debris. These <a href="/wiki/Debris_Avoidance_Manoeuvre" class="mw-redirect" title="Debris Avoidance Manoeuvre">Debris Avoidance Manoeuvres</a> (DAMs) are not uncommon, taking place if computational models show the debris will approach within a certain threat distance. Ten DAMs had been performed by the end of 2009.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-newscientist-20090316&#95;347-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-newscientist-20090316-347"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>337<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-ODOct08&#95;348-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-ODOct08-348"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>338<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-349" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-349"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>339<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Usually, an increase in orbital velocity of the order of 1&#160;m/s is used to raise the orbit by one or two kilometres. If necessary, the altitude can also be lowered, although such a manoeuvre wastes propellant.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-ODOct08&#95;348-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-ODOct08-348"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>338<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-esa-20080828&#95;350-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-esa-20080828-350"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>340<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> If a threat from orbital debris is identified too late for a DAM to be safely conducted, the station crew close all the hatches aboard the station and retreat into their spacecraft in order to be able to evacuate in the event the station was seriously damaged by the debris. Partial station evacuations have occurred on 13 March 2009, 28 June 2011, 24 March 2012, 16 June 2015,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-bbc-20120324&#95;351-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-bbc-20120324-351"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>341<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> November 2021,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-reuters-20211203&#95;352-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-reuters-20211203-352"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>342<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and <a href="/wiki/Resurs-P_No.1" title="Resurs-P No.1">27 June 2024</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-cnn&#95;27Jun2024&#95;353-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-cnn_27Jun2024-353"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>343<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The November 2021 evacuation was caused by a Russian anti-satellite <a href="/wiki/Kosmos_1408" title="Kosmos 1408">weapon test</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-verge-20211115&#95;354-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-verge-20211115-354"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>344<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-BBC&#95;16Nov21&#95;355-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-BBC_16Nov21-355"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>345<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> NASA administrator <a href="/wiki/Bill_Nelson" title="Bill Nelson">Bill Nelson</a> said it was unthinkable that Russia would endanger the lives of everyone on ISS, including their own cosmonauts.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-cnn&#95;15Nov21&#95;356-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-cnn_15Nov21-356"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>346<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<ul class="gallery mw-gallery-traditional">
		<li class="gallerybox" style="width: 235px">
			<div class="thumb" style="width: 230px; height: 190px;"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:SDIO_KEW_Lexan_projectile.jpg" class="mw-file-description" title="A 7-gram object (shown in center) shot at 7&#160;km/s (23,000&#160;ft/s), the orbital velocity of the ISS, made this 15&#160;cm (5.9&#160;in) crater in a solid block of aluminium."><img alt="A 7-gram object (shown in center) shot at 7&#160;km/s (23,000&#160;ft/s), the orbital velocity of the ISS, made this 15&#160;cm (5.9&#160;in) crater in a solid block of aluminium." src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/SDIO_KEW_Lexan_projectile.jpg/250px-SDIO_KEW_Lexan_projectile.jpg" decoding="async" width="200" height="158" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/SDIO_KEW_Lexan_projectile.jpg/500px-SDIO_KEW_Lexan_projectile.jpg 2x" data-file-width="3000" data-file-height="2375" /></a></span></div>
			<div class="gallerytext">A 7-gram object (shown in center) shot at 7&#160;km/s (23,000&#160;ft/s), the orbital velocity of the ISS, made this 15&#160;cm (5.9&#160;in) crater in a solid block of <a href="/wiki/Aluminium" title="Aluminium">aluminium</a>.</div>
		</li>
		<li class="gallerybox" style="width: 235px">
			<div class="thumb" style="width: 230px; height: 190px;"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:Debris-GEO1280.jpg" class="mw-file-description" title="Radar-trackable objects, including debris, with distinct ring of geostationary satellites"><img alt="Radar-trackable objects, including debris, with distinct ring of geostationary satellites" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/Debris-GEO1280.jpg/250px-Debris-GEO1280.jpg" decoding="async" width="200" height="160" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/Debris-GEO1280.jpg/500px-Debris-GEO1280.jpg 2x" data-file-width="1280" data-file-height="1024" /></a></span></div>

		</li>
		<li class="gallerybox" style="width: 235px">
			<div class="thumb" style="width: 230px; height: 190px;"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:ISS_impact_risk.jpg" class="mw-file-description" title="Example of risk management: A NASA model showing areas at high risk from impact for the International Space Station"><img alt="Example of risk management: A NASA model showing areas at high risk from impact for the International Space Station" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/ISS_impact_risk.jpg/250px-ISS_impact_risk.jpg" decoding="async" width="200" height="158" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/ISS_impact_risk.jpg/500px-ISS_impact_risk.jpg 2x" data-file-width="1612" data-file-height="1276" /></a></span></div>
