		</li>
</ul>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Visibility_from_Earth">Visibility from Earth</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Further information: <a href="/wiki/Satellite_watching" title="Satellite watching">Satellite watching</a> and <a href="/wiki/Satellite_flare" title="Satellite flare">Satellite flare</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:The_ISS_passing_nearly_overhead_shortly_after_sunset_in_June_2014.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4d/The_ISS_passing_nearly_overhead_shortly_after_sunset_in_June_2014.jpg/250px-The_ISS_passing_nearly_overhead_shortly_after_sunset_in_June_2014.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="166" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4d/The_ISS_passing_nearly_overhead_shortly_after_sunset_in_June_2014.jpg/500px-The_ISS_passing_nearly_overhead_shortly_after_sunset_in_June_2014.jpg 2x" data-file-width="4288" data-file-height="2848" /></a><figcaption>The ISS visible as a skytrack line drawn in the sky nearly overhead shortly after sunset by a long exposure photo</figcaption></figure>
<p>The ISS is visible in the <a href="/wiki/Sky" title="Sky">sky</a> to the <a href="/wiki/Naked_eye" title="Naked eye">naked eye</a> as a visibly moving, bright white dot, when crossing the sky and being illuminated by the Sun, during <a href="/wiki/Twilight" title="Twilight">twilight</a>, the hours after sunset and before sunrise, when the station remains sunlit, outside of <a href="/wiki/Earth%27s_shadow" title="Earth&#39;s shadow">Earth's shadow</a>, but the ground and sky are dark.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Price2005&#95;357-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Price2005-357"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>347<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> It crosses the skies at latitudes between the <a href="/wiki/Polar_region" class="mw-redirect" title="Polar region">polar regions</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-d361&#95;358-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-d361-358"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>348<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Depending on the path it takes across the sky, the time it takes the station to move across the horizon or from one to the other may be short or up to 10 minutes, while likely being only visible part of that time because of it moving into or out of Earth's shadow. It then returns around every 90 minutes, with the time of the day that it crosses the sky shifting over the course of some weeks, and therefore before returning to twilight and visible illumination.
</p><p>Because of the size of its reflective surface area, the ISS is the brightest artificial object in the sky (excluding other <a href="/wiki/Satellite_flare" title="Satellite flare">satellite flares</a>), with an approximate maximum <a href="/wiki/Apparent_magnitude" title="Apparent magnitude">magnitude</a> of −4 when in sunlight and overhead (similar to <a href="/wiki/Venus" title="Venus">Venus</a>), and a maximum <a href="/wiki/Angular_size" class="mw-redirect" title="Angular size">angular size</a> of 63 arcseconds.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Angular&#95;size&#95;359-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Angular_size-359"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>349<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Tools are provided by a number of websites such as <a href="/wiki/Heavens-Above" title="Heavens-Above">Heavens-Above</a> (see <a href="#Live_viewing"><i>Live viewing</i></a> below) as well as <a href="/wiki/Smartphone" title="Smartphone">smartphone</a> applications that use <a href="/wiki/Ephemeris" title="Ephemeris">orbital data</a> and the observer's longitude and latitude to indicate when the ISS will be visible (weather permitting), where the station will appear to rise, the altitude above the horizon it will reach and the duration of the pass before the station disappears either by setting below the horizon or entering into Earth's shadow.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-see&#95;360-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-see-360"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>350<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-361" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-361"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>351<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-362" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-362"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>352<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-daytime&#95;visibility&#95;363-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-daytime_visibility-363"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>353<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>In November 2012 NASA launched its "Spot the Station" service, which sends people text and email alerts when the station is due to fly above their town.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-364" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-364"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>354<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The station is visible from 95% of the inhabited land on Earth, but is not visible from extreme northern or southern latitudes.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-MCC&#95;Answer&#95;324-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-MCC_Answer-324"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>314<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Under specific conditions, the ISS can be observed at night on five consecutive orbits. Those conditions are 1) a mid-latitude observer location, 2) near the time of the solstice with 3) the ISS passing in the direction of the pole from the observer near midnight local time. The three photos show the first, middle and last of the five passes on 5–6&#160;June 2014.
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Astrophotography">Astrophotography</h4></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Isshtv120090917200858nm.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/23/Isshtv120090917200858nm.jpg/250px-Isshtv120090917200858nm.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="172" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/23/Isshtv120090917200858nm.jpg/500px-Isshtv120090917200858nm.jpg 2x" data-file-width="744" data-file-height="512" /></a><figcaption>The ISS and HTV photographed from Earth by <a href="/wiki/Ralf_Vandebergh" title="Ralf Vandebergh">Ralf Vandebergh</a></figcaption></figure>
<p>Using a telescope-mounted camera to photograph the station is a popular hobby for astronomers,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-hobbyspace-satwatching&#95;365-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-hobbyspace-satwatching-365"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>355<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> while using a mounted camera to photograph the Earth and stars is a popular hobby for crew.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-nasa-20030324&#95;366-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-nasa-20030324-366"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>356<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The use of a telescope or binoculars allows viewing of the ISS during daylight hours.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-367" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-367"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>357<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Composite_of_6_photos_of_the_ISS_transiting_the_gibbous_Moon.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4b/Composite_of_6_photos_of_the_ISS_transiting_the_gibbous_Moon.jpg/250px-Composite_of_6_photos_of_the_ISS_transiting_the_gibbous_Moon.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="250" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4b/Composite_of_6_photos_of_the_ISS_transiting_the_gibbous_Moon.jpg/500px-Composite_of_6_photos_of_the_ISS_transiting_the_gibbous_Moon.jpg 2x" data-file-width="2848" data-file-height="2848" /></a><figcaption>Composite of six photos of the ISS transiting the <a href="/wiki/Lunar_phase" title="Lunar phase">gibbous</a> Moon</figcaption></figure>
<p><a href="/wiki/Transit_(astronomy)" class="mw-redirect" title="Transit (astronomy)">Transits</a> of the ISS in front of the Sun, particularly during an <a href="/wiki/Solar_eclipse" title="Solar eclipse">eclipse</a> (and so the Earth, Sun, Moon, and ISS are all positioned approximately in a single line) are of particular interest for amateur astronomers.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-368" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-368"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>358<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-wired-20110105&#95;369-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-wired-20110105-369"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>359<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Environment,_safety_and_crew_health"><span id="Environment.2C_safety_and_crew_health"></span>Environment, safety and crew health</h2></div>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Environment">Environment</h3></div>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Microgravity">Microgravity</h4></div>
<p>Gravity at the altitude of the ISS is approximately 90% as strong as on Earth's surface, but objects in orbit are in continuous <a href="/wiki/Free_fall" title="Free fall">freefall</a>, resulting in a state of <a href="/wiki/Weightlessness" title="Weightlessness">weightlessness</a> known as microgravity.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-nasa-whatismicrogravity&#95;370-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-nasa-whatismicrogravity-370"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>360<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> This perceived weightlessness is disturbed by five effects:<sup id="cite&#95;ref-gravity&#95;371-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-gravity-371"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>361<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<ul><li>Drag from the residual atmosphere.</li>
<li>Vibration from the movements of mechanical systems and the crew.</li>
<li>Actuation of the on-board attitude <a href="/wiki/Control_moment_gyroscope" title="Control moment gyroscope">control moment gyroscopes</a>.</li>
<li><a href="/wiki/Rocket_engine" title="Rocket engine">Thruster</a> firings for attitude or orbital changes.</li>
<li><a href="/wiki/Gravity-gradient_stabilization" title="Gravity-gradient stabilization">Gravity-gradient effects</a>, also known as <a href="/wiki/Tidal_force" title="Tidal force">tidal</a> effects. Items at different locations within the ISS would, if not attached to the station, follow slightly different orbits. Being mechanically connected, these items experience small forces that keep the station moving as a <a href="/wiki/Rigid_body" title="Rigid body">rigid body</a>.</li></ul>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Radiation">Radiation</h4></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">See also: <a href="/wiki/Coronal_mass_ejection" title="Coronal mass ejection">Coronal mass ejection</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><span><video id="mwe_player_1" poster="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/Aurora_Australis_from_ISS_2011_-_1.ogv/330px--Aurora_Australis_from_ISS_2011_-_1.ogv.jpg" controls="" preload="none" data-mw-tmh="" class="mw-file-element" width="280" height="186" data-durationhint="34" data-mwtitle="Aurora_Australis_from_ISS_2011_-_1.ogv" data-mwprovider="wikimediacommons" resource="/wiki/File:Aurora_Australis_from_ISS_2011_-_1.ogv"><source src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/a/ad/Aurora_Australis_from_ISS_2011_-_1.ogv" type="video/ogg; codecs=&quot;theora&quot;" data-width="640" data-height="425" /><source src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/a/ad/Aurora_Australis_from_ISS_2011_-_1.ogv/Aurora_Australis_from_ISS_2011_-_1.ogv.144p.mjpeg.mov" type="video/quicktime" data-transcodekey="144p.mjpeg.mov" data-width="216" data-height="144" /><source src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/a/ad/Aurora_Australis_from_ISS_2011_-_1.ogv/Aurora_Australis_from_ISS_2011_-_1.ogv.240p.vp9.webm" type="video/webm; codecs=&quot;vp9, opus&quot;" data-transcodekey="240p.vp9.webm" data-width="362" data-height="240" /></video></span><figcaption>Video of the <a href="/wiki/Aurora_Australis" class="mw-redirect" title="Aurora Australis">Aurora Australis</a>, taken by the crew of <a href="/wiki/Expedition_28" title="Expedition 28">Expedition 28</a> on an ascending pass from south of <a href="/wiki/Madagascar" title="Madagascar">Madagascar</a> to just north of Australia over the Indian Ocean</figcaption></figure>
<p>The ISS is partially protected from the space environment by <a href="/wiki/Earth%27s_magnetic_field" title="Earth&#39;s magnetic field">Earth's magnetic field</a>. From an average distance of about 70,000&#160;km (43,000&#160;mi) from the Earth's surface, depending on Solar activity, the <a href="/wiki/Magnetosphere" title="Magnetosphere">magnetosphere</a> begins to deflect <a href="/wiki/Solar_wind" title="Solar wind">solar wind</a> around Earth and the space station. <a href="/wiki/Solar_flare" title="Solar flare">Solar flares</a> are still a hazard to the crew, who may receive only a few minutes warning. In 2005, during the initial "proton storm" of an X-3 class solar flare, the crew of <a href="/wiki/Expedition_10" title="Expedition 10">Expedition 10</a> took shelter in a more heavily shielded part of the ROS designed for this purpose.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-spacecom-20060223&#95;372-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-spacecom-20060223-372"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>362<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-373" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-373"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>363<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Subatomic charged particles, primarily <a href="/wiki/Proton" title="Proton">protons</a> from cosmic rays and solar wind, are normally absorbed by Earth's atmosphere. When they interact in sufficient quantity, their effect is visible to the naked eye in a phenomenon called an <a href="/wiki/Aurora" title="Aurora">aurora</a>. Outside Earth's atmosphere, ISS crews are exposed to approximately one <a href="/wiki/Millisievert" class="mw-redirect" title="Millisievert">millisievert</a> each day (about a year's worth of natural exposure on Earth), resulting in a higher risk of cancer. Radiation can penetrate living tissue and damage the DNA and <a href="/wiki/Chromosome" title="Chromosome">chromosomes</a> of <a href="/wiki/Lymphocyte" title="Lymphocyte">lymphocytes</a>; being central to the <a href="/wiki/Immune_system" title="Immune system">immune system</a>, any damage to these cells could contribute to the lower <a href="/wiki/Immunity_(medical)" class="mw-redirect" title="Immunity (medical)">immunity</a> experienced by astronauts. Radiation has also been linked to a higher incidence of <a href="/wiki/Cataract" title="Cataract">cataracts</a> in astronauts. Protective shielding and medications may lower the risks to an acceptable level.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-JCB&#95;59-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-JCB-59"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>57<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Radiation levels on the ISS are between 12 and 28.8 milli rads per day,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Forbes&#95;2018&#95;374-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Forbes_2018-374"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>364<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> about five times greater than those experienced by airline passengers and crew, as Earth's electromagnetic field provides almost the same level of protection against solar and other types of radiation in low Earth orbit as in the stratosphere. For example, on a 12-hour flight, an airline passenger would experience 0.1 millisieverts of radiation, or a rate of 0.2 millisieverts per day; this is one fifth the rate experienced by an astronaut in LEO. Additionally, airline passengers experience this level of radiation for a few hours of flight, while the ISS crew are exposed for their whole stay on board the station.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-375" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-375"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>365<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Microbiological_environmental_hazards">Microbiological environmental hazards</h4></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">See also: <a href="/wiki/Mir#Microbiological_environmental_hazards" title="Mir">Microbiological environmental hazards on the Mir space station</a></div>
<p>Hazardous molds that can foul air and water filters may develop aboard space stations. They can produce acids that degrade metal, glass, and rubber. They can also be harmful to the crew's health. Microbiological hazards have led to a development of the <a href="/wiki/LOCAD#Portable_Test_System" title="LOCAD">LOCAD-PTS</a> (a portable test system) which identifies common bacteria and molds faster than standard methods of <a href="/wiki/Cell_culture" title="Cell culture">culturing</a>, which may require a sample to be sent back to Earth.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-376" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-376"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>366<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Researchers in 2018 reported, after detecting the presence of five <i><a href="/wiki/Enterobacter" title="Enterobacter">Enterobacter bugandensis</a></i> bacterial strains on the ISS (none of which are <a href="/wiki/Pathogen" title="Pathogen">pathogenic</a> to humans), that microorganisms on the ISS should be carefully monitored to continue assuring a medically healthy environment for astronauts.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-EA-20181122&#95;377-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-EA-20181122-377"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>367<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-BMC-20181123&#95;378-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-BMC-20181123-378"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>368<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Contamination on space stations can be prevented by reduced humidity, and by using paint that contains mold-killing chemicals, as well as the use of antiseptic solutions. All materials used in the ISS are tested for resistance against <a href="/wiki/Fungi" class="mw-redirect" title="Fungi">fungi</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-379" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-379"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>369<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Since 2016, a series of ESA-sponsored experiments have been conducted to test the anti-bacterial properties of various materials, with the goal of developing "smart surfaces" that mitigate bacterial growth in multiple ways, using the best method for a particular circumstance. Dubbed "Microbial Aerosol Tethering on Innovative Surfaces" (MATISS), the program involves deployment of small plaques containing an array of glass squares covered with different test coatings. They remain on the station for six months before being returned to earth for analysis.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-eoportal-iss-matiss&#95;380-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-eoportal-iss-matiss-380"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>370<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The most recent and final experiment of the series was launched on 5 June 2023 aboard the <a href="/wiki/SpaceX_CRS-28" title="SpaceX CRS-28">SpaceX CRS-28</a> cargo mission to ISS, comprising four plaques. Whereas previous experiments in the series were limited to analysis by <a href="/wiki/Microscopy" title="Microscopy">light microsocopy</a>, this experiment uses quartz glass made of pure silica, which will allow <a href="/wiki/Spectroscopy" title="Spectroscopy">spectrographic analysis</a>. Two of the plaques were returned after eight months and the remaining two after 16 months.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-arstechnica-20230608&#95;381-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-arstechnica-20230608-381"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>371<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>In April 2019, NASA reported that a comprehensive study had been conducted into the microorganisms and fungi present on the ISS. The experiment was performed over a period of 14 months on three different flight missions, and involved taking samples from 8 predefined locations inside the station, then returning them to Earth for analysis. In prior experiments, analysis was limited to culture-based methods, thus overlooking microbes which cannot be grown in culture. The present study used <a href="/wiki/Molecular_diagnostics" title="Molecular diagnostics">molecular</a>-based methods in addition to culturing, resulting in a more complete catalog. The results may be useful in improving the health and safety conditions for astronauts, as well as better understanding other closed-in environments on Earth such as clean rooms used by the pharmaceutical and medical industries.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-EA-20190407&#95;382-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-EA-20190407-382"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>372<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-MBJ-20190408&#95;383-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-MBJ-20190408-383"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>373<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Noise">Noise</h4></div>
<p>Space flight is not inherently quiet, with noise levels exceeding acoustic standards as far back as the <a href="/wiki/Apollo_missions" class="mw-redirect" title="Apollo missions">Apollo missions</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-384" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-384"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>374<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-385" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-385"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>375<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> For this reason, NASA and the International Space Station international partners have developed <a href="/wiki/Noise_control" title="Noise control">noise control</a> and <a href="/wiki/Hearing_loss" title="Hearing loss">hearing loss</a> prevention goals as part of the health program for crew members. Specifically, these goals have been the primary focus of the ISS Multilateral Medical Operations Panel (MMOP) Acoustics Subgroup since the first days of ISS assembly and operations.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-MMOP&#95;SSP&#95;50260&#95;386-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-MMOP_SSP_50260-386"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>376<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-iss-acoustics&#95;387-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-iss-acoustics-387"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>377<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The effort includes contributions from <a href="/wiki/Acoustical_engineering" title="Acoustical engineering">acoustical engineers</a>, <a href="/wiki/Audiologists" class="mw-redirect" title="Audiologists">audiologists</a>, <a href="/wiki/Industrial_hygienists" class="mw-redirect" title="Industrial hygienists">industrial hygienists</a>, and physicians who comprise the subgroup's membership from NASA, Roscosmos, the European Space Agency (ESA), the Japanese Aerospace Exploration Agency (JAXA), and the Canadian Space Agency (CSA).
</p><p>When compared to terrestrial environments, the noise levels experienced by astronauts and cosmonauts on the ISS may seem insignificant and typically occur at levels that would not be of major concern to the <a href="/wiki/Occupational_Safety_and_Health_Administration" title="Occupational Safety and Health Administration">Occupational Safety and Health Administration</a> – rarely reaching 85 dBA. But crew members are exposed to these levels 24 hours a day, seven days a week, with current missions averaging six months in duration. These levels of noise also impose risks to crew health and performance in the form of sleep interference and communication, as well as reduced alarm <a href="/wiki/Hearing" title="Hearing">audibility</a>.
</p><p>Over the 19 plus year history of the ISS, significant efforts have been put forth to limit and reduce noise levels on the ISS. During design and pre-flight activities, members of the Acoustic Subgroup have written acoustic limits and verification requirements, consulted to design and choose the quietest available payloads, and then conducted acoustic verification tests prior to launch.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-MMOP&#95;SSP&#95;50260&#95;386-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-MMOP_SSP_50260-386"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>376<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup class="reference nowrap"><span title="Page / location: 5.7.3">&#58;&#8202;5.7.3&#8202;</span></sup> During spaceflights, the Acoustics Subgroup has assessed each ISS module's in flight sound levels, produced by a large number of vehicle and science experiment noise sources, to assure compliance with strict acoustic standards. The acoustic environment on ISS changed when additional modules were added during its construction, and as additional spacecraft arrive at the ISS. The Acoustics Subgroup has responded to this dynamic operations schedule by successfully designing and employing acoustic covers, absorptive materials, <a href="/wiki/Noise_barrier" title="Noise barrier">noise barriers</a>, and vibration isolators to reduce noise levels. Moreover, when pumps, fans, and ventilation systems age and show increased noise levels, this Acoustics Subgroup has guided ISS managers to replace the older, noisier instruments with quiet fan and pump technologies, significantly reducing <a href="/wiki/Ambient_noise_level" title="Ambient noise level">ambient noise levels</a>.
</p><p>NASA has adopted most-conservative damage risk criteria (based on recommendations from the <a href="/wiki/National_Institute_for_Occupational_Safety_and_Health" title="National Institute for Occupational Safety and Health">National Institute for Occupational Safety and Health</a> and the <a href="/wiki/World_Health_Organization" title="World Health Organization">World Health Organization</a>), in order to protect all crew members. The MMOP Acoustics Subgroup has adjusted its approach to managing noise risks in this unique environment by applying, or modifying, terrestrial approaches for hearing loss prevention to set these conservative limits. One innovative approach has been NASA's Noise Exposure Estimation Tool (NEET), in which noise exposures are calculated in a task-based approach to determine the need for <a href="/wiki/Hearing_protection_device" title="Hearing protection device">hearing protection devices</a> (HPDs). Guidance for use of HPDs, either mandatory use or recommended, is then documented in the Noise Hazard Inventory, and posted for crew reference during their missions. The Acoustics Subgroup also tracks spacecraft noise exceedances, applies <a href="/wiki/Engineering_controls" class="mw-redirect" title="Engineering controls">engineering controls</a>, and recommends hearing protective devices to reduce crew noise exposures. Finally, hearing thresholds are monitored on-orbit, during missions.
</p><p>There have been no persistent mission-related hearing threshold shifts among US Orbital Segment crewmembers (JAXA, CSA, ESA, NASA) during what is approaching 20 years of ISS mission operations, or nearly 175,000 work hours. In 2020, the MMOP Acoustics Subgroup received the <a href="/wiki/Safe-In-Sound_Award" class="mw-redirect" title="Safe-In-Sound Award">Safe-In-Sound Award</a> for Innovation for their combined efforts to mitigate any health effects of noise.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-388" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-388"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>378<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Fire_and_toxic_gases">Fire and toxic gases</h4></div>
<p>An onboard fire or a toxic gas leak are other potential hazards. Ammonia is used in the external radiators of the station and could potentially leak into the pressurised modules.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-389" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-389"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>379<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Overall_health_effects">Overall health effects</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">Main article: <a href="/wiki/Effect_of_spaceflight_on_the_human_body" title="Effect of spaceflight on the human body">Effect of spaceflight on the human body</a></div>
<p>On 12 April 2019, NASA reported medical results from the <a href="/wiki/ISS_year_long_mission" class="mw-redirect" title="ISS year long mission">Astronaut Twin Study</a>. Astronaut <a href="/wiki/Scott_Kelly_(astronaut)" title="Scott Kelly (astronaut)">Scott Kelly</a> spent a year in space on the ISS, while his identical twin <a href="/wiki/Mark_Kelly" title="Mark Kelly">Mark Kelly</a> spent the year on Earth. Several long-lasting changes were observed, including those related to alterations in <a href="/wiki/DNA" title="DNA">DNA</a> and <a href="/wiki/Cognition" title="Cognition">cognition</a>, when one twin was compared with the other.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-NYT-20190412&#95;390-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-NYT-20190412-390"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>380<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-SCI-20190412&#95;391-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-SCI-20190412-391"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>381<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>In November 2019, researchers reported that astronauts experienced serious <a href="/wiki/Hemodynamics" title="Hemodynamics">blood flow</a> and <a href="/wiki/Thrombus" title="Thrombus">clot</a> problems while on board the ISS, based on a six-month study of 11 healthy astronauts. The results may influence long-term spaceflight, including a mission to the planet Mars, according to the researchers.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-CNN-20191115&#95;392-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-CNN-20191115-392"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>382<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-JAMA-20191113&#95;393-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-JAMA-20191113-393"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>383<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Stress">Stress</h4></div>
<p>There is considerable evidence that <a href="/wiki/Psychosocial" title="Psychosocial">psychosocial</a> stressors are among the most important impediments to optimal crew morale and performance.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-394" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-394"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>384<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Cosmonaut <a href="/wiki/Valery_Ryumin" title="Valery Ryumin">Valery Ryumin</a> wrote in his journal during a particularly difficult period on board the <a href="/wiki/Salyut_6" title="Salyut 6"><i>Salyut</i> 6</a> space station: "All the conditions necessary for murder are met if you shut two men in a cabin measuring 18 feet by 20 [5.5&#160;m × 6&#160;m] and leave them together for two months."
</p><p>NASA's interest in <a href="/wiki/Stress_(psychological)" class="mw-redirect" title="Stress (psychological)">psychological stress</a> caused by space travel, initially studied when their crewed missions began, was rekindled when astronauts joined cosmonauts on the Russian space station <i>Mir</i>. Common sources of stress in early US missions included maintaining high performance under public scrutiny and isolation from peers and family. The latter is still often a cause of stress on the ISS, such as when the mother of NASA astronaut <a href="/wiki/Daniel_M._Tani" title="Daniel M. Tani">Daniel Tani</a> died in a car accident, and when Michael Fincke was forced to miss the birth of his second child.
</p><p>A study of the longest spaceflight concluded that the first three weeks are a critical period where attention is adversely affected because of the demand to adjust to the extreme change of environment.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-395" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-395"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>385<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> ISS crew flights typically last about five to six months.
</p><p>The ISS working environment includes further stress caused by living and working in cramped conditions with people from very different cultures who speak a different language. First-generation space stations had crews who spoke a single language; second- and third-generation stations have crew from many cultures who speak many languages. Astronauts must speak English and Russian, and knowing additional languages is even better.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-396" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-396"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>386<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Due to the lack of gravity, confusion often occurs. Even though there is no up and down in space, some crew members feel like they are oriented upside down. They may also have difficulty measuring distances. This can cause problems like getting lost inside the space station, pulling switches in the wrong direction or misjudging the speed of an approaching vehicle during docking.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-bbc-20141007&#95;397-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-bbc-20141007-397"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>387<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="Medical">Medical</h4></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Frank_De_Winne_on_treadmill_cropped.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img alt="A man running on a treadmill, smiling at the camera, with bungee cords stretching down from his waistband to the sides of the treadmill" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/91/Frank_De_Winne_on_treadmill_cropped.jpg/250px-Frank_De_Winne_on_treadmill_cropped.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="197" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/91/Frank_De_Winne_on_treadmill_cropped.jpg/500px-Frank_De_Winne_on_treadmill_cropped.jpg 2x" data-file-width="2864" data-file-height="2261" /></a><figcaption>Astronaut <a href="/wiki/Frank_De_Winne" title="Frank De Winne">Frank De Winne</a>, attached to the <a href="/wiki/Treadmill_with_Vibration_Isolation_Stabilization" title="Treadmill with Vibration Isolation Stabilization">TVIS treadmill</a> with bungee cords aboard the ISS</figcaption></figure>
<p>The <a href="/wiki/Physiology" title="Physiology">physiological</a> effects of long-term weightlessness include <a href="/wiki/Muscle_atrophy" title="Muscle atrophy">muscle atrophy</a>, deterioration of the skeleton (<a href="/wiki/Spaceflight_osteopenia" title="Spaceflight osteopenia">osteopenia</a>), fluid redistribution, a slowing of the cardiovascular system, decreased production of red blood cells, balance disorders, and a weakening of the immune system. Lesser symptoms include loss of body mass, and puffiness of the face.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-JCB&#95;59-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-JCB-59"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>57<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Sleep is regularly disturbed on the ISS because of mission demands, such as incoming or departing spacecraft. Sound levels in the station are unavoidably high. The atmosphere is unable to <a href="/wiki/Thermosiphon" title="Thermosiphon">thermosiphon</a> naturally, so fans are required at all times to process the air which would stagnate in the freefall (zero-G) environment.
</p><p>To prevent some of the adverse effects on the body, the station is equipped with: two <a href="/wiki/Treadmill_with_Vibration_Isolation_Stabilization" title="Treadmill with Vibration Isolation Stabilization">TVIS</a> treadmills (including the COLBERT); the <a href="/wiki/ARED" class="mw-redirect" title="ARED">ARED</a> (Advanced Resistive Exercise Device), which enables various weightlifting exercises that add muscle without raising (or compensating for) the astronauts' reduced bone density;<sup id="cite&#95;ref-398" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-398"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>388<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and a stationary bicycle. Each astronaut spends at least two hours per day exercising on the equipment.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-ESALife&#95;399-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-ESALife-399"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>389<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-NASACrewEquip&#95;400-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-NASACrewEquip-400"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>390<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Astronauts use bungee cords to strap themselves to the treadmill.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-401" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-401"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>391<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-402" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-402"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>392<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Life_aboard">Life aboard</h2></div>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Living_quarters">Living quarters</h3></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Nikolai_Budarin_in_a_sleep_station_in_Zvezda.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/Nikolai_Budarin_in_a_sleep_station_in_Zvezda.jpg/250px-Nikolai_Budarin_in_a_sleep_station_in_Zvezda.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="166" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/Nikolai_Budarin_in_a_sleep_station_in_Zvezda.jpg/500px-Nikolai_Budarin_in_a_sleep_station_in_Zvezda.jpg 2x" data-file-width="1024" data-file-height="680" /></a><figcaption>Cosmonaut <a href="/wiki/Nikolai_Budarin" title="Nikolai Budarin">Nikolai Budarin</a> at work inside the <i><a href="/wiki/Zvezda_(ISS_module)" title="Zvezda (ISS module)">Zvezda</a></i> service module crew quarters</figcaption></figure><p>The living and working space aboard the International Space Station (ISS) is larger than a six-bedroom house, equipped with seven private sleeping quarters, three bathrooms, two dining rooms, a gym, and a panoramic 360-degree-view bay window.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-403" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-403"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>393<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The station provides dedicated crew quarters for long-term crew members. Two are located in <i>Zvezda</i>, one in <i>Nauka</i>, and four in <i>Harmony</i>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-roscomos-20210811&#95;404-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-roscomos-20210811-404"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>394<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-405" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-405"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>395<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-roscomos-nauka-booklet&#95;406-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-roscomos-nauka-booklet-406"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>396<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-sae-08ICES-0222&#95;407-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-sae-08ICES-0222-407"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>397<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> These soundproof, person-sized booths offer privacy, ventilation, and basic amenities such as a sleeping bag, a reading lamp, and storage for personal items.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-ESALife&#95;399-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-ESALife-399"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>389<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-NASACrewEquip&#95;400-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-NASACrewEquip-400"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>390<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-CSALife&#95;408-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-CSALife-408"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>398<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The quarters in <i>Zvezda</i> include a small window but have less ventilation and soundproofing.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-409" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-409"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>399<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Visiting crew members use tethered sleeping bags attached to available wall space or inside their spacecraft. While it is possible to sleep floating freely, this is generally avoided to prevent collisions with sensitive equipment.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-SRLife&#95;410-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-SRLife-410"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>400<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Proper ventilation is critical, as astronauts risk oxygen deprivation if exhaled carbon dioxide accumulates in a bubble around their heads.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-ESALife&#95;399-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-ESALife-399"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>389<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The station's lighting system is adjustable, allowing for dimming, switching off, and <a href="/wiki/Color_temperature" title="Color temperature">color temperature</a> changes to support crew activities and rest.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-411" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-411"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>401<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-nasa-stem-sleepscience&#95;412-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-nasa-stem-sleepscience-412"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>402<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Crew_activities">Crew activities</h3></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size mw-halign-left" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Exp18home_nasa_big.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/81/Exp18home_nasa_big.jpg/250px-Exp18home_nasa_big.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="166" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/81/Exp18home_nasa_big.jpg/500px-Exp18home_nasa_big.jpg 2x" data-file-width="3072" data-file-height="2034" /></a><figcaption>Engineer <a href="/wiki/Gregory_Chamitoff" title="Gregory Chamitoff">Gregory Chamitoff</a> looking out of a window</figcaption></figure>
<p>The ISS operates on <a href="/wiki/Coordinated_Universal_Time" title="Coordinated Universal Time">Coordinated Universal Time</a> (UTC).<sup id="cite&#95;ref-bbc-iss-timezone&#95;413-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-bbc-iss-timezone-413"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>403<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> A typical day aboard the ISS begins at 06:00 with wake-up, post-sleep routines, and a morning inspection of the station. After breakfast, the crew holds a daily planning conference with Mission Control, starting work around 08:10. Morning tasks include scheduled exercise, scientific experiments, maintenance, or operational duties. Following a one-hour lunch break at 13:05, the crew resumes their afternoon schedule of work and exercise. Pre-sleep activities, including dinner and a crew conference, begin at 19:30, with the scheduled sleep period starting at 21:30.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-:3&#95;414-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:3-414"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>404<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The crew works approximately 10 hours on weekdays and 5 hours on Saturdays, with the remaining time allocated for relaxation or catching up on tasks. Free time often involves enjoying personal hobbies, communicating with family, or gazing out at Earth through the station's windows.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-:3&#95;414-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:3-414"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>404<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The crew can watch TV aboard the station.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-415" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-415"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>405<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>When the Space Shuttle was operating, the ISS crew aligned with the shuttle crew's <a href="/wiki/Mission_Elapsed_Time" title="Mission Elapsed Time">Mission Elapsed Time</a>, a flexible schedule based on the shuttle's launch.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-416" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-416"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>406<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-417" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-417"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>407<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-418" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-418"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>408<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>To simulate night conditions, the station's windows are covered during designated sleep periods, as the ISS experiences 16 sunrises and sunsets daily due to its orbital speed.
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Reflection_and_material_culture">Reflection and material culture</h3></div>
<p>Reflection of individual and crew characteristics are found particularly in the decoration of the station and expressions in general, such as religion.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-z534&#95;419-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-z534-419"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>409<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The latter has produced a certain material economy between the station and Russia in particular.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-q938&#95;420-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-q938-420"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>410<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The micro-society of the station, as well as wider society, and possibly the emergence of distinct station cultures,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-p157&#95;421-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-p157-421"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>411<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> is being studied by analyzing many aspects, from art to dust accumulation, as well as archaeologically how material of the ISS has been discarded.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-e121&#95;422-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-e121-422"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>412<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Food">Food</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1320445320" /><div role="note" class="hatnote navigation-not-searchable">See also: <a href="/wiki/Space_food" title="Space food">Space food</a> and <a href="/wiki/Plants_in_space" title="Plants in space">Plants in space</a></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:ISS-43_Food_table_in_the_Unity_module.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/ISS-43_Food_table_in_the_Unity_module.jpg/250px-ISS-43_Food_table_in_the_Unity_module.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="166" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/ISS-43_Food_table_in_the_Unity_module.jpg/500px-ISS-43_Food_table_in_the_Unity_module.jpg 2x" data-file-width="4928" data-file-height="3280" /></a><figcaption>Main dining desk in <i>Unity</i></figcaption></figure><p>Food aboard the International Space Station (ISS) is preserved and packaged to withstand long storage times, minimize waste, and prevent contamination of station systems. Because microgravity dulls taste, meals are often seasoned more heavily than on Earth.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-ESALife&#95;399-3" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-ESALife-399"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>389<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Crews particularly look forward to resupply missions, which deliver perishable items such as fresh fruit and vegetables. To reduce the risk of crumbs and spills damaging equipment, foods are prepared in specialized packaging, liquid condiments are preferred over powdered ones, and containers are secured with Velcro or magnets. Drinks are delivered as powders to be mixed with water, while soups and beverages are sipped from plastic bags with straws.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-NASACrewEquip&#95;400-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-NASACrewEquip-400"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>390<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-CSALife&#95;408-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-CSALife-408"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>398<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Solid foods are eaten with utensils attached to trays by magnets, and any stray food must be collected to prevent it from clogging air filters and other systems.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-CSALife&#95;408-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-CSALife-408"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>398<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The first <a href="/wiki/Galley_(kitchen)" title="Galley (kitchen)">galley</a> was installed in <i>Zvezda</i>, equipped with an electro-resistive can warmer and a water dispenser for both hot and ambient water. Many Russian meals are still packaged in <a href="/wiki/Steel_and_tin_cans" title="Steel and tin cans">cans</a>, which are eaten directly, while others are provided in <a href="/wiki/Retort_pouch" title="Retort pouch">retort pouches</a> rehydrated with the water dispenser.
</p>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Iss067e043370.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/Iss067e043370.jpg/250px-Iss067e043370.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="167" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/Iss067e043370.jpg/500px-Iss067e043370.jpg 2x" data-file-width="5568" data-file-height="3712" /></a><figcaption>The Expedition 67 crew during a group dinner in <i>Unity</i></figcaption></figure>
<p>A second galley was later added to <i>Unity</i> to support the station's larger crew. It contains two briefcase-shaped food warmers, a refrigerator (added in 2008), and a water dispenser.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-NASACrewEquip&#95;400-3" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-NASACrewEquip-400"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>390<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Most food in the U.S. Orbital Segment is packaged in retort pouches, which are rehydrated if necessary and heated or chilled in a food warmer or refrigerator as desired.
</p><p>While crews occasionally gather for group meals in <i>Unity</i>, especially during holidays or special occasions, they more often eat in small groups because of differing schedules. Russian cosmonauts also retain the option of dining separately in <i>Zvezda</i>, where the can warmer is located. With the growing diversity of NASA's astronaut corps and the large number of international astronauts who have flown to the ISS, the variety of food available has expanded significantly. Efforts are made to provide meals that reflect astronauts' cultural backgrounds and personal preferences, and food is often shared among crew members.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-423" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-423"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>413<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<figure class="mw-default-size" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><span><video id="mwe_player_2" poster="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/ScienceCasts-_Historic_Vegetable_Moment_on_the_Space_Station.webm/250px--ScienceCasts-_Historic_Vegetable_Moment_on_the_Space_Station.webm.jpg" controls="" preload="none" data-mw-tmh="" class="mw-file-element" width="250" height="141" data-durationhint="235" data-mwtitle="ScienceCasts-_Historic_Vegetable_Moment_on_the_Space_Station.webm" data-mwprovider="wikimediacommons" resource="/wiki/File:ScienceCasts-_Historic_Vegetable_Moment_on_the_Space_Station.webm"><source src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/0/0b/ScienceCasts-_Historic_Vegetable_Moment_on_the_Space_Station.webm/ScienceCasts-_Historic_Vegetable_Moment_on_the_Space_Station.webm.480p.vp9.webm" type="video/webm; codecs=&quot;vp9, opus&quot;" data-transcodekey="480p.vp9.webm" data-width="854" data-height="480" /><source src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/0/0b/ScienceCasts-_Historic_Vegetable_Moment_on_the_Space_Station.webm" type="video/webm; codecs=&quot;vp9, opus&quot;" data-width="1920" data-height="1080" /><source src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/0/0b/ScienceCasts-_Historic_Vegetable_Moment_on_the_Space_Station.webm/ScienceCasts-_Historic_Vegetable_Moment_on_the_Space_Station.webm.1080p.vp9.webm" type="video/webm; codecs=&quot;vp9, opus&quot;" data-transcodekey="1080p.vp9.webm" data-width="1920" data-height="1080" /><source src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/0/0b/ScienceCasts-_Historic_Vegetable_Moment_on_the_Space_Station.webm/ScienceCasts-_Historic_Vegetable_Moment_on_the_Space_Station.webm.144p.mjpeg.mov" type="video/quicktime" data-transcodekey="144p.mjpeg.mov" data-width="256" data-height="144" /><source src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/0/0b/ScienceCasts-_Historic_Vegetable_Moment_on_the_Space_Station.webm/ScienceCasts-_Historic_Vegetable_Moment_on_the_Space_Station.webm.240p.vp9.webm" type="video/webm; codecs=&quot;vp9, opus&quot;" data-transcodekey="240p.vp9.webm" data-width="426" data-height="240" /><track src="https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/api.php?action=timedtext&amp;title=File%3AScienceCasts-_Historic_Vegetable_Moment_on_the_Space_Station.webm&amp;lang=en&amp;trackformat=vtt&amp;origin=%2A" kind="subtitles" type="text/vtt" srclang="en" label="English ‪(en)‬" data-dir="ltr" /></video></span><figcaption>Fresh vegetables are grown on ISS</figcaption></figure>
<p>Experiments have also been conducted aboard the ISS to grow fresh vegetables in orbit.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-semp&#95;424-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-semp-424"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>414<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> These studies aim to supplement astronauts' diets with additional nutrients, provide psychological benefits, and advance space agricultural techniques needed for long-duration missions to the Moon and Mars.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-semp&#95;424-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-semp-424"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>414<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> As of 2023, crops grown include three types of <a href="/wiki/Lettuce" title="Lettuce">lettuce</a>, <a href="/wiki/Chinese_cabbage" title="Chinese cabbage">Chinese cabbage</a>, <a href="/wiki/Mizuna" title="Mizuna">mizuna mustard</a>, and red Russian <a href="/wiki/Kale" title="Kale">kale</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-semp&#95;424-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-semp-424"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>414<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Some of the plants are harvested and eaten by the crew, while others were returned to Earth for analysis. In the future, NASA plans to grow tomatoes and <a href="/wiki/Bell_pepper" title="Bell pepper">peppers</a>, and eventually berries, beans, and other nutrient-rich foods. Such crops could offer not only improved nutrition, but also potential protection against space radiation for crew members who consume them.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-semp&#95;424-3" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-semp-424"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>414<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Personal_hygiene">Personal hygiene</h3></div>
<link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1273380762/mw-parser-output/.tmulti" /><div class="thumb tmulti tright"><div class="thumbinner multiimageinner" style="width:242px;max-width:242px"><div class="trow"><div class="tsingle" style="width:118px;max-width:118px"><div class="thumbimage" style="height:177px;overflow:hidden"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:Zvezda_toilet.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/Zvezda_toilet.jpg/120px-Zvezda_toilet.jpg" decoding="async" width="116" height="176" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/Zvezda_toilet.jpg/250px-Zvezda_toilet.jpg 2x" data-file-width="263" data-file-height="400" /></a></span></div><div class="thumbcaption">The space toilet in the <i><a href="/wiki/Zvezda_(ISS_module)" title="Zvezda (ISS module)">Zvezda</a></i> module in the Russian segment</div></div><div class="tsingle" style="width:120px;max-width:120px"><div class="thumbimage" style="height:177px;overflow:hidden"><span typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:Waste_and_hygiene_compartment_in_the_Destiny_laboratory_of_the_International_Space_Station_-_20090412.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/Waste_and_hygiene_compartment_in_the_Destiny_laboratory_of_the_International_Space_Station_-_20090412.jpg/120px-Waste_and_hygiene_compartment_in_the_Destiny_laboratory_of_the_International_Space_Station_-_20090412.jpg" decoding="async" width="118" height="178" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/Waste_and_hygiene_compartment_in_the_Destiny_laboratory_of_the_International_Space_Station_-_20090412.jpg/250px-Waste_and_hygiene_compartment_in_the_Destiny_laboratory_of_the_International_Space_Station_-_20090412.jpg 2x" data-file-width="2040" data-file-height="3072" /></a></span></div><div class="thumbcaption">The main toilet in the US Segment inside the <i><a href="/wiki/Tranquility_(ISS_module)" title="Tranquility (ISS module)">Tranquility</a></i> module</div></div></div></div></div>
<p>The ISS is equipped with three Russian-designed toilets, located in <i>Zvezda</i>, <i>Tranquility</i> and <i>Nauka</i>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-NASACrewEquip&#95;400-4" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-NASACrewEquip-400"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>390<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-425" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-425"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>415<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Inside these "Waste and Hygiene Compartments" the occupant fastens themselves to the toilet seat, which is equipped with spring-loaded restraining bars to ensure a proper seal.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-ESALife&#95;399-4" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-ESALife-399"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>389<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> A lever activates a powerful fan while opening a suction port at the bottom of the toilet bowl, and the airstream carries waste away. Solid waste is stored in individual bags placed in an aluminium container, which is later transferred to cargo spacecraft that will burn up on reentry.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-NASACrewEquip&#95;400-5" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-NASACrewEquip-400"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>390<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-426" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-426"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>416<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Liquid waste is collected through a hose with anatomically shaped funnel adapters so that both men and women can use the same system. The <a href="/wiki/Urine_diversion" title="Urine diversion">urine is diverted</a> to the Water Recovery System, where it is processed into drinking water.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-CSALife&#95;408-3" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-CSALife-408"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>398<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Showers were first introduced on space stations in the early 1970s aboard <i>Skylab</i> and <i>Salyut</i>&#160;3.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-livingandworking&#95;427-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-livingandworking-427"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>417<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup class="reference nowrap"><span title="Page / location: 139">&#58;&#8202;139&#8202;</span></sup> However, crews complained about the complexity of showering, and by the time of <i>Salyut 6</i> in the early 1980s, it had been reduced to a monthly activity.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Portree1995-86&#95;428-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Portree1995-86-428"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>418<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The ISS, like later Russian stations after has no shower; instead, astronauts clean themselves with wet wipes or with a water jet and using soap dispensed from a toothpaste-like tube. Rinseless shampoo and edible toothpaste are also provided to conserve water.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-SRLife&#95;410-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-SRLife-410"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>400<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-429" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-429"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>419<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="End_of_mission">End of mission</h2></div>
<figure class="mw-default-size mw-halign-right" typeof="mw:File/Thumb"><a href="/wiki/File:Jules_Verne_Automated_Transfer_Vehicle_re-enters_Earth%27s_atmosphere.jpg" class="mw-file-description"><img src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8b/Jules_Verne_Automated_Transfer_Vehicle_re-enters_Earth%27s_atmosphere.jpg/250px-Jules_Verne_Automated_Transfer_Vehicle_re-enters_Earth%27s_atmosphere.jpg" decoding="async" width="250" height="161" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8b/Jules_Verne_Automated_Transfer_Vehicle_re-enters_Earth%27s_atmosphere.jpg/500px-Jules_Verne_Automated_Transfer_Vehicle_re-enters_Earth%27s_atmosphere.jpg 2x" data-file-width="3773" data-file-height="2434" /></a><figcaption>Many ISS resupply spacecraft have already undergone <a href="/wiki/Atmospheric_entry" title="Atmospheric entry">atmospheric re-entry</a>, such as <a href="/wiki/Jules_Verne_ATV" title="Jules Verne ATV"><i>Jules Verne</i> ATV</a>.</figcaption></figure>
<p>The ISS was originally intended for a 15-year mission, but the mission has been repeatedly extended due to its success and support.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-s750&#95;430-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-s750-430"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>420<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-q972&#95;431-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-q972-431"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>421<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> As a result, the oldest modules of the ISS have been in orbit for more than 25 years.
</p><p>The US planned in 2009 to deorbit the ISS in 2016.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-q972&#95;431-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-q972-431"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>421<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> But on 30 September 2015, Boeing's contract with NASA as prime contractor for the ISS was extended to 30 September 2020. Part of Boeing's services under the contract related to extending the station's primary structural hardware past 2020 to the end of 2028.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-spacedaily-20150930&#95;432-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-spacedaily-20150930-432"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>422<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In July 2018, the Space Frontier Act of 2018 was intended to extend operations of the ISS to 2030. This bill was unanimously approved in the Senate, but failed to pass in the U.S. House.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-nelson-20181020&#95;433-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-nelson-20181020-433"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>423<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-434" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-434"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>424<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In September 2018, the Leading Human Spaceflight Act was introduced with the intent to extend operations of the ISS to 2030, and was confirmed in December 2018.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-auto&#95;435-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-auto-435"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>425<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-:1&#95;436-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:1-436"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>426<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-437" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-437"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>427<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Congress later passed similar provisions in its <a href="/wiki/CHIPS_and_Science_Act" title="CHIPS and Science Act">CHIPS and Science Act</a>, signed into law by U.S. President <a href="/wiki/Joe_Biden" title="Joe Biden">Joe Biden</a> on 9 August 2022.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-politico-20220809&#95;438-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-politico-20220809-438"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>428<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-Errick&#95;2022&#95;439-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Errick_2022-439"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>429<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>NASA has suggested extending ISS operations beyond 2030 if <a href="/wiki/Commercial_LEO_Destinations_program" title="Commercial LEO Destinations program">Commercial LEO Destinations providers</a> prove insufficient to accommodate NASA's needs.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-2024DV&#95;440-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-2024DV-440"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>430<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In February 2026, during a vote to approve the NASA Reauthorization Act of 2026, members of the <a href="/wiki/United_States_House_Committee_on_Science,_Space,_and_Technology" title="United States House Committee on Science, Space, and Technology">United States House Committee on Science, Space, and Technology</a> introduced an amendment requiring NASA to explore the feasibility of relocating the ISS to a safe orbital harbor following its end-of-life so that it can be preserved for potential re-use, as an alternative to destructive atmospheric re-entry, with the amendment submitted by <a href="/wiki/George_T._Whitesides" title="George T. Whitesides">George T. Whitesides</a> with bipartisan support from <a href="/wiki/Nick_Begich_III" title="Nick Begich III">Nick Begich III</a>, <a href="/wiki/Brian_Babin" title="Brian Babin">Brian Babin</a> and <a href="/wiki/Don_Beyer" title="Don Beyer">Don Beyer</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-store&#95;441-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-store-441"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>431<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>In a January 2026 plan, <a href="/wiki/Axiom_Station" title="Axiom Station">Axiom Station</a>, while a segment of the ISS, would pick up and use established science hardware and the <a href="/wiki/Canadarm2" class="mw-redirect" title="Canadarm2">Canadarm2</a> from the ISS.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-v614&#95;211-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-v614-211"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>206<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-axiom-assembly&#95;209-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-axiom-assembly-209"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>204<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Russia meanwhile has stated that it plans to pull out of the ISS program after 2025.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-y941&#95;442-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-y941-442"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>432<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> However, Russian modules will provide <a href="/wiki/Orbital_station-keeping" title="Orbital station-keeping">orbital station-keeping</a> until 2028.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-w234&#95;443-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-w234-443"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>433<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Recommendations by the U.S.-Russian Joint Commission identified the thrusters of the <a href="/wiki/Russian_Orbital_Segment" title="Russian Orbital Segment">Russian ISS segment</a> as a deorbit backup.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-v629&#95;444-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-v629-444"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>434<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In late 2025 Russia has under budget constraints reconsidered <a href="/wiki/Russian_Orbital_Service_Station" title="Russian Orbital Service Station">their future space station plans</a> to again include modules from the ISS.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-s157&#95;445-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-s157-445"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>435<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="End_of_mission_considerations">End of mission considerations</h3></div>
<p>The reliability of the station has decreased over its longer than designed operation.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-w234&#95;443-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-w234-443"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>433<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Astronauts have been having to spend half of their time on station maintenance,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-s157&#95;445-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-s157-445"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>435<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and air leaks, as well as mold, have increasingly called the safety of the station into doubt.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-v629&#95;444-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-v629-444"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>434<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Furthermore <a href="/wiki/Orbital_station-keeping" title="Orbital station-keeping">station-keeping</a> needs funds, which have been suggested to be used elsewhere, while its preservation, as an institution or landmark, has also been raised.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-q972&#95;431-2" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-q972-431"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>421<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-store&#95;441-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-store-441"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>431<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>An unmaintained station would pose a <a href="/wiki/Space_debris" title="Space debris">space debris</a> and <a href="/wiki/Re-entry" class="mw-redirect" title="Re-entry">re-entry</a> hazard. According to the <a href="/wiki/Outer_Space_Treaty" title="Outer Space Treaty">Outer Space Treaty</a>, the parties are legally responsible for all spacecraft or modules they launch.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-outerspacetreaty&#95;446-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-outerspacetreaty-446"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>436<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Post_mission">Post mission</h3></div>
<p>NASA considered originally several possible options for the station's fate after its mission: natural orbital decay with random reentry (as with Skylab), boosting the station to a higher altitude (which would delay reentry), and a controlled de-orbit targeting a remote ocean area.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-ISSEIS&#95;447-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-ISSEIS-447"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>437<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading4"><h4 id="De-orbiting_plans">De-orbiting plans</h4></div>
<p>NASA determined that random reentry carried an unacceptable risk of producing hazardous space debris that could hit people or property and re-boosting the station would be costly and could also create hazards.
</p><p>Prior to 2010, plans had contemplated using a slightly modified Progress spacecraft to de-orbit the ISS. However, NASA concluded Progress would not be adequate for the job, and decided on a spacecraft specifically designed for the task.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-448" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-448"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>438<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<style data-mw-deduplicate="TemplateStyles:r1291884410">.mw-parser-output .locmap .od{position:absolute}.mw-parser-output .locmap .id{position:absolute;line-height:0}.mw-parser-output .locmap .pv>div{display:inline;padding:1px}.mw-parser-output .locmap .pl>div{display:inline;padding:1px;float:right}.mw-parser-output .locmap .pr>div{display:inline;padding:1px;float:left}@media
