(prefers-color-scheme:dark){html.skin-theme-clientpref-os .mw-parser-output .locmap img{filter:grayscale(0.6)}html.skin-theme-clientpref-os .mw-parser-output .od,html.skin-theme-clientpref-os .mw-parser-output .od .pv>div,html.skin-theme-clientpref-os .mw-parser-output .od .pl>div,html.skin-theme-clientpref-os .mw-parser-output .od .pr>div{background:white!important;color:#000!important}html.skin-theme-clientpref-os .mw-parser-output .infobox-full-data .locmap div{background:transparent!important}}</style><div class="locmap noresize thumb tright"><div class="thumbinner" style="width:222px"><div style="position:relative;width:220px;border:1px solid lightgray"><span class="notpageimage noviewer" typeof="mw:File"><a href="/wiki/File:Pacific_Ocean_laea_location_map.svg" class="mw-file-description" title="International Space Station is located in Pacific Ocean"><img alt="International Space Station is located in Pacific Ocean" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/Pacific_Ocean_laea_location_map.svg/250px-Pacific_Ocean_laea_location_map.svg.png" decoding="async" width="220" height="200" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/Pacific_Ocean_laea_location_map.svg/500px-Pacific_Ocean_laea_location_map.svg.png 2x" data-file-width="709" data-file-height="645" /></a></span><div class="od notheme" style="top:73.839%;left:60.203%;font-size:91%"><div class="id" style="left:-10px;top:-10px"><span class="notpageimage noviewer" typeof="mw:File"><span title="International Space Station"><img alt="International Space Station" src="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/Cercle_rouge_100%25.svg/20px-Cercle_rouge_100%25.svg.png" decoding="async" width="20" height="20" class="mw-file-element" srcset="//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/Cercle_rouge_100%25.svg/40px-Cercle_rouge_100%25.svg.png 2x" data-file-width="200" data-file-height="200" /></span></span></div></div></div><div class="thumbcaption"><div class="magnify"><a href="/wiki/File:Pacific_Ocean_laea_location_map.svg" title="File:Pacific Ocean laea location map.svg"> </a></div>Destination of the deorbiting ISS: the <a href="/wiki/Spacecraft_cemetery" title="Spacecraft cemetery">spacecraft cemetery</a> (roughly centered on "<a href="/wiki/Pole_of_inaccessibility#Oceanic_pole_of_inaccessibility" title="Pole of inaccessibility">Point Nemo</a>", the <a href="/wiki/Pole_of_inaccessibility#Oceanic_pole_of_inaccessibility" title="Pole of inaccessibility">oceanic pole of inaccessibility</a>) in the Pacific Ocean</div></div></div>
<p>In January 2022, NASA announced a planned date of January 2031 to de-orbit the ISS using the "U.S. Deorbit Vehicle" and direct any remnants into a remote area of the South Pacific Ocean that has come to be known as the <a href="/wiki/Spacecraft_cemetery" title="Spacecraft cemetery">spacecraft cemetery</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-sky-20220201&#95;449-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-sky-20220201-449"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>439<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> NASA plans to launch the deorbit vehicle in 2030, docking at the Harmony forward port.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-450" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-450"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>440<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> The deorbit vehicle will remain attached, dormant, for about a year as the station's orbit naturally decays to 220&#160;km (140&#160;mi). The spacecraft would then conduct one or more orientation burns to lower the perigee to 150&#160;km (93&#160;mi), followed by a final deorbiting burn.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-451" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-451"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>441<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-452" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-452"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>442<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>NASA began planning for the deorbit vehicle after becoming wary of Russia pulling out of the ISS abruptly, leaving the other partners with few good options for a controlled reentry.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-Nelson&#95;453-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Nelson-453"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>443<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> In June 2024, NASA selected SpaceX to develop the U.S. Deorbit Vehicle, a contract potentially worth $843 million. The vehicle will consist of an existing <a href="/wiki/SpaceX_Dragon_2" title="SpaceX Dragon 2">Cargo Dragon</a> spacecraft which will be paired with a significantly lengthened <a href="/wiki/Service_module" title="Service module">trunk module</a> which will be equipped with 46 Draco thrusters (instead of the normal 16) and will carry 30,000&#160;kg (66,000&#160;lb) of propellant, nearly six times the normal load. NASA is still working to secure all the necessary funding to build, launch and operate the deorbit vehicle.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-:2b&#95;27-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-:2b-27"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>25<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-Nelson&#95;453-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-Nelson-453"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>443<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>On 20 February 2025, <a href="/wiki/Elon_Musk" title="Elon Musk">Elon Musk</a>, CEO of SpaceX and <a href="/wiki/Senior_Advisor_to_the_President_of_the_United_States" title="Senior Advisor to the President of the United States">Senior Advisor to President Trump</a>, suggested in a tweet that the International Space Station be de-orbited "two years from now" as Musk believes the station has "served its purpose" and has "very little incremental utility". Despite this, no official decisions on moving up the de-orbiting date have been made yet by the president.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-454" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-454"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>444<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-455" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-455"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>445<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Follow_up_plans">Follow up plans</h3></div>
<p>The follow-up to NASA's program/strategy is the <a href="/wiki/Commercial_LEO_Destinations_program" title="Commercial LEO Destinations program">Commercial LEO Destinations Program</a>, meant to allow private industry to build and maintain their own stations, and NASA procuring access as a customer, starting in 2028.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-f400&#95;456-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-f400-456"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>446<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Similarly, the ESA has been seeking new private space stations to provide orbital services, as well as retrieve materials, from the ISS.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-s075&#95;457-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-s075-457"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>447<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-y695&#95;458-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-y695-458"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>448<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Axiom_Station" title="Axiom Station">Axiom Station</a> is planned to begin as a single module temporarily hosted at the ISS in 2027.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-sn-20241218&#95;210-3" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-sn-20241218-210"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>205<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Additionally, there have been suggestions in the commercial space industry that the ISS could be converted to commercial operations after it is retired by government entities,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-trump-budget-request&#95;459-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-trump-budget-request-459"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>449<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> including turning it into a space hotel.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-q972&#95;431-3" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-q972-431"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>421<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>Russia previously has planned to use its orbital segment for the construction of its <a href="/wiki/Orbital_Piloted_Assembly_and_Experiment_Complex" title="Orbital Piloted Assembly and Experiment Complex">OPSEK</a> station after the ISS is decommissioned. The modules under consideration for removal from the current ISS included the Multipurpose Laboratory Module (<i>Nauka</i>; <i>MLM</i>), launched in July 2021, and the other new Russian modules that are proposed to be attached to <i>Nauka</i>. These newly launched modules would still be well within their useful lives in 2024.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-RussiaSave&#95;460-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-RussiaSave-460"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>450<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> At the end of 2011, the <a href="/wiki/Exploration_Gateway_Platform" title="Exploration Gateway Platform">Exploration Gateway Platform</a> concept also proposed using leftover USOS hardware and <i>Zvezda 2</i> as a refuelling depot and service station located at one of the Earth–Moon <a href="/wiki/Lagrange_points" class="mw-redirect" title="Lagrange points">Lagrange points</a>. However, the entire USOS was not designed for disassembly and will be discarded.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-461" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-461"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>451<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>In 2022, companies such as CisLunar suggested using the ISS as a platform to develop orbital salvage capacities. They propose to repurpose components from the station, or at least to use space debris for its raw materials, or fuel,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-g664&#95;462-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-g664-462"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>452<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> instead of plunging it into the ocean.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-y941&#95;442-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-y941-442"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>432<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>In July 2024, NASA stated that it had not seen any viable proposals for reuse of the ISS or parts of it.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-2024DV&#95;440-1" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-2024DV-440"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>430<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Cost">Cost</h2></div>
<p>The ISS has been described as the <a href="/wiki/Lists_of_most_expensive_items_by_category" title="Lists of most expensive items by category">most expensive single item</a> ever constructed.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-zidbits-expensive&#95;463-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-zidbits-expensive-463"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>453<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> As of 2010, the total cost was US$150&#160;billion. This includes <a href="/wiki/NASA" title="NASA">NASA</a>'s budget of $58.7&#160;billion ($89.73&#160;billion in 2021 dollars) for the station from 1985 to 2015, Russia's $12&#160;billion, Europe's $5&#160;billion, Japan's $5&#160;billion, Canada's $2&#160;billion, and the cost of 36 shuttle flights to build the station, estimated at $1.4&#160;billion each, or $50.4&#160;billion in total. Assuming 20,000 man-days of use from 2000 to 2015 by two- to six-person crews, each man-day would cost $7.5&#160;million, less than half the inflation-adjusted $19.6&#160;million ($5.5&#160;million before inflation) per man-day of <a href="/wiki/Skylab" title="Skylab">Skylab</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-lafleur20100308&#95;464-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-lafleur20100308-464"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>454<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="In_culture">In culture</h2></div>
<p>The ISS has become an international symbol of human capabilities, particularly human cooperation and science,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-w619&#95;465-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-w619-465"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>455<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> defining a cooperative international approach and period, instead of a looming <a href="/wiki/Commercialization_of_space" class="mw-redirect" title="Commercialization of space">commercialized</a> and <a href="/wiki/Militarization_of_space" class="mw-redirect" title="Militarization of space">militarized</a> interplanetary world.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-v592&#95;466-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-v592-466"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>456<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Film">Film</h3></div>
<p>Beside numerous documentaries such as the IMAX documentaries <i><a href="/wiki/Space_Station_3D" title="Space Station 3D">Space Station 3D</a></i> from 2002,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-467" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-467"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>457<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> or <i><a href="/wiki/A_Beautiful_Planet" title="A Beautiful Planet">A Beautiful Planet</a></i> from 2016,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-468" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-468"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>458<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and films like <i><a href="/wiki/Apogee_of_Fear" title="Apogee of Fear">Apogee of Fear</a></i> (2012)<sup id="cite&#95;ref-huffpost-20120114&#95;469-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-huffpost-20120114-469"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>459<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and <i><a href="/wiki/Yolki_5" title="Yolki 5">Yolki 5</a></i> (2016)<sup id="cite&#95;ref-ria-20161212&#95;470-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-ria-20161212-470"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>460<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-471" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-471"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>461<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> the ISS is the subject of feature films such as <i><a href="/wiki/The_Day_After_Tomorrow" title="The Day After Tomorrow">The Day After Tomorrow</a></i> (2004),<sup id="cite&#95;ref-shaw2008&#95;472-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-shaw2008-472"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>462<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> <i><a href="/wiki/Love_(2011_film)" title="Love (2011 film)">Love</a></i> (2011),<sup id="cite&#95;ref-473" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-473"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>463<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> together with the Chinese station <a href="/wiki/Tiangong_1" class="mw-redirect" title="Tiangong 1">Tiangong 1</a> in <i><a href="/wiki/Gravity_(2013_film)" title="Gravity (2013 film)">Gravity</a></i> (2013),<sup id="cite&#95;ref-474" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-474"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>464<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> <i><a href="/wiki/Life_(2017_film)" title="Life (2017 film)">Life</a></i> (2017),<sup id="cite&#95;ref-sony-life&#95;475-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-sony-life-475"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>465<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and <i><a href="/wiki/I.S.S._(film)" title="I.S.S. (film)">I.S.S.</a></i> (2023).<sup id="cite&#95;ref-476" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-476"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>466<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>In 2022, the movie <a href="/wiki/The_Challenge_(2023_film)" title="The Challenge (2023 film)"><i>The Challenge</i> (<i>Doctor's House Call</i>)</a> was filmed aboard the ISS, and was notable for being the first feature film in which both professional actors and director worked together in space.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-nyt-20210916&#95;477-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-nyt-20210916-477"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>467<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Literature">Literature</h3></div>
<p><a href="/wiki/Neal_Stephenson" title="Neal Stephenson">Neal Stephenson</a>'s 2015 novel <i><a href="/wiki/Seveneves" title="Seveneves">Seveneves</a></i> is set largely on the ISS for the first and second parts of the novel. The ISS is depicted largely as it was when the novel was written, but with the addition of a large captured asteroid attached to the station.
</p><p>The 2023 novel <i><a href="/wiki/Orbital_(novel)" title="Orbital (novel)">Orbital</a></i> by English writer <a href="/wiki/Samantha_Harvey" title="Samantha Harvey">Samantha Harvey</a> is set on the ISS. It won the <a href="/wiki/2024_Booker_Prize" title="2024 Booker Prize">2024 Booker Prize</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-478" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-478"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>468<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-479" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-479"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>469<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p><a href="/wiki/Ceridwen_Dovey" title="Ceridwen Dovey">Ceridwen Dovey</a>'s <i>Only the Astronauts</i>, a 2024 collection of short stories in which the narrator in each story is an inanimate object in space, includes the International Space Station.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-480" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-480"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>470<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>
<div class="mw-heading mw-heading3"><h3 id="Video_games">Video games</h3></div>
<p>The ISS is blown up during the <i><a href="/wiki/Call_of_Duty:_Modern_Warfare_2" title="Call of Duty: Modern Warfare 2">Call of Duty: Modern Warfare 2</a></i> mission "Second Sun", in which the character <a href="/wiki/Captain_Price" title="Captain Price">Captain Price</a> launches an <a href="/wiki/Intercontinental_ballistic_missile" title="Intercontinental ballistic missile">ICBM</a> into the earth's atmosphere; the resulting shockwave destroys the station.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-481" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-481"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>471<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p><p>The ISS is present in <i><a href="/wiki/Far_Cry_New_Dawn" title="Far Cry New Dawn">Far Cry New Dawn</a></i> as an expedition site, having fallen to earth during a <a href="/wiki/Nuclear_warfare" title="Nuclear warfare">nuclear war</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-482" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-482"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>472<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup>
</p>

<div class="mw-heading mw-heading2"><h2 id="Notes">Notes</h2></div>

<li id="cite&#95;note-4"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-4">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text">standard;<sup id="cite&#95;ref-j675&#95;2-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-j675-2"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>2<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> past min. 2; past max. 13<sup id="cite&#95;ref-s636&#95;3-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-s636-3"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>3<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-5"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-5">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text">With the launch of the first module: <a href="/wiki/Zarya_(ISS_module)" title="Zarya (ISS module)"><i>Zarya</i></a></span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-117"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-117">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><i>Pirs</i> was connected to the nadir port of Zvezda now occupied by <i>Nauka</i>.</span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-118"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-118">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text">partially retracted</span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-120"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-120">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text">"Zarya" has several meanings: "daybreak" or "dawn" (in the morning) or "afterglow", "evening glow" or "sunset" (in the evening), but NASA and Roscosmos translate it as "sunrise."<sup id="cite&#95;ref-119" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-119"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>115<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-278"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-278">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text">Privately funded travellers who have objected to the term include Dennis Tito,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-273" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-273"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>268<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> <a href="/wiki/Mark_Shuttleworth" title="Mark Shuttleworth">Mark Shuttleworth</a>,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-274" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-274"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>269<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> Gregory Olsen, <a href="/wiki/Richard_Garriott" title="Richard Garriott">Richard Garriott</a>,<sup id="cite&#95;ref-275" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-275"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>270<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup><sup id="cite&#95;ref-nbc-20050913&#95;276-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-nbc-20050913-276"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>271<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup> and <a href="/wiki/Anousheh_Ansari" title="Anousheh Ansari">Anousheh Ansari</a>.<sup id="cite&#95;ref-esa-notatourist&#95;277-0" class="reference"><a href="#cite_note-esa-notatourist-277"><span class="cite-bracket">&#91;</span>272<span class="cite-bracket">&#93;</span></a></sup></span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-282"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-282">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text">ESA director Jörg Feustel-Büechl said Russia had no right to send “amateurs” to the ISS. A standoff at the Johnson Space Center occurred when NASA manager <a href="/wiki/Robert_Cabana" class="mw-redirect" title="Robert Cabana">Robert Cabana</a> refused to train Tito alongside commander <a href="/wiki/Talgat_Musabayev" title="Talgat Musabayev">Talgat Musabayev</a> and his crew, who insisted Tito was adequately qualified.</span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-290"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-290">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text">Including the modified DC-1, M-MIM2 and M-UM module transports</span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-291"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-291">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text">Includes both crewed and uncrewed missions</span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-Prichal-294"><span class="mw-cite-backlink">^ <a href="#cite_ref-Prichal_294-0"><sup><i><b>a</b></i></sup></a> <a href="#cite_ref-Prichal_294-1"><sup><i><b>b</b></i></sup></a> <a href="#cite_ref-Prichal_294-2"><sup><i><b>c</b></i></sup></a> <a href="#cite_ref-Prichal_294-3"><sup><i><b>d</b></i></sup></a></span> <span class="reference-text">The Prichal aft, forward, port and starboard ports still have their protective covers in place and have yet to be used since the module originally docked at the station.</span>
</li>
</ol></div></div>

<li id="cite&#95;note-nasa-img-ksc-08pd0991-105"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-nasa-img-ksc-08pd0991_105-0">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite id="CITEREFShiflett2008" class="citation web cs1">Shiflett, Kim (22 April 2008). <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://images.nasa.gov/details/KSC-08pd0991">"KSC-08pd0991"</a>. <i>NASA Image and Video Library</i>. Cape Canaveral, Florida. <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://web.archive.org/web/20231124185310/https://images.nasa.gov/details/KSC-08pd0991">Archived</a> from the original on 24 November 2023<span class="reference-accessdate">. Retrieved <span class="nowrap">5 July</span> 2019</span>. <q>In the Space Station Processing Facility at NASA's Kennedy Space Center, an overhead crane moves the Kibo Japanese Experiment Module – Pressurized Module toward the payload canister (lower right). The canister will deliver the module, part of the payload for space shuttle Discovery's STS-124 mission, to Launch Pad 39A. On the mission, the STS-124 crew will transport the Kibo module as well as the Japanese Remote Manipulator System to the International Space Station to complete the Kibo laboratory. The launch of Discovery is targeted for May 31.</q></cite><span title="ctx&#95;ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft&#95;val&#95;fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Ajournal&amp;rft.genre=unknown&amp;rft.jtitle=NASA+Image+and+Video+Library&amp;rft.atitle=KSC-08pd0991&amp;rft.date=2008-04-22&amp;rft.aulast=Shiflett&amp;rft.aufirst=Kim&amp;rft&#95;id=https%3A%2F%2Fimages.nasa.gov%2Fdetails%2FKSC-08pd0991&amp;rfr&#95;id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AInternational+Space+Station" class="Z3988"></span></span>

<li id="cite&#95;note-ISStD-106"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-ISStD_106-0">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite class="citation web cs1"><a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://web.archive.org/web/20150611163133/http://www.nasa.gov/mission_pages/station/structure/isstodate.html">"The ISS to Date"</a>. NASA. 9 March 2011. <li id="cite&#95;note-268"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-268">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite id="CITEREFMorring2012" class="citation magazine cs1">Morring, Frank (27 July 2012). <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://web.archive.org/web/20130501214851/http://www.aviationweek.com/article.aspx?id=%2Farticle-xml%2Fasd_07_26_2012_p01-02-480253.xml">"ISS Research Hampered By Crew Availability"</a>. <i>Aviation Week</i>. Archived from <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="http://www.aviationweek.com/article.aspx?id=/article-xml/asd_07_26_2012_p01-02-480253.xml">the original</a> on 1 May 2013<span class="reference-accessdate">. Retrieved <span class="nowrap">30 July</span> 2012</span>. <q>A commercial capability would allow the station's crew to grow from six to seven by providing a four-seat vehicle for emergency departures in addition to the three-seat Russian Soyuz capsules in use today.</q></cite><span title="ctx&#95;ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft&#95;val&#95;fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Ajournal&amp;rft.genre=article&amp;rft.jtitle=Aviation+Week&amp;rft.atitle=ISS+Research+Hampered+By+Crew+Availability&amp;rft.date=2012-07-27&amp;rft.aulast=Morring&amp;rft.aufirst=Frank&amp;rft&#95;id=http%3A%2F%2Fwww.aviationweek.com%2Farticle.aspx%3Fid%3D%2Farticle-xml%2Fasd&#95;07&#95;26&#95;2012&#95;p01-02-480253.xml&amp;rfr&#95;id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AInternational+Space+Station" class="Z3988"></span></span>

<li id="cite&#95;note-airandspace-201104-269"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-airandspace-201104_269-0">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite id="CITEREFHoversten2011" class="citation magazine cs1">Hoversten, Paul (April 2011). <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://www.smithsonianmag.com/air-space-magazine/assembly-nearly-complete-2091210/">"Assembly (Nearly) Complete"</a>. <i><a href="/wiki/Air_%26_Space" class="mw-redirect" title="Air &amp; Space">Air &amp; Space</a></i>. <a href="/wiki/Smithsonian_Institution" title="Smithsonian Institution">Smithsonian Institution</a>. <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://web.archive.org/web/20230607224549/https://www.smithsonianmag.com/air-space-magazine/assembly-nearly-complete-2091210/">Archived</a> from the original on 7 June 2023<span class="reference-accessdate">. Retrieved <span class="nowrap">8 May</span> 2011</span>. <q>In fact, we're designed on the U.S. side to take four crew. The ISS design is actually for seven. We operate with six because first, we can get all our work done with six, and second, we don't have a vehicle that allows us to fly a seventh crew member. Our requirement for the new vehicles being designed is for four seats. So I don't expect us to go down in crew size. I would expect us to increase it.</q></cite><span title="ctx&#95;ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft&#95;val&#95;fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Ajournal&amp;rft.genre=article&amp;rft.jtitle=Air+%26+Space&amp;rft.atitle=Assembly+%28Nearly%29+Complete&amp;rft.date=2011-04&amp;rft.aulast=Hoversten&amp;rft.aufirst=Paul&amp;rft&#95;id=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.smithsonianmag.com%2Fair-space-magazine%2Fassembly-nearly-complete-2091210%2F&amp;rfr&#95;id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AInternational+Space+Station" class="Z3988"></span></span>

<li id="cite&#95;note-spacecom-20210810-292"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-spacecom-20210810_292-0">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite id="CITEREFThompson2021" class="citation news cs1">Thompson, Amy (10 August 2021). <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://www.space.com/northrop-grumman-heaviest-cygnus-cargo-ship-launch">"Antares rocket launches heaviest Cygnus cargo ship ever to space station for NASA"</a>. <i><a href="/wiki/Space.com" title="Space.com">Space.com</a></i>. <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://web.archive.org/web/20230405033657/https://www.space.com/northrop-grumman-heaviest-cygnus-cargo-ship-launch">Archived</a> from the original on 5 April 2023<span class="reference-accessdate">. Retrieved <span class="nowrap">11 August</span> 2021</span>.</cite><span title="ctx&#95;ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft&#95;val&#95;fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Ajournal&amp;rft.genre=article&amp;rft.jtitle=Space.com&amp;rft.atitle=Antares+rocket+launches+heaviest+Cygnus+cargo+ship+ever+to+space+station+for+NASA&amp;rft.date=2021-08-10&amp;rft.aulast=Thompson&amp;rft.aufirst=Amy&amp;rft&#95;id=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.space.com%2Fnorthrop-grumman-heaviest-cygnus-cargo-ship-launch&amp;rfr&#95;id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AInternational+Space+Station" class="Z3988"></span></span>
</li>
<li id="cite&#95;note-her-293"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-her_293-0">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite id="CITEREFCookAksamentovHoffmanBruner2011" class="citation conference cs1">Cook, John; Aksamentov, Valery; Hoffman, Thomas; Bruner, Wes (September 2011). <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://ntrs.nasa.gov/api/citations/20110010964/downloads/20110010964.pdf"><i>ISS Interface Mechanisms and their Heritage</i></a> <span class="cs1-format">(PDF)</span>. AIAA Space. Houston, Texas: <a href="/wiki/Boeing" title="Boeing">Boeing</a>. <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://web.archive.org/web/20230810133110/https://ntrs.nasa.gov/api/citations/20110010964/downloads/20110010964.pdf">Archived</a> <span class="cs1-format">(PDF)</span> from the original on 10 August 2023<span class="reference-accessdate">. Retrieved <span class="nowrap">31 March</span> 2015</span>. <q>Docking is when one incoming spacecraft rendezvous with another spacecraft and flies a controlled collision trajectory in such a manner so as to align and mesh the interface mechanisms. The spacecraft docking mechanisms typically enter what is called soft capture, followed by a load attenuation phase, and then the hard docked position which establishes an air-tight structural connection between spacecraft. Berthing, by contrast, is when an incoming spacecraft is grappled by a robotic arm and its interface mechanism is placed in close proximity of the stationary interface mechanism. Then typically there is a capture process, coarse alignment and fine alignment and then structural attachment.</q></cite><span title="ctx&#95;ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft&#95;val&#95;fmt=info%3Aofi%2Ffmt%3Akev%3Amtx%3Abook&amp;rft.genre=conference&amp;rft.btitle=ISS+Interface+Mechanisms+and+their+Heritage&amp;rft.place=Houston%2C+Texas&amp;rft.pub=Boeing&amp;rft.date=2011-09&amp;rft.aulast=Cook&amp;rft.aufirst=John&amp;rft.au=Aksamentov%2C+Valery&amp;rft.au=Hoffman%2C+Thomas&amp;rft.au=Bruner%2C+Wes&amp;rft&#95;id=https%3A%2F%2Fntrs.nasa.gov%2Fapi%2Fcitations%2F20110010964%2Fdownloads%2F20110010964.pdf&amp;rfr&#95;id=info%3Asid%2Fen.wikipedia.org%3AInternational+Space+Station" class="Z3988"></span></span>
</li>

<li id="cite&#95;note-376"><span class="mw-cite-backlink"><b><a href="#cite_ref-376">^</a></b></span> <span class="reference-text"><link rel="mw-deduplicated-inline-style" href="mw-data:TemplateStyles:r1333433106" /><cite id="CITEREFBell2007" class="citation web cs1">Bell, Trudy E. <li><a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://www.g3xwh.com/index.php/home/iss-contact-march-2002/hlc-video-of-the-iss-contact">Amateur radio ISS contact with Harrogate Ladies College in 2002</a> <a rel="nofollow" class="external text" href="https://web.archive.org/web/20231017200406/https://g3xwh.com/index.php/home/iss-contact-march-2002/hlc-video-of-the-iss-contact">Archived</a> 17 October 2023 at the <a href="/wiki/Wayback_Machine" title="Wayback Machine">Wayback Machine</a></li>

<ul><li>1981:&#160;<a href="/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_L%C3%B3pez_Portillo" title="José López Portillo">José López Portillo</a></li>

<li>1984:&#160;<a href="/wiki/Contadora_group" title="Contadora group">Contadora group</a></li>

<li>1986:&#160;<a href="/wiki/University_of_Salamanca" title="University of Salamanca">University of Salamanca</a> and <a href="/wiki/University_of_Coimbra" title="University of Coimbra">University of Coimbra</a></li>
<li>1987:&#160;<a href="/wiki/Javier_P%C3%A9rez_de_Cu%C3%A9llar" title="Javier Pérez de Cuéllar">Javier Pérez de Cuéllar</a></li>
<li>1988:&#160;<a href="/wiki/%C3%93scar_Arias" title="Óscar Arias">Óscar Arias</a></li>
<li>1989:&#160;<a href="/wiki/Jacques_Delors" title="Jacques Delors">Jacques Delors</a> and <a href="/wiki/Mikhail_Gorbachev" title="Mikhail Gorbachev">Mikhail Gorbachev</a></li>

<li>1991:&#160;<a href="/wiki/United_Nations_High_Commissioner_for_Refugees" title="United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees">United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees</a> (UNHCR)</li>
<li>1992:&#160;<a href="/wiki/F._W._de_Klerk" title="F. W. de Klerk">Frederik W. de Klerk</a> and <a href="/wiki/Nelson_Mandela" title="Nelson Mandela">Nelson Mandela</a></li>
<li>1993:&#160;<a href="/wiki/United_Nations_Protection_Force" title="United Nations Protection Force">United Nations Blue Berets stationed in Ex-Yugoslavia</a></li>
<li>1994:&#160;<a href="/wiki/Yitzhak_Rabin" title="Yitzhak Rabin">Yitzhak Rabin</a> and <a href="/wiki/Yasser_Arafat" title="Yasser Arafat">Yasser Arafat</a></li>
<li>1995:&#160;<a href="/wiki/M%C3%A1rio_Soares" title="Mário Soares">Mário Soares</a></li>
